concepts/advertising-sign-standards.title: advertising-sign-standards
concepts/advertising-sign-standards.content:
Advertising Sign Standards encompass the municipal regulations dictating the design, structural integrity, placement, and maintenance of outdoor advertising displays. As outlined in the 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324, the city-of-miami implemented strict controls to ensure public safety, aesthetic quality, and structural reliability against environmental forces.
Key aspects of these standards include:

Zoning and Placement Restrictions: Billboards and general advertising signs are prohibited in residential (R) and certain business (B-1, B-2) districts. Special prohibitions exist along major thoroughfares like biscayne-boulevard between NE 13th and NE 54th Streets to prevent traffic hazards and visual blight.
Structural and Material Requirements: Signs must be constructed to withstand wind-pressure-design loads, with specific requirements for metal or fireproof materials for larger structures. Projecting signs over five feet require permits and detailed engineering drawings to ensure they can safely support their own weight and environmental stresses.
Electrical and Safety Clearances: Electric signs must maintain strict clearances from overhead power lines (18 inches for under 750 volts, 4 feet for over 750 volts) to prevent electrical hazards. All signs must be securely anchored using approved metal fasteners, prohibiting the use of staples or wooden plugs.
Permitting and Inspection: The building-permits process requires detailed plans for signs exceeding specific area thresholds. The building-inspection authority retains the right to mandate repairs or removal of unsafe, dilapidated, or unpermitted structures within 48 hours of notification.
Definitions and Classifications: The code distinguishes between various sign types, including projecting signs, roof signs, banner signs, and poster boards, each with specific dimensional limits and construction standards.

These standards reflect the broader municipal focus on urban planning, public safety, and the regulation of commercial infrastructure within fire-zones and designated districts.

concepts/allowable-working-stress.title: allowable-working-stress
concepts/allowable-working-stress.content:
Allowable Working Stress
Allowable working stress defines the maximum unit stress permitted for structural materials under combined live and dead loads, ensuring a factor of safety against failure. In the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, these limits are strictly regulated for masonry-standards, wood-framing-standards, reinforced-concrete-standards, and structural steel to account for material variability and construction methods.
Masonry Standards
Under Section 2410, allowable compressive stresses for masonry vary by material type, unit strength, and mortar composition.

Brick: Stresses range from 90 to 400 psi depending on the unit’s average compressive strength (1,250 to 4,500 psi) and the mortar type (Lime, Lime-Cement, or Cement) (p. 150-151).
Concrete: Plain concrete allows for compressive stresses up to 0.20 of the ultimate strength (f'_c) and shear stresses of 0.02 f'_c (p. 151).
Block and Tile: Concrete block or tile and load-bearing clay tile are limited to 80 psi gross cross-sectional area when laid in cement mortar, or 70 psi when laid in lime-cement mortar (p. 151-152).
Stone: Rubble stonework is limited to 140 psi (Portland cement mortar) or 100 psi (lime-cement mortar). Ashlar masonry limits are higher, reaching 800 psi for granite in Portland cement mortar (p. 153).
Concentrated Loads: Concentrated loads may exceed allowable working stresses by no more than 25% (p. 153).

Solid masonry walls must maintain these compressive stresses within code limits under combined live, dead, and wind loads (p. 224). Wall thickness scales with building height, and lateral support via reinforced concrete tie-beams and coping is mandated to resist wind forces and ensure structural continuity (p. 223-224).
Wood Standards
Wood stresses are determined by the quality grade of the lumber, categorized as “Structural,” “Yard,” or “Other” lumber (p. 154-156).

Structural Lumber: Must be grade-marked and graded according to american-society-for-testing-materials or lumber association rules. Allowable stresses for bending, shear, and compression are provided in detailed tables for species such as Douglas Fir, Southern Pine, and Oak (p. 156-157).
Columns: The allowable compressive stress for wood columns depends on the slenderness ratio (unsupported length to least dimension, l/d):

Short Columns (l/d \le 10): Use standard compressive stress values (p. 159).
Intermediate Columns: Use a parabolic reduction formula until the stress reduces to one-third of the short column value (p. 159).
Long Columns: Use the Euler formula, with a maximum slenderness limit of l/d = 50 (p. 159).



Gypsum
For gypsum used in structural applications (floor/roof construction), allowable working stresses vary by composition. Neat gypsum has higher allowable stresses (e.g., 350 psi extreme fiber compression) compared to gypsum fiber concrete or coarse aggregate concrete, which range from 100 to 220 psi depending on the percentage of wood chips or aggregate (p. 152).
Reinforced Concrete
Allowable stresses for reinforced-concrete-standards are calculated based on the minimum ultimate 28-day compressive strength of the concrete (f'_c) and the modular ratio n = 30,000/f'_c (p. 176, 182). The code specifies distinct limits for flexure, shear, bond, and axial compression:

Flexure: Extreme fiber compressive stress in concrete is limited to 0.40f'_c for general cases and 0.45f'_c adjacent to supports of continuous beams or rigid frames (p. 176). For standard strength grades, these values range from 800 to 1,350 psi (p. 176).
Shear: Permissible shear stress on concrete (v_c) depends on the presence of web reinforcement and the anchorage of longitudinal steel. Values range from 0.02f'_c (no web reinforcement, no special anchorage) to 0.09f'_c (with web reinforcement and special anchorage) (p. 176). Flat slabs and footings have specific shear limits based on anchorage conditions (p. 176).
Bond: Allowable bond stress (u) varies by bar type and member geometry. Deformed bars permit higher bond stresses (up to 0.05f'_c) compared to plain bars (0.04f'_c) in beams and slabs, with reduced values for two-way footings (p. 177). Special anchorage provisions allow doubling these bond values (p. 177).
Axial Compression: Concrete in columns is limited to 0.25f'_c for general axial compression and 0.225f'_c for columns with lateral ties (p. 177). Spirally reinforced columns permit higher stresses, calculated as 300 + (0.10 + 4p)f'_c, where p is the ratio of longitudinal reinforcement, allowing stresses up to 1,320 psi for high reinforcement ratios (p. 177).
Reinforcement Steel: Tensile stress limits for intermediate grade billet steel and rail steel bars are capped at 20,000 psi, while web reinforcement is limited to 16,000 psi (p. 177). Compression stresses in steel bars are limited to nf'_c, with structural steel and cast iron sections in composite columns capped at 15,000 psi and 9,000 psi, respectively (p. 177).
Wind Load Adjustments: When designing for wind loads, allowable unit stresses for both concrete and reinforcement may be increased to 150% of the standard values, provided the structural section meets minimum requirements for dead and live loads alone (p. 184).

Unreinforced concrete pedestals are strictly limited to a maximum compressive stress of 0.25f'_c on their gross area, with depth-to-width ratios capped at three times the least width to prevent buckling (p. 206).
Structural Steel and Iron
Allowable unit stresses for structural steel and iron are strictly defined to prevent failure under tension, compression, bending, and shear. Rolled steel allows 18,000 psi for tension and compression on short lengths, while cast steel permits 16,000 psi, and cast iron is limited to 10,000 psi in compression (p. 207). Column design incorporates slenderness ratios (l/r), with maximum stresses capped at 15,000 psi for structural steel columns and governed by specific formulas for steel pipe and cast iron columns (p. 207-208). Bending stress on extreme fibers is limited to 18,000 psi, with reductions for laterally unsupported lengths exceeding 15 times the flange width (p. 208). Shear limits range from 13,500 psi for power-driven rivets to 12,000 psi for beam webs, with bearing stresses capped at 30,000 psi for double shear and 24,000 psi for single shear (p. 208-209).
welded connections have distinct allowable stresses: 13,000 psi for tension in butt welds, 11,300 psi for shear in fillet welds, and 15,000 psi for compression (p. 213). Combined stresses from wind and other loads require adherence to specific permissible working stress multipliers, and members subject to stress reversal must be designed for the largest section plus 50% of the smaller stress (p. 209).
Foundations and Piles
Soil bearing capacities dictate foundation design, with limits set at 5 tons per square foot for solid rock, 2.5 tons for natural bed sand, and 0.5 tons for filled land (p. 219-220). Pile foundations must adhere to strict load limits based on material and driving methods. Concrete piles are limited to a maximum working stress of 12% of the concrete’s ultimate compressive strength, while concrete-filled steel tubes allow up to 25% plus 7,500 psi for the steel component (p. 221-222). Wood piles are capped at 20 tons for untreated piles and 15 tons for creosoted piles, with allowable loads further calculated using hammer drop or steam hammer penetration formulas (p. 221). Load tests for piles require sustaining twice the design load for 48 hours, with settlement limits tied to the test load and structural continuity (p. 222).

concepts/building-board-of-appeals.title: building-board-of-appeals
concepts/building-board-of-appeals.content:
The Building Board of Appeals is a quasi-judicial municipal body established to review and adjudicate disputes regarding the enforcement of building codes and safety regulations, specifically concerning the vacation, repair, or demolition of dangerous-buildings.
Establishment and Composition
Created by Ordinance No. 3963, the board consists of five members appointed by the city-commission, with one elected by the members to serve as Chairman (p. 356). Members must reside or maintain a principal place of business within the city-of-miami. The composition requires specific professional expertise: two members must be master builders, and the remaining three must be structural engineers and/or architects, each possessing at least ten years of professional experience (p. 356).
Powers and Duties
The primary function of the Board is to hear appeals from property owners, occupants, or other interested parties who contest the decisions of the chief-building-inspector regarding unsafe structures (p. 356). Appeals must be submitted in writing within five days of the Inspector’s final decision (p. 356).
Key responsibilities include:

Hearings and Findings: The Board conducts formal hearings where city departments, the Building Inspector, and property owners can present testimony. It issues written findings of fact by a majority vote of at least three members to determine if a structure qualifies as a dangerous-buildings under municipal standards (p. 357).
Enforcement Orders: Following a personal inspection, the Board issues binding orders commanding the repair, vacation, or demolition of unsafe buildings (p. 357).
Non-Compliance and Liens: If the responsible party fails to comply within ten days (or a reasonable extension), the Board is authorized to arrange for the necessary repairs or demolition. The associated costs are placed as a lien against the property or attached to tax statements, and the Board reports violators to the City Attorney for further legal action (p. 357).
Emergency Powers: In cases of immediate threat to life or safety, the Board can order the immediate repair, vacation, or demolition of a structure, with costs collected similarly to standard enforcement actions (p. 358).

The Board operates as a critical check on administrative enforcement, ensuring that decisions regarding property condemnation and public safety are reviewed by qualified professionals while protecting the rights of property owners. For detailed regulatory context, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364

concepts/building-height-and-area-limits.title: building-height-and-area-limits
concepts/building-height-and-area-limits.content:
Building height and area limits are established based on occupancy-classification, the construction-types used, and the number of streets the building fronts (p. 68). The 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84 provides specific tables for Groups F, G, and H that correlate maximum height and stories with allowable floor areas (p. 68, 71, 74). Additionally, the 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p105_124 outlines specific height and area constraints tied directly to construction types and fire zone locations.
Construction Type and Frontage
construction-types Type I generally imposes no restrictions on height or floor area (p. 68, 109). Conversely, Type II and Type III buildings face strict vertical limits: Type II is capped at 75 feet (7 stories), and Type III at 55 feet (5 stories) (p. 114, 119). Buildings on sloping ground may exceed these base heights by the vertical change in slope, up to absolute maximums of 85 feet for Type II and 65 feet for Type III (p. 114, 119). Non-habitable towers, spires, and steeples may extend an additional 20 feet for Type II and 15 feet for Type III (p. 114, 119).
construction-types Type V construction is subject to the strictest area limits. For Group F buildings, Type V construction is capped at 38 feet in height (3 stories) and a maximum floor area of 7,000 square feet for buildings fronting three or more streets (p. 68). Buildings benefit from larger allowable areas as street frontage increases. For example, a Group F building of Type II construction (75 feet, 7 stories) may have a floor area of 12,000 square feet if fronting one street, increasing to 18,000 square feet if fronting three or more streets (p. 68). Floor areas for Type II and Type III buildings are otherwise limited according to their specific occupancy classifications (p. 114, 119).
Sprinkler Incentives
The code allows for a 100% increase in the maximum allowable floor area if the building is entirely protected by sprinkler-systems (p. 68). This incentive applies to various construction types, including Type V buildings in Group F, which can double their allowable area from 7,000 to 14,000 square feet (p. 68).
Specific Group Restrictions
Certain occupancies have unique constraints:

Group E: Public garages of Type IV construction are limited to 600 square feet in area or 25 feet in height (p. 67). Dry cleaning plants using combustible solvents must be Type I construction, not exceed one story, and feature four-hour fire-resistive-construction partitions (p. 66).
Group F: Moderately hazardous occupancies (retail, offices, factories) require exterior walls within four feet of property lines to be masonry or reinforced concrete (p. 68).
Group G: Non-hazardous occupancies (ice plants, power plants) cap Type V construction at three stories and 38 feet (p. 71). Vertical openings do not require enclosure except as specified in Chapter 33, and under-roof fireproofing may be omitted (p. 72).
Group H: Hotels and apartments face stricter dimensional limits, restricting Type V construction to two stories plus an attic (p. 74). Type IV and Type V construction are prohibited for one-exit apartments and residential apartments (p. 74). Boiler rooms require a “Special Fire Separation” from the rest of the building (p. 75).
Group I: Small dwellings (max 2 families) and minor hospitals (max 6 patients) cap floor areas at 10,000 square feet for Types III/IV and 7,500 square feet for Type V (p. 76). Attached private garages must be separated by a one-hour fire-resistive-construction ceiling or floor (p. 77).
Group J: Private garages, accessory structures, and stadiums. Stadiums require one 7-foot-wide exit per 2,000 persons (p. 79). Type V construction is limited to 1,000 square feet and two stories, except for open skeleton-framed amusement structures (p. 78).

Draft Stops and Large Rooms
In buildings with rooms exceeding 30,000 square feet, tight draft stops must be installed to divide under-roof spaces into sections not exceeding 20,000 square feet (p. 68).
Fire Zone Restrictions
Allowable heights and areas are subject to further restrictions in fire-zones. In Fire Zone No. 1, buildings of Type IV and Type V construction exceeding 400 square feet are heavily restricted; they cannot be altered, raised, enlarged, or moved without meeting strict exceptions (p. 105). In Fire Zone No. 2, Type IV buildings greater than 1,000 square feet are prohibited from new construction (p. 106). Buildings spanning multiple fire zones are governed by the more restrictive zone if more than one-third of their total floor area lies within it (p. 106).

concepts/building-inspection.title: building-inspection
concepts/building-inspection.content:
Building Inspection
Building inspection is a critical regulatory process designed to ensure that construction, alteration, repair, and demolition activities comply with established safety standards and building codes. Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, the chief-building-inspector holds broad authority to enforce provisions, enter premises, halt unsafe work, verify material quality, and manage permit issuance (p. 29, p. 145, p. 347).
Permit Requirements & Site Preparation
Before any construction begins, strict administrative and site preparation standards apply. Applications for building-permits must now be accompanied by a registered land surveyor’s certificate and plan detailing property corners, line dimensions, interior angles, existing structures, utilities, elevations, rights-of-way, easements, and zoning records (p. 350).
Permit applications are generally accepted only from contractors licensed in their respective classifications. Exceptions are made for sole owners performing or supervising work on their own private residences, as well as for maintenance, repairs, or additions under $2,500 in value, provided no structural changes are involved (p. 360). Additionally, a permit card must be posted conspicuously on the premises before work commences (p. 26).
To protect public infrastructure, the Chief Building Inspector requires a performance bond or cash deposit prior to permit issuance for construction, excavating, or grading (p. 347). The deposit minimum is 50, with the exact amount determined by the value of public improvements at the site (p. 348). Similar financial guarantees apply to temporary events: carnivals require a minimum 100 deposit, and circuses require $500, based on site area and travel routes (p. 362). If damage occurs or debris is left behind, the Inspector can notify the responsible party to correct conditions within 48 to 5 days; failure to comply allows the city to use the deposit for repairs and cleanup, refunding any remainder after final inspection (p. 348, p. 363). Appeals regarding deposit amounts are directed to the department-of-public-service (p. 348, p. 362).
Inspection Process
The Building Inspector conducts mandatory inspections at key stages:

Foundation Inspection: Conducted after trench excavation and form erection (p. 26).
Frame Inspection: Performed once roofing, framing, fireblocking, and bracing are complete (p. 26).
Stucco Inspection: Required before stucco application, once lathing and materials are ready (p. 26).
Final Inspection: Completed when the building is ready for occupancy, preceding the issuance of a certificate-of-occupancy (p. 26).

No work may proceed past an inspected stage without written approval, and structural elements cannot be concealed without inspector authorization (p. 26).
Specialized System Inspections
Beyond standard structural phases, the code mandates rigorous oversight for vertical transportation, signage, and exterior protections:

Elevators and Escalators: The Building Inspector must inspect passenger and employee elevators at least once every three months (p. 314). If deemed unsafe, the Inspector can halt operations until repairs are made. Inspections may also be conducted by other competent authorities if accepted by the Inspector (p. 314). Furthermore, Ordinance No. 2149 (p. 365) mandates that all inspection reports, whether filed by city employees or private insurance or casualty company inspectors, be submitted directly to the Building Inspector (p. 365-366). Based on these reports, the Inspector issues operation certificates valid for three months for passenger and freight elevators, and six months for dumbwaiters (p. 366). The fee structure is set at 2.50 for inspection and 0.50 for the certificate, with a provision to waive the inspection fee for approved private inspectors after July 1, 1939 (p. 366). All fees collected are directed to the Tax Collector of the City of Miami rather than a special elevator fund (p. 367).
Signs and Billboards: Should any sign be found unsafe, insecure, or non-compliant during inspection, the owner has forty-eight hours to rectify the issue. Non-compliance results in removal at the owner’s expense (p. 308).
Awnings and Canopies: Installation, repair, or alteration of awnings, canopies, and tents over public property requires a permit and strict adherence to structural standards, with ongoing oversight to ensure safety against wind stress and fire hazards (p. 314).

Material Testing & Quality Control
The Building Inspector possesses the authority to mandate structural and fire-resistive material testing whenever there is reason to believe materials do not meet code requirements, and may require retesting if quality degrades over time (p. 145). All material tests must be conducted in accordance with american-society-for-testing-materials (ASTM) standard specifications (p. 145, p. 170). The financial responsibility for these tests falls on the Owner, General Contractor, or Manufacturer (p. 145).
Manufacturers may register identifying symbols for their products with the Inspector to facilitate verification, though this does not waive construction or testing requirements (p. 148). Additionally, for “Yard” or “Other” lumber that lacks standardized grade marks, the Inspector must visually inspect the material, assess defects, and assign allowable unit working stresses based on comparisons to American Lumber Standards (p. 154).
For reinforced concrete projects, the Inspector’s oversight extends to rigorous field testing and quality assurance. The Inspector may require concrete specimens to be taken at the point of deposit, cured, and tested per ASTM standards to verify mix quality (p. 170). During construction, at least one compression test specimen is required for every 100 cubic yards of concrete placed, or one per 50 cubic yards if specific material tests were used to establish the mix (p. 173, p. 174). If test results indicate that the 28-day strength falls below the planned minimum, the Inspector has the authority to mandate load tests, order changes to the water-cement ratio, or require adjusted curing conditions (p. 173, p. 174).
Architect & Engineer Supervision
While the Building Inspector enforces code compliance, the code mandates that all concrete work be actively inspected by the responsible Architect or Engineer, or a competent representative (p. 170). This professional supervision requires maintaining a detailed record of material quantities, water quality, mixing procedures, reinforcement placement, and ambient temperatures below 40°F (p. 170). These records must be preserved for two years and remain available for the Building Inspector’s review at all times (p. 170). Furthermore, concrete cannot be placed until the forms and reinforcement have been specifically inspected and approved by the Architect or Engineer (p. 178). Splices of reinforcement at points of maximum stress also require explicit Inspector approval (p. 180).
Load Testing
The Inspector retains the right to order load tests on any completed structural portion if conditions raise reasonable doubt about its adequacy (p. 170). Such tests cannot be conducted on concrete less than 60 days old (p. 170). The structure must withstand a superimposed load equal to 1.5 times the live load plus 0.5 times the dead load for 24 hours (p. 170). Failure is defined by evident structural compromise or a deflection recovery of less than 75% within 24 hours after load removal; failing structures must be modified or re-rated (p. 170).
Registered Inspectors & Special Supervision
For Type I buildings or projects with complex designs and new materials, the code mandates a “Registered Inspector” (p. 27). This individual must be bonded, constantly present on-site, and responsible for material verification, defect reporting, and code enforcement (p. 27-28). Their duties conclude only upon the issuance of a Certificate of Compliance (p. 28).
Dangerous Buildings & Enforcement
The Building Inspector plays a central role in identifying and mitigating dangerous-buildings and public nuisances. Dangerous structures are defined by criteria including structural listing, significant deterioration (33% of supporting members or 50% of enclosing walls), inadequate egress, fire hazards, and substandard sanitation or overcrowding, assessed using standards developed by the american-public-health-association (p. 352-353).
The Inspector is tasked with semi-annual inspections of public assembly buildings and annual inspections of all city buildings to identify violations (p. 354). Upon discovering a dangerous condition, the Inspector places a conspicuous notice on the structure and issues a written directive via registered mail to all interested parties, outlining required repairs, vacation, or demolition (p. 354-355). Standards dictate that buildings be ordered repaired if feasible, vacated if immediately hazardous to occupants, or demolished if 50% damaged or unrepairable (p. 354).
The Fire and Police divisions must report suspected dangerous buildings to the Inspector within 24 hours of discovery (p. 358). If an owner disputes the Inspector’s decision or fails to comply within the mandated timeframe (typically 30 to 60 days), the matter is escalated to the building-board-of-appeals (p. 355-356). This five-member board, composed of master builders, structural engineers, and architects, conducts hearings, makes written findings, and issues final orders for repair, vacation, or demolition (p. 356-357). Non-compliance with board orders can result in city-mandated action, with costs liened against the property or pursued legally (p. 357).
Violations of inspection and enforcement provisions are punishable by fines up to $500, imprisonment up to 60 days, or both, with each day constituting a separate offense (p. 348, p. 359). The Inspector also retains specific duties regarding pest control; upon discovering termite infestations, the Inspector must notify the owner or agent in writing and order extermination within a reasonable timeframe, not exceeding sixty days (p. 163).
Historically, applicants disputing code interpretations or enforcement actions could appeal to the board-of-examiners-and-appeals (p. 32), but specialized dangerous building appeals are now handled by the dedicated Building Board of Appeals to ensure expert review of structural and safety condemnations (p. 356).

concepts/building-permits.title: building-permits
concepts/building-permits.content:
Building Permits
Building permits are the primary regulatory mechanism used by the city-of-miami to authorize the erection, alteration, repair, moving, or demolition of buildings and structures. Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, no construction work exceeding $50 in cost for repairs, or any remodeling or alteration job of any value, may commence without first obtaining a permit from the Building Inspector (p. 333).
Application Requirements
Applicants must submit a written application detailing the property location and intended use or occupancy-classification (p. 20). The application must be accompanied by duplicate sets of scaled plans (minimum 1/8 inch to the foot) and a lot plan showing existing and proposed structures (p. 20). Plans must be stamped and approved by multiple city divisions, including Plumbing, Electrical, Zoning, and Public Health, depending on the project scope (pp. 20–21). Additionally, the Building Inspector may require specific approvals from the Fire Department and the Department of Public Service for street lines and grades, which must be stamped directly onto the plans (p. 334). To ensure worker safety and sanitation, all construction sites lacking water-washed facilities must provide flyproof privies with at least two private stalls (p. 334).
Recent amendments have tightened application standards to prevent property disputes and ensure compliance. Ordinance No. 3827 mandates that all permit applications for new structures, additions, or building moves must include a registered land surveyor’s certificate and plan (p. 350). This survey must detail property corner stakes, line dimensions, existing utilities, elevations, rights-of-way, easements, setbacks, and zoning records (p. 350). Furthermore, Ordinance No. 3965 stipulates that permit applications will generally be accepted only from licensed contractors (p. 360). An exception exists for sole owners applying for permits to construct, repair, or alter their own private residences for personal occupancy (p. 360). Maintenance, repairs, or additions costing less than $2,500 are also exempt from the contractor licensing requirement, unless they involve structural changes (p. 360).
Professional Oversight
For projects with an estimated cost of $5,000 or more, plans and specifications must be prepared and sealed by a registered architect or structural engineer licensed in Florida (p. 21). The professional must certify that the plans fully comply with the code, and plans cannot be reused for different locations without revision (p. 21).
Fees, Bonds, and Valuation
Permit fees are calculated based on the estimated cost of construction, determined by multiplying the building’s cubic content by a schedule of cost-per-cubic-foot rates categorized by building Group and Type (pp. 325–330). These fee schedules were significantly updated by Ordinance No. 2972 in 1945 and further amended by Ordinance No. 4049 in 1950 to align with actual increased building costs (p. 330). The Building Inspector is required to maintain a permanent, accurate ledger of all fees collected, including payer names, dates, amounts, and property locations (p. 327). If construction begins or proceeds before the permit is officially issued, the required fees are doubled, though payment does not exempt the violator from other penalties or code compliance (p. 327). Government entities remain exempt from permit fees but must still secure the necessary documentation (p. 24).
In addition to standard fees, the city has implemented performance bonds and cash deposits to protect public infrastructure. Ordinance No. 3746 requires a minimum 50 performance bond or cash deposit for permits involving construction, excavating, clearing, filling, or grading issued by the Divisions of the Department of Public Service (p. 347). The Chief Building Inspector sets the exact amount based on the value of public improvements at the site, with unused funds returned after final inspection confirms no damage or debris remains (p. 348). Similarly, Ordinance No. 3971 mandates cash deposits before issuing permits for carnivals (100 minimum) or circuses ($500 minimum) (p. 362). These deposits, determined by site area and travel routes, secure the removal of trash and repair of pavements, with refunds issued upon satisfactory cleanup (p. 363).
Specialized Structure Permits
Beyond standard building construction, the code mandates specific permitting processes for ancillary structures and mechanical systems.

Signs and Billboards: Permits are required for projecting signs over five feet in length, as well as for bulletin boards, poster boards, and roof signs (p. 307). Applications must include detailed drawings specifying location, construction materials, weight, illumination methods, and wind stress compliance (p. 307). Additionally, any individual or business manufacturing or painting signs must secure a separate license (p. 307).
Elevators and Escalators: Installation or alteration of vertical transportation systems requires a triplicate application with accompanying plans, subject to vertical-transportation-safety standards (pp. 311–312). No system may enter service until it passes a rigorous building-inspection and receives a formal certificate of approval (p. 312).
Awnings and Tents: Erecting, altering, or removing temporary-canvas-structures over public property requires a permit and approved plans when demanded by the Building Inspector (p. 315). Special permits are also necessary for tents pitched within designated fire-zones or for canopies protecting multiple vehicles (pp. 315–316).

Moving Buildings
Relocating a structure within or into city limits requires a separate written permit from the City Manager, contingent on recommendations from the Building Inspector and Director of Public Service (p. 21). Applicants must provide detailed improvement plans, a performance bond, and a 300 cash bond to cover potential street or property damage (p. 22). A city inspector must accompany the move at all times at a rate of 5 per eight hours, and structures left in streets after dark must display red warning lights (pp. 21–22).
Issuance, Compliance, and Appeals
Once plans are verified and fees paid, the Building Inspector stamps the documents “Approved” and issues the permit (p. 22). One approved set must be retained on-site throughout construction and remain open to inspection (p. 22). Upon completion of the work, the owner must secure a certificate of occupancy before the building can be used (p. 5). If a party disagrees with a condemnation or permit decision, they may appeal to a Board of Appeals within five days of the final ruling, with the board’s decision being final (p. 321).

concepts/certificate-of-occupancy.title: certificate-of-occupancy
concepts/certificate-of-occupancy.content:
Certificate of Occupancy
A certificate-of-occupancy is an official document issued by the city-of-miami Building Inspector that legally authorizes the use or occupancy of a building or structure. Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, no building may be occupied in any part until this certificate is granted, ensuring that the structure complies with all applicable building codes, laws, and ordinances (p. 28).
Issuance and Requirements
The certificate is issued only after a formal application by the property owner or agent and a successful final inspection confirming compliance with the code (p. 26). The document explicitly states the permitted use of the structure and specifies the maximum allowable floor loads for each level (p. 28). In cases where a building is partially complete, the Building Inspector may issue a temporary certificate to allow occupancy of a finished section before the entire project is finished (p. 28).
Change of Occupancy
If the intended use or occupancy-classification of a building changes, a new certificate must be obtained before the change takes effect. This requirement applies particularly when the new occupancy necessitates alterations or repairs to meet specific code standards. The inspector will only approve the change if the building fully complies with the updated requirements (p. 28).

concepts/column-design.title: column-design
concepts/column-design.content:
Column design in the 1935 code focuses on reinforced concrete vertical supports, categorizing them by reinforcement type, slenderness, and loading conditions. The regulations establish strict limits on dimensions, reinforcement ratios, and stress allowances to ensure structural stability.
Limiting Dimensions and Unsupported Length
Columns are generally restricted to a length no greater than eleven times their least lateral dimension unless designed as long columns (p. 200). Principal columns require a minimum diameter or thickness of 12 inches, while non-continuous posts require 6 inches (p. 200). The unsupported length varies by floor system: in flat-slabs it is measured to the underside of the capital, while in beam-and-slab construction it extends to the shallowest framing beam (p. 200).
Spiral and Tied Columns
The code differentiates between columns reinforced with closely spaced spirals and those with separate lateral ties. Spiral columns permit higher axial loads calculated via core area and reinforcement ratios, requiring a minimum of six longitudinal bars and a spiral ratio of at least one-fourth the longitudinal ratio (p. 200). Tied columns use gross area calculations, limit longitudinal steel to 0.005–0.02 of the total area, and mandate lateral ties spaced no more than 12 inches apart (p. 201).
Bending and Combined Stresses
Columns must account for bending moments arising from loading conditions and end restraint. In flat-slab construction, the least column dimension cannot be less than one-fifteenth of the average span or 16 inches (p. 201). When subjected to combined axial load and bending, allowable stresses increase but remain capped: spiral columns allow up to 0.15f'_c additional stress, while tied columns are limited to 0.3f'_c (p. 202). Tension in longitudinal reinforcement due to bending must not exceed 16,000 psi (p. 202).
Composite and Long Columns
Composite columns, consisting of steel or cast-iron sections encased in concrete, allow combined unit stresses based on the core area and spiral hooping (p. 202). Slender or long columns—defined as those exceeding 40 times the radius of gyration for tied columns or 50 times for spiral/composite columns—require reduction factors applied to the permissible working load to account for buckling risks (p. 204).
These provisions align with broader reinforced-concrete-standards and allowable-working-stress calculations outlined in the code. For detailed coefficients and stress formulas, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204

concepts/construction-site-regulations.title: construction-site-regulations
concepts/construction-site-regulations.content:
Construction Site Regulations
Construction site regulations govern the temporary use of public rights-of-way during building projects, the financial guarantees required to protect municipal infrastructure, and the permanent projection of structures over public property. These rules ensure public safety, maintain pedestrian access, and protect infrastructure from damage caused by heavy equipment and construction debris.
Temporary Street and Sidewalk Use
During construction, the placement of building materials on streets or sidewalks requires temporary permission from the department-of-public-safety (p. 301). Material storage is restricted to a maximum of one-third of the street width and must remain at least five feet from any railway track to avoid obstructing transit or emergency access (p. 301-302). A temporary sidewalk at least five feet wide must be constructed, protected by an eight-foot tight fence on the building side (p. 302).
In fire-zones designated as Fire Zone No. 1, or for buildings exceeding two stories, a covered walkway is mandatory. This structure must be at least five feet wide with an eight-foot clear height, capable of supporting 150 pounds per square foot, and positioned at least ten feet above the pedestrian path to shield the public from falling debris (p. 302). The walkway must remain illuminated between sunset and sunrise, with red warning lights marking the occupied street area (p. 302).
Performance Bonds and Site Protection
To prevent damage to pavements, trees, shrubs, and other public property caused by heavy equipment during construction, clearing, excavating, filling, or grading, the city requires a performance bond or cash deposit prior to permit issuance (p. 347). The chief-building-inspector determines the deposit amount based on the value of public improvements at the site, with a minimum requirement of $50.00 (p. 348). Owners or contractors who believe the amount is excessive may appeal in writing to the department-of-public-service (p. 348).
Following final inspection, the bond or deposit is returned provided there is no damage to public property and the site has been cleared of all rubbish, construction sheds, and undesirable materials (p. 348). If damage occurs or debris remains, the owner or contractor must correct the conditions within five days. Failure to do so authorizes the Chief Building Inspector to use the deposited funds to cover repair and cleanup costs, with any unexpended balance refunded (p. 348).
Permanent Projections and Public Property
The code strictly limits permanent building projections over public streets, alleys, and sidewalks. Structural elements like roof cornices may project up to 40 inches if located more than ten feet above the sidewalk, while show window cornices are limited to an 18-inch overhang (p. 303). Marquees and exterior balconies must maintain a minimum 10-foot clearance from the sidewalk below and extend no more than nine and three feet from the building, respectively (p. 303). Occupiable porches require special building-permits and must be supported by masonry or metal columns anchored to the structure (p. 303-304).
Crucially, the construction of vaults, cellar-ways, or excavations beneath sidewalks is explicitly prohibited to preserve structural integrity and public safety (p. 304). All projections over public property require a revocable permit from the Building Inspector, ensuring municipal oversight of both temporary construction activities and permanent architectural features (p. 303).

concepts/construction-types.title: construction-types
concepts/construction-types.content:
Building Construction Types
Building construction types are a classification system used in building codes to categorize structures based on their materials, fire resistance, and structural integrity. The 1935-miami-dade-building-code divides all buildings into five distinct types, ranging from the most fire-resistive to the least, to ensure appropriate safety standards are met based on occupancy-classification and location within fire-zones (p. 108). The code’s comprehensive index explicitly categorizes these five types, mapping them to specific regulatory sections, occupancy groups, and safety mandates (p. 375).
Classification System
The code establishes a hierarchy of five construction types:

Type I: fire-resistive-construction
Type II: Heavy Timber Construction
Type III: Ordinary Masonry Construction
Type IV: Metal Frame Construction
Type V: Wood Frame Construction

When a single building incorporates two or more types without an fire-separation, the entire structure is classified under the least fire-resistive type present (p. 108). Buildings erected prior to the code that do not fit neatly into these categories are classified according to the least fire-resistive type they most closely resemble.
Type I: Fire-Resistive Construction
Type I represents the highest standard of public safety and fire resistance. These buildings feature structural frames of fireproofed structural steel/iron or reinforced concrete, with exterior and inner court walls made of masonry or reinforced concrete (p. 109). There are no limits on height or floor area for Type I buildings (p. 109). Extensive structural-fireproofing is required, with protection ratings scaling based on building height (e.g., 4-hour protection for columns in buildings over 85 feet) (p. 110-111). Combustible materials are strictly regulated, though wood is permitted for decorative trim, limited mezzanine floors, and specific roof sheathing applications (p. 113).
Type II: Heavy Timber Construction
Type II buildings are limited to 75 feet in height and seven stories. While they require masonry or reinforced concrete foundations and exterior walls, the structural frame may utilize heavy timbers, provided wood columns are at least 8 inches in their least dimension (p. 114, 116). Fireproofing for steel members is mandatory unless the structure is a single story or under 65 feet tall (p. 116). Floor and roof construction must use substantial wood dimensions and be covered with fire-retardant roofing (p. 117).
Type III: Ordinary Masonry Construction
Type III structures are capped at 55 feet in height and five stories. They allow for interior load-bearing construction using masonry, concrete, steel, or wood, but mandate masonry or reinforced concrete for all exterior walls (p. 119). Fireproofing of structural members is generally omitted unless required by specific occupancy or fire zone locations (p. 123). In buildings with cellars or basements, the underside of combustible first-floor construction must be protected by a 1-hour fire-resistive ceiling (p. 124).
Type IV and V: Metal Frame and Wood Frame
Although detailed specifications for Types IV and V are referenced in the code, their use is heavily restricted in high-risk areas. For example, Type IV buildings are prohibited from exceeding 1,000 square feet in fire-zones No. 2, and Type V buildings face strict alteration and exterior wall fire-resistance requirements depending on their zone (p. 106).
Type IV (Metal Frame) utilizes a structural framework of steel, iron, masonry, or reinforced concrete, with exterior walls and roofs made of metal or other incombustible materials. Unlike heavier types, fireproofing of structural members is not required. Height is limited to forty-five feet (fifty-five on slopes), and wood is permitted only where specifically allowed. Foundations must include ventilated openings covered with wire mesh (p. 125-127).
Type V (Wood Frame) encompasses buildings where enclosing walls, partitions, floors, and roofs are primarily wood or wood-combination. Height is capped at thirty-eight feet (forty-five on slopes). Exterior walls require 2x4 studs spaced sixteen inches on centers (thicker for three-story buildings) and must be covered with approved materials such as weatherboarding, shingles, stucco over metal lath, or masonry veneer. Fire stopping is mandated every seven feet to prevent concealed air spaces. Special provisions apply to attached private garages, requiring one-hour fire-resistive walls and ceilings, gravity-closing doors, and dedicated ventilation-requirements (p. 127-140).
Regulatory Integration & Index Cross-References
Construction type directly dictates building-height-and-area-limits, allowable materials, and required safety features. The code ensures that as fire resistance decreases from Type I to Type V, corresponding restrictions on height, area, and location are applied to mitigate risk. Compliance with these types is a fundamental requirement for obtaining building-permits and ensuring public safety against fire and windstorms.
The official building code index organizes these construction types alongside extensive fire safety provisions, including automatic sprinkler requirements, fire-resistive-construction, and exit lighting (p. 370-372). It also cross-references structural and material specifications, covering concrete mixes, masonry quality, steel and iron design, and lumber stress tables (p. 369-370). Administrative procedures such as building-inspection protocols, board of appeals processes, and penalty enforcement are tied directly to compliance with these construction standards (p. 368, 372-375). Together, these sections illustrate the city’s mid-20th century approach to standardizing protocols and comprehensively regulating structural integrity, fire prevention, and building occupancy across Miami’s growing urban landscape.
Furthermore, structures—particularly Type V buildings—must adhere to strict engineering regulations regarding structural-loads and wind-pressure-design. Owners are required to conspicuously post designed live loads and maximum seating capacities, and occupancy permits require verification of floor load suitability (p. 140-144).

concepts/dangerous-buildings.title: dangerous-buildings
concepts/dangerous-buildings.content:
Dangerous Buildings
dangerous-buildings refers to structures deemed unsafe, insanitary, or hazardous to public health, morals, safety, or general welfare. Municipal regulations, such as those adopted by the city-of-miami, establish strict criteria for identifying, inspecting, and remediating these properties to prevent structural failure and fire hazards.
Definition and Criteria
A structure is classified as dangerous if it exhibits specific structural or sanitary deficiencies. Key indicators include:

Structural instability, such as leaning walls or overloaded floors (p. 352).
Significant deterioration: 33% or more damage to supporting members, or 50% damage to non-supporting walls (p. 352).
Fire damage or hazards that threaten occupants and the community (p. 352).
Inadequate sanitation, lighting, air, or egress facilities (p. 352).
Overcrowding and lack of basic utilities like electricity, running water, or proper sewage disposal (p. 353).

Assessments often utilize appraisal techniques developed by the american-public-health-association to standardize the evaluation of housing quality and deficiencies (p. 353).
Inspection and Notification
The chief-building-inspector is responsible for conducting semi-annual or annual inspections of public and private buildings to identify violations (p. 354). The department-of-public-safety, including fire and police divisions, must report suspected dangerous buildings to the inspector within 24 hours of discovery (p. 358).
Upon identification, the inspector posts a warning notice on the property and sends a formal written notice via registered mail to the owner, occupant, and mortgagee (p. 355). The notice details the specific violations and provides a deadline—typically 30 days to commence repairs or vacate, and up to 60 days for completion (p. 355).
Remediation and Enforcement
Remediation standards dictate whether a building should be repaired, vacated, or demolished:

Repair: Ordered if the building can be reasonably restored to compliance (p. 354).
Vacation: Required if the condition poses an immediate danger to occupants (p. 354).
Demolition: Mandated if the structure is 50% damaged, decayed, or beyond reasonable repair (p. 354).

Dangerous buildings are legally declared public nuisances. If owners fail to comply with orders, the municipality may perform the necessary work and attach the costs as a tax lien against the property (p. 354, p. 357). Violations can result in fines up to $500 and imprisonment for up to 60 days (p. 359).
Appeals and Emergency Measures
Owners may appeal inspection decisions to a building-board-of-appeals within five days of the final written decision (p. 356). This board, composed of experienced builders, engineers, and architects, holds hearings to verify conditions and issue final orders (p. 356).
In emergency cases where immediate danger to life or safety exists, the inspector and board can authorize immediate repair, vacation, or demolition without standard notice periods (p. 358).
For detailed regulatory text and historical context, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364

concepts/emergency-egress.title: emergency-egress
concepts/emergency-egress.content:
Emergency Egress
Emergency egress refers to the design, sizing, and arrangement of exits, aisles, stairs, and ramps that allow occupants to safely evacuate a building during an emergency. The 1935-miami-dade-building-code establishes rigorous standards for egress based on the occupancy-classification of the structure, prioritizing clear width, direct access to streets, and unobstructed travel paths.
General Requirements
All egress paths must maintain their clear width without obstruction from furniture, radiators, or fixtures (p. 49). Every building must feature a main corridor on each floor with a minimum clear width of forty-two (42) inches, increasing by one inch for each room beyond twelve up to a maximum of six feet, and must run through to an outside wall (p. 249-250). Exit doors must swing in the direction of travel, open onto a landing at least equal to the door’s width, and cannot reduce the effective stair or landing width below thirty inches when open (p. 245). If latched, doors must use self-releasing panic hardware or pressure-release locks that open upon pressure and never require a key from the inside (p. 246, 250-251). All exits must be marked with illuminated signs featuring white lights not less than five inches high on a green field, kept lit whenever the building is occupied (p. 249). Stairways and ramps must sustain a minimum live load of one hundred (100) pounds per square foot, with uniform tread and riser dimensions (variation not exceeding three-sixteenths of an inch) and slip-resistant surfaces (p. 243). All treads require a nosing of at least one inch, and width cannot decrease along the direction of exit travel (p. 243). Elevators, escalators, and revolving doors are explicitly excluded from exit calculations (p. 243). Ramps with a slope not greater than one foot in eight feet may substitute for stairways and must comply with all stairway construction, width, enclosure, landing, lighting, and ventilation requirements (p. 249).
Requirements by Occupancy Group
Egress standards scale with building size and risk:

Group A (Large Assembly): Requires exits on at least three sides of the auditorium, each minimum five feet wide, opening directly to a street or exit court (p. 46). A main foyer is mandatory, sized at one square foot per seat, connected by ramps with a maximum 1:10 slope (p. 46). Balconies over 50 seats require exits on both sides, and buildings with multiple balconies must include smokeproof-towers (p. 47, 49).
Group B (Medium Assembly): Follows Group A standards but waives the foyer requirement and prohibits balconies except in Type I construction (p. 52).
Group C (Small Assembly/Schools): Allows stairs instead of ramps and permits seats to remain unfixed. Small rooms (Division 3) may have all exits on one side if no occupant is more than 35 feet from an exit (p. 57). School hallways must be at least 4.5 feet wide, and single classrooms require two 3-foot doors or one 5-foot door (p. 58). Basement student areas need direct exterior exits (p. 58).
Group D (Institutional/Care): Mandates at least two exits per floor regardless of size. Hospitals and sanitariums require ramps with a maximum 1:6 slope for bedridden patients (p. 61). Exit doors cannot be fastened except with panic devices, except in detention facilities (p. 61-62).
Group E (Moderately Hazardous): Requires not less than two means of egress from each story, including basements or cellars, unless they are used solely for heating apparatus (p. 65). Automobile transfer ramps must meet the ground floor level at a point at least twenty feet from the building exit (p. 65). Smokeproof towers and other stairs comply with Chapter 33 specifications (p. 65).
Group F (Moderately Hazardous Industrial/Commercial) & Group G (Non-Hazardous): Both groups mandate stairs, exits, passageways, and corridors as specified in Chapter 33, with smokeproof-towers provided as required (p. 69, 72).
Group H (Hotels & Residential): All stairs and exits must open directly onto a street or alley, or onto a yard/court at least four feet wide that connects to a street or alley via a passageway. This passageway must match the width of the stairway opening and maintain a minimum height of seven feet (p. 75).
Group I (Dwellings & Small Hospitals): Stairs and exits are provided as specified in Chapter 33, with no mandatory enclosure required for stairs in these structures (p. 77).
Group J (Private Garages, Accessory Structures, Stadiums): Stairs and exits for amusement park devices follow Chapter 33, though stairs and ramps in buildings of two stories or fewer need not be enclosed (p. 79). Stadiums require one exit at least seven feet wide per 2,000 persons or fraction, spaced no more than seventy-five feet apart, with serving passageways maintaining a minimum seven-foot clear height and width (p. 79).

Aisles and Seating
Aisles must be free of steps or obstructions. Main floor aisles require a minimum width of three feet (seats on one side) or 3.5 feet (seats on both sides), increasing by 1.5 inches for every five feet of length toward the foyer (p. 48). No more than six seats may exist between any seat and an aisle (p. 48). In Group A buildings, seats must be securely fastened to the floor and spaced at least 33 inches back-to-back (p. 48).
For stadiums and reviewing stands in Group J, aisles must be at least three feet six inches wide, ensuring no more than twenty seats exist between any seat and an aisle (p. 79). Unmarked seating is calculated at eighteen inches per seat along a bench. Seats must be spaced at least twenty-six inches back-to-back, or thirty inches if backs are provided (p. 79-80). If the space beneath a stadium is used for other purposes, exits passing through it must be separated by walls, floors, and ceilings of at least one-hour fire-resistive-construction (p. 80). Stairs in these venues must have a rise of no more than seven and one-half inches and a tread of at least ten inches, excluding the nosing (p. 79).
Vertical Enclosures and Stairway Construction
Stairways, ramps, and elevators must be enclosed to protect escape routes. Enclosure passageways must be at least as wide as the stairway or ramp they serve, extending continuously from the lowest to highest required points (p. 238). Interior stairways must be enclosed with walls and partitions achieving a minimum two-hour fire-resistive-construction rating, with self-closing doors of at least one-hour fire-resistive rating swinging in the direction of travel (p. 245-247). Windows in these enclosures require wire glass with metal frames and sash (p. 238). Elevator shafts are limited to two elevators per enclosure, with one-hour fire-resistive walls separating distinct elevator banks (p. 238). Wood stairways must be protected on the underside by one-hour fire-resistive construction, while fully enclosed metal stairways are exempt from additional fireproofing mandates (p. 243).
Stairway dimensions are strictly regulated: minimum width of forty-four inches, maximum rise of seven and one-half inches, and minimum tread depth of ten inches (p. 247). No more than seventeen risers are permitted in a single flight between landings (p. 247). Landings at the top and bottom of every flight must be level, at least forty-four inches long, and match the width of the connected stair flight, with outer corners curved or splayed at a forty-five-degree angle to eliminate right angles (p. 247). Outside stairways are permitted for buildings not exceeding three stories or fifty-five feet in height but may not constitute more than fifty percent of the required exit capacity and must be constructed of incombustible materials (p. 251).
Roof access is integrated into egress design: in buildings three stories or higher, half of the required stairways must continue to and through the roof via a penthouse with approved openings (p. 243). Two-story buildings require 2’x3’ scuttles for roof access, and all stairways leading to roofs must be clearly marked with ground-level signs (p. 243).
Integration with Fire Safety
Egress systems work in tandem with fire-separation to protect escape routes. Stair and ramp enclosures must be fire-resistive, and vertical openings must be sealed to prevent smoke migration (p. 50, 56). smokeproof-towers are mandatory for buildings three or more stories in height across most occupancy groups, featuring four-hour fire-resistive masonry walls, two-hour floors and ceilings, and exterior access vestibules or balconies open to the street or court (p. 250-251). These towers provide continuous uniform egress to street grade via passageways with a minimum forty-four-inch clear width and eight-foot clear height (p. 251). Horizontal exits, consisting of protected openings through fire walls or bridges between buildings, offer additional refuge areas and require one-hour self-closing doors that remain continuously unlocked during occupancy (p. 249). For detailed code specifications, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p45_64 and 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84.

concepts/fire-rated-openings.title: fire-rated-openings
concepts/fire-rated-openings.content:
Fire-Rated Openings
Fire-rated openings refer to doors, shutters, windows, and other penetrations in fire-resistive-construction that are specifically designed and tested to maintain a fire barrier for a designated period, typically one hour. These components are critical for fire-separation, preventing the spread of flames and smoke between compartments, stairwells, and exit routes.
Fire Doors and Hardware
The code specifies construction standards for one-hour fire doors, which must either pass standardized fire tests or bear the label of underwriters-laboratories (p. 292-293). Approved types include tin-clad wood-core doors, double sheet-metal doors with asbestos insulation, and metal-clad wood panel doors. All metal fastenings must be secured by nailing, bolting, or riveting, with solder prohibited except for joint filling (p. 293).
Hardware must be constructed from high-quality malleable iron or flat-rolled structural steel meeting strict thickness requirements. Hinges, locks, and latches are dimensionally regulated, and paired swinging doors must include astragals to ensure a tight seal when closed (p. 293-294).
Windows and Shutters
Fire-resistive shutters must meet the same construction criteria as fire doors (p. 294). One-hour fire-resistive windows require frames and sashes of solid or hollow metal fabricated without solder or fusible alloys. Glazing is restricted to ¼-inch wire glass, with individual light areas capped at 720 square inches and strict dimensional limits on frame widths and heights to maintain structural integrity under heat (p. 294-295).
Installation and Operational Requirements
Installations must comply with regulations set by the national-board-fire-underwriters and the national-fire-protection-association (p. 294). Openings must be hung or framed to fit tightly against walls or frames, effectively stopping fire and smoke. Clearances around fire doors must remain unobstructed at all times to ensure emergency operation, and building occupants are mandated to close all fire doors, shutters, and windows at the close of each business day (p. 294-295).
These standards integrate with broader structural-fireproofing and fire-zones regulations to ensure comprehensive building safety. For detailed code specifications, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304

concepts/fire-resistive-construction.title: fire-resistive-construction
concepts/fire-resistive-construction.content:
Fire-resistive construction is a fundamental building standard in the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code, mandating specific materials and structural designs to withstand fire for a designated duration. The code specifies ratings such as one-hour, three-hour, and four-hour fire-resistive construction depending on the building’s occupancy classification and location. These ratings are formally defined in Part VIII of the code based on Standard Specifications for Fire Tests of Building Construction and Materials (ASTM C19-33) established by the american-society-for-testing-materials (p. 283).
Fire Walls, Parapets & Separation
Key applications include exterior walls and fire barriers. Buildings located close to adjacent property lines must use fire-resistive construction to prevent fire spread. For example, exterior walls less than three to four feet from property lines in Groups F, G, H, I, and J require at least one-hour fire-resistive construction, and openings within these distances must be protected by one-hour fire-resistive doors or windows. Solid masonry and reinforced concrete fire walls must maintain minimum thicknesses corresponding to building height, never less than twelve inches for masonry or eight inches for reinforced concrete, and must extend continuously from the foundation to eighteen inches above the immediate roof level (p. 234). Fire division walls require a minimum four-hour fire-resistive rating and thickness equivalent to exterior bearing walls, except in skeleton construction where panel wall standards apply (p. 235). Parapet walls must project at least eighteen inches above the roof and be laterally supported if exceeding six times their thickness (p. 235).
Section 4302 defines minimum thicknesses for fire-resistive bearing and non-bearing walls and partitions, accounting for solid, hollow, and plastered constructions using brick, clay tile, concrete, and gypsum blocks (p. 287-290). Plaster must average at least ½ inch per side to contribute to fire resistance, with thicker applications requiring embedded reinforcement (p. 287).
Vertical Openings & Egress Enclosures
Elevator shafts, vent shafts, stairways, and ramps must be enclosed according to construction types to act as fire barriers. Vertical openings require enclosed, fire-resistive construction with self-closing doors and wire glass to prevent smoke and fire spread (pp. 237-239). Elevator enclosures are strictly limited to two elevators per shaft, with one-hour fire-resistive walls required between separate banks (p. 238).
Stairways and ramps must sustain a minimum live load of 100 pounds per square foot, feature uniform tread and riser dimensions, and utilize slip-resistant surfaces (pp. 243-244). Interior stairways require 2-hour fire-resistive enclosures (p. 247), while passageways serving exits must feature 1-hour fire doors (p. 245). smokeproof-towers demand 4-hour fire-resistive walls and continuous uniform egress (p. 250). Outside stairways require incombustible materials, with adjacent windows protected by 1-hour fire doors or wire glass (p. 251). Multi-story buildings require roof access via scuttles or penthouses, with specific sizing and fire-resistive requirements for attic penetrations to support emergency egress (pp. 242-243).
Section 4304 details construction requirements for 1-hour fire-rated-openings, including tin-clad wood-core, double sheet-metal with asbestos fill, and metal-clad wood panel types (p. 292-293). Hardware must be made of malleable iron or structural steel, with specified dimensions for hinges, locks, latches, and astragals on paired doors (p. 293-294). Fire-resistive windows require solid or hollow metal frames, ¼-inch wire glass, and specific groove depths and bearing dimensions, with maximum light areas and frame sizes strictly regulated (p. 294-295). All fire doors, shutters, and windows must be closed at the end of each business day (p. 295).
Floor, Roof & Structural Protection
High-risk occupancies demand higher ratings. Dry cleaning plants using combustible solvents require four-hour fire-resistive partitions. Multi-story commercial buildings over five or six stories must have floors with at least three-hour fire-resistive construction. Private garages attached to dwellings require the ceiling above and all separating walls to have a one-hour fire resistance rating. Exits passing through spaces under stadiums must be separated by one-hour fire-resistive walls, floors, and ceilings.
Achieving these ratings relies on strict material quality and structural-fireproofing protocols. The code specifies minimum thicknesses for insulating materials applied to steel columns, beams, trusses, and reinforcing steel to achieve 1 to 4-hour fire-resistive ratings (p. 285). Materials include concrete, gunite, brick, clay tile, gypsum blocks, and plaster, with thickness requirements varying by structural component (p. 285). Concrete is classified into two grades: Grade A contains aggregates with no more than 65% siliceous material, while Grade B uses other coarse aggregates, typically requiring thicker protective layers (p. 286). Plaster protections exceeding 1 inch must incorporate embedded galvanized metal lath or wire mesh securely tied to supporting members (p. 286). Concrete, gypsum, and gunite protections require embedded galvanized wire or expanded metal mesh, with specific weight and gauge standards for ties and bases (p. 286-287).
Section 4303 outlines floor construction standards for 3-hour, 2-hour, and 1-hour ratings, mandating reinforced concrete, solid masonry, or protected steel joists, while permitting wood joisted construction with double flooring and fire-resistive ceilings for 1-hour ratings (p. 291). Ceiling protection thicknesses may be reduced by ½ inch if a free-air space of at least 1 inch is maintained above the member (p. 291). Roof structures and penthouses are strictly regulated; walls within 4 feet of exterior walls must match the fire-resistive rating of the story below, while other penthouse walls require a minimum 1-hour rating (p. 255).
Roof Coverings
Section 4305 categorizes roofs into “Fire Retardant” and “Ordinary” classes under roofing-standards. Fire retardant roofings require multiple layers of saturated felt, asphalt, or mineral surfacing, applied with bituminous mopping to prevent unmopped layer contact (p. 295-296). Approved materials include hydraulic compressed shingles, metal coverings, slate, and tile, with strict installation rules for nailing, cementing, and edge protection (p. 296-297). Ordinary roofings permit fewer layers or specific felt combinations but still require non-corrosive fasteners and proper lapping (p. 298). Physical properties for roofing felts and membranes are standardized by weight, saturation percentage, and american-society-for-testing-materials testing methods for pliability, heating resistance, and surface integrity (p. 299-301).
Theatrical & Specialized Fire Protection
Theatrical spaces demand rigorous fire isolation under theatrical-stage-safety regulations. Proscenium walls must be solid masonry with a 4-hour fire rating or metal-stud assemblies with cement plaster, limited to four protected openings (pp. 275-276). Stage floors require Type I construction or heavy timber/steel supports rated for 125 psf (p. 276). Motion picture booths must be enclosed in 1-hour fire-resistive rooms with self-closing fire doors, restricted viewing ports, and metal shutters on fusible links (pp. 277-278). Proscenium curtains must be constructed from metal-reinforced asbestos cloth, fully painted with mineral silicate, and mechanically operated with counterweights to lower completely in under 30 seconds (pp. 279-282). Backstage areas require fire separation from the main stage (p. 275).
Fire Suppression Infrastructure
sprinkler-systems are mandated in basements, theaters, woodworking and mattress factories, film exchanges, and multi-story commercial buildings (pp. 269-270). Systems must adhere to 1931 national-board-fire-underwriters standards (p. 270). standpipe-systems are required in buildings four stories or taller, with strict specifications for wrought iron or galvanized steel construction, hydrostatic testing, and sizing based on building height (4-inch for up to 6 stories, 6-inch for taller) (pp. 271-272). Standpipes must be located near exits and supplied by pressure tanks or fire pumps (pp. 272-273).
Fire-Stopping, Veneers & Framing Details
Beyond rated assemblies, the code enforces rigorous fire-stopping measures to prevent the spread of flames and smoke through concealed spaces. Wood framing details require:

Concealed Spaces: Continuous bridging or fire-stopping must separate stud walls and partitions, ensuring no concealed air space exceeds seven feet in any dimension.
Vertical and Horizontal Barriers: Fire stops are mandatory at all intersections of interior and exterior walls with floors, ceilings, and roofs. Stair stringers require mid-run fire-stopping, and sliding door pockets must be completely sealed at all ends.
Party Wall Protection: Wood members entering masonry party fire walls must be separated by at least four inches of masonry and feature a fire-cut bevel of no less than three inches in depth to prevent structural failure from heat.
Chimney Clearances: All spaces between chimneys and wood framing must be solidly filled with incombustible materials placed in incombustible supports.

Chimney construction mandates ⅝-inch thick fire clay linings with a softening point of at least 1994°F (p. 256). Steel smokestacks require 25 feet of fire brick lining and external masonry or ventilated metal shielding (p. 260). heating-and-chimney-safety rules dictate that gas vents near combustibles must be protected by 1-hour fire-resistive construction (p. 261), and warm air furnaces require 1-hour protection for overhead wood framing (p. 264). Fireplaces must feature fire brick facing and properly supported hearths (p. 263).
Additionally, wall-veneer-and-facings require specific attachment methods and backing rigidity to maintain fire integrity, with glass veneer installations demanding approved mastic cements, metal shelf angles, and expansion joints to prevent heat transfer and structural failure (pp. 231-233). Exit enclosure doors must be self-closing, normally closed, and constructed to 1-hour fire-resistive standards (pp. 245-246). Wire glass panels in skylights are restricted to 720 square inches or 48 inches in any single dimension (p. 253).
Construction & Public Property Regulations
Chapter 44 governs the temporary use of streets and sidewalks during construction, limiting material storage to one-third of the street width and prohibiting obstruction of rail tracks, fire hydrants, and drainage (p. 301-302). In fire-zones No. 1 or for buildings over two stories, a covered walkway with specific load-bearing capacity, height, and lighting requirements must be maintained (p. 302). Chapter 45 regulates permanent projections over public property, allowing limited overhangs for cornices, marquees, balconies, and porches, provided they are fire-resistive, properly anchored, and maintain at least 10 feet of clearance from sidewalks (p. 303-304). The code explicitly prohibits vaults, excavations, or cellar-ways beneath sidewalks to preserve public safety and infrastructure integrity (p. 304).
Testing & Material Standards
Achieving fire-resistive ratings relies on strict material quality and installation protocols. Approved materials include brick, concrete, gypsum, metal, and asbestos combinations (p. 283). Installation requires solid bedding in compatible mortars, metal ties for structural bonding, and reinforced edges for applied steel protection to prevent cracking (p. 284). Plaster fire protection must utilize gypsum or Portland cement mortar, with galvanized metal lath reinforcement unless applied directly to masonry surfaces (p. 284). Products bearing the label of the underwriters-laboratories are accepted as meeting these requirements for doors, windows, and roofings.
These requirements work in tandem with occupancy-classification and fire-zones to ensure structural integrity and life safety during a fire event. By compartmentalizing spaces, protecting egress routes, and detailing structural connections, fire-resistive construction supports emergency-egress and limits fire spread across a building or to adjacent properties.
