concepts/fire-separation.title: fire-separation
concepts/fire-separation.content:
Fire Separation
Fire Separation refers to the structural barriers—comprising walls, partitions, and/or floors—required to segregate different occupancy-classification groups within a single building. Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, when a building contains multiple occupancies classified under different groups, a fire separation must be provided to create a complete, secure, and continuous fire-break (p. 41). These separations can be vertical, horizontal, or a combination of both, ensuring each segregated portion complies with its specific occupancy requirements (p. 42). This requirement applies across all occupancy groups, with specific separation mandates referenced in Section 503 for Groups E through J (p. 67, 70, 73, 76, 78, 81).
Types of Fire Separation
The code classifies fire separations into three tiers based on their required fire-resistance duration and restrictions on openings:

Absolute Fire Separation: Provides effective resistance to fire passage for at least four hours. No openings are permitted through an absolute separation (p. 42).
Special Fire Separation: Requires at least three hours of fire resistance. Openings are allowed but must be protected on both sides by self-closing, one-hour fire-resistive doors kept normally closed. The total width of openings in any vertical separation cannot exceed 25% of the wall length in that story, and no single opening may exceed 120 square feet (p. 42).
Ordinary Fire Separation: Provides at least one hour of fire resistance. Openings must be protected by self-closing metal-clad doors kept normally closed (p. 42).

Requirements and Application
If any fire separation is mandated, the minimum standard is a one-hour separation. However, the required separation cannot be more fire-resistive than the exterior walls of the building where it occurs (p. 42). The specific level of separation required between different occupancy groups is dictated by a detailed matrix, which assigns Absolute (A), Special (S), Ordinary (O), or No Separation (N) based on the combination of groups involved (p. 44).
Proper implementation of fire separations is critical for life safety and is subject to review during the building-inspection process before a certificate-of-occupancy is granted. For detailed fee schedules and occupancy classifications tied to these requirements, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p25_44.
Hazardous Areas and Mechanical Rooms
Beyond separating distinct occupancy groups, fire separations are critical for isolating internal hazards within the building. Across various occupancy groups, boiler rooms and heating plants require specific separation based on the fuel type used. Rooms containing heating plants that burn liquid or solid fuel must be separated from the rest of the building with a “Special Fire Separation” (p. 50, 53, 59, 63, 75). Conversely, heating plants burning gas fuel require at least an “Ordinary Fire Separation” (p. 50, 53, 59, 63).
Specialized hazardous operations also demand rigorous separation. Dry cleaning plants in Group E buildings utilizing combustible solvents must be constructed of Type I materials with four-hour fire-resistive partitions (p. 66). In Group H buildings, any room where volatile inflammable liquids are used or stored must be accessed via one-hour fire-resistive doors that are kept normally closed (p. 75-76).
Attached Garages and Special Structures
The code imposes strict fire separation requirements for accessory structures and specialized occupancies. When a private garage is attached to or located beneath a Group I occupancy, the ceiling construction above the garage must be unpierced and provide at least one hour of fire-resistive-construction. The garage’s walls, partitions, and the underside of any stairway must also meet one-hour fire-resistive standards, with openings restricted to a single gravity-closing or spring-hinged door (p. 77-78).
In Type V buildings, attached private garages specifically require one-hour fire-resistive walls and ceilings, gravity-closing doors, and dedicated ventilation (p. 139-140).
For Group J occupancies like stadiums and reviewing stands, if the space beneath the structure is used for any purpose, exits passing through this area must be separated from the underlying space by walls, floors, and ceilings of not less than one-hour fire-resistive construction (p. 80). Additionally, enclosed amusement park structures exceeding one story or 200 square feet in area require exterior walls, bearing partitions, and floors to be at least one-hour fire-resistive (p. 81).
Construction Specifics
In specific construction types, such as two-story Type V buildings, usable space or basements must be surrounded by four-hour fire-resistive construction to ensure safety (p. 59).
In Type V buildings, all exterior walls and partitions must be thoroughly and effectively fire stopped at the floor and ceiling, and at the spring of the cove in a coved ceiling. Fire stops shall consist of not less than two-inch nominal size material and shall be the full thickness of the stud wall, ensuring no concealed air space has a dimension greater than seven feet (p. 131). Additionally, roof spaces in Type V buildings must be subdivided by tightly fitted partitions of matched wood or approved incombustible materials into areas not exceeding 2,500 square feet, with all openings provided with self-closing doors (p. 138).
Enclosure of vertical openings like elevator shafts, vent shafts, and stairwells in Type V buildings (except dwellings) requires one-hour fire-resistive construction with self-closing slab wood doors not less than 1 3/8 inches thick (p. 139).

concepts/fire-zones.title: fire-zones
concepts/fire-zones.content:
Fire Zones
Fire Zones are designated geographic areas within the city-of-miami that dictate specific building restrictions, safety requirements, and construction standards based on fire risk and urban density. Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, the entire city is established as a Fire District and divided into three distinct zones: Fire Zone 1, Fire Zone 2, and Fire Zone 3 (p. 81).
Classification and Boundaries
The zoning system is detailed in Chapter 16 of the code, supplemented by official Fire Zone Maps and exhaustive legal boundary descriptions for streets and districts (pp. 81-84). Each zone has tailored regulations to ensure structural safety and fire prevention:

Fire Zone 1: Subject to the strictest regulations under Section 1602. The boundary begins at the mouth of the miami-river, extends north along the harbor line of biscayne-bay to northeast-thirteenth-street, travels west to ne-2nd-avenue, north to NE 20th Street, and west through the johnson-waddell-addition (p. 81). From there, it proceeds south to NW 14th Street, west through Japes or Sosts’ Addition, south to Block 67-North, and west to the East Channel line of seybold-canal (pp. 81-82). The boundary continues south along the canal to the miami-river, loops southeast along the river channel, crosses west to Block 10-S, travels west through Lawrence Estate Land Company Subdivision to NW 12th Avenue, south to Block 18, and east back to the North side of the Miami River channel (p. 83). Finally, it follows the East Channel line to SW 2nd Avenue, south to SW 8th Street, east to the Biscayne Bay harbor line, and north back to the mouth of the river (p. 83). Section 1602 explicitly prohibits any building or structure of Type V Construction from being erected, constructed, moved, removed, raised, altered, or added to within Fire Zone No. 1 (p. 104). Type IV construction is also subject to strict limitations in this zone (p. 104). Buildings over 400 square feet must comply with stringent rules, including mandatory “fire retardant” roofing and one-hour fire-resistive-construction for exterior doors and windows (p. 105). Alterations exceeding 20% of a building’s value in this zone require full compliance with new building standards, and temporary structures require special permits (p. 105). In 1941, Ordinance No. 2471 significantly expanded Fire Zone 1 to prevent new frame structures and reduce fire hazards, adding areas such as Greenwood Park, Highland Park, Spring Garden Subdivisions, and territories along the miami-river and Northwest avenues (p. 341). This expansion was further extended by Ordinance No. 3970 in April 1950, which added a substantial northern tract to the zone (p. 345). The new boundary traces northward from northeast-thirteenth-street and northeast-second-avenue, follows northeast-second-avenue to Northeast Twentieth Street, and proceeds west through numerous residential plats—including West Bay Side, Bay View Addition, Rural Home, West Edgewater, Park Place, Pomelo Park, Halcyon Heights, Flagler Park, Western Boulevard Tract, and West Broadmoor—until reaching the florida-east-coast-railroad right-of-way (pp. 345-346). From there, the boundary follows the railroad to Northeast Thirty-sixth Street, west to Northeast Second Avenue, north to Northeast Thirty-seventh Street, and east to the U.S. Harbor Line of biscayne-bay, returning south along the harbor line to the starting point (p. 346). Violations of these expanded zone restrictions are punishable by fines up to $500 or imprisonment for up to 60 days (p. 346).
Fire Zone 2: Governed by Section 1603 as the “Secondary Fire District.” The boundary commences at the present fire limits at ne-2nd-avenue and northeast-thirteenth-street, extending east along said fire limits line (p. 83). It then travels north through a series of specific lots and subdivisions, including Walden Court, Pershing Court, Garden of Eden, Nelson Villa, Biscayne Park, Rice & Sullivan, Miramar, Coral Park, Bayside Park, Bayonne, Edgewater, Bird, Banyan Place, Escotonia Park, Cold Court, Bankers Park, Broadmoor, elwood-court, Beverly Terrace, Sandricourt, Jeffreys & Robbins, John F. Collins, Buena Vista Biscayne Badger Club, and Magnolia Park (pp. 83-84). Continuing north, it runs parallel to Biscayne Boulevard through Bayshore, White’s Lemon City, Knight’s Addition, Fallesen Park, Arlington, South Elmira, Elmira, Baywood, Arcadia, Washington Place, Belle Meade, Aqua Marine, Shore Crest, Biscayne Heights, North Shore Crest, and ashbury-park (p. 84). The boundary then runs parallel to and 100 feet east of federal-highway, traversing Federal Plaza, miami-shores, Julia D. Tuttle, biscayne-shores, Dixie Highway, Bay Ridge, Vista Biscayne, and Water View Park, before heading through alleys to the city limits (p. 84). It outlines specific construction and safety mandates tailored to its risk profile (pp. 5, 22). Type V construction requires one-hour fire-resistive exterior walls, and Type IV buildings are restricted to under 1,000 square feet (p. 106). Alterations exceeding 50% of a building’s value trigger full compliance requirements (p. 106). This zone was later expanded by Ordinance No. 2568 in 1941, which incorporated areas of coconut-grove into the secondary fire district to further restrict new frame construction and align with the city’s general plan (pp. 322-323).
Fire Zone 3: Covered under baseline restrictions, comprising all remaining territory or portions of the city not otherwise included in Zones 1 and 2 (p. 81). Any building complying with the general code requirements may be erected or moved into this zone (p. 107).

When a building spans two fire zones, it is classified under the more restricted zone if more than one-third of its total floor area lies within it (p. 106).
Construction and Compliance Workflow
Determining a property’s fire zone is a critical early step in the compliance process. After verifying land use through zoning maps, builders must identify the applicable fire zone to understand special construction requirements before classifying the building’s occupancy-classification and selecting materials (p. 5). Fire zone designations directly impact allowable construction-types, particularly restricting Types IV and V in hazardous areas per Sections 1602 and 1603 (pp. 68, 71, 74, 76, 78). The precise boundaries of these zones are defined by tracing routes along major thoroughfares and subdivision blocks, such as north-miami-avenue, nw-7th-avenue, west-flagler-street, and south-bayshore-drive (pp. 85-103).
Zone classifications also dictate specific requirements for auxiliary structures. For example, all awnings, stationary awnings, and canopies erected within Fire Zones 1 and 2 must utilize metal frames, though heavy wooden posts may support canopy frames under certain conditions (pp. 315-316). Pipe thickness and structural bracing for these temporary canvas protections are strictly regulated based on span width to ensure they withstand wind pressure without compromising fire safety (p. 316).
Fire zone designations also impact administrative procedures. Moving a building within or into the city limits requires strict adherence to Section 201 and the specific zone’s regulations (p. 81). Furthermore, major renovations exceeding 50% of a building’s value in Zone 1 trigger mandatory code compliance or demolition orders (p. 18).
Relationship to Building Permits and Inspection
Fire zone classification directly influences the building-permits and building-inspection processes. Applicants must submit plans that comply with the specific restrictions of their designated zone, and the Building Inspector verifies conformity with Chapter 16 before issuing approval. Zone-specific standards also dictate requirements for fire-resistive-construction and egress. Failure to meet zone-specific standards can result in permit denial or mandatory structural corrections (pp. 5, 22).

concepts/flat-slabs.title: flat-slabs
concepts/flat-slabs.content:
Flat Slabs
Flat slabs are concrete floor systems reinforced in two or four directions that transfer loads directly to supporting columns without the use of beams or girders 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204. This construction method relies on specific geometric limitations, moment distribution coefficients, and detailed reinforcement layouts to ensure structural integrity.
Definition and Limitations
According to the 1935 code, flat slabs are defined as slabs reinforced in two or four directions without beams or girders to carry the load to supporting members (p. 193). Standard design provisions apply to series of slabs of approximately uniform size arranged in three or more rows of panels in each direction, with a panel length-to-width ratio not exceeding 1.33 (p. 193–194). Irregular panels or structures narrower than three rows require specific moment analysis and computation submission for approval (p. 194).
Panel Strips and Design Sections
For design purposes, a flat slab panel is divided into two primary strips (p. 194):

Middle Strip: One-half the panel width, centered on the panel centerline.
Column Strip: The remaining one-half panel width, occupying the areas outside the middle strip.

Principal design sections for moment calculations are located at the panel edges and column centers for negative moments, and at the panel centerlines for positive moments (p. 194).
Moment Distribution and Thickness
The total numerical sum of positive and negative moments in a rectangular panel is calculated using specific moment-coefficients, such as M_o = 0.09Wl(1 - 2c/3l)^2 for general cases (p. 194). A simplified coefficient (M_o = 0.065Wl) applies when the column capital diameter c equals 0.225 times the average span length (p. 195). Moments are distributed between column and middle strips based on whether the slab uses two-way or four-way reinforcement and whether dropped panels are present (p. 195).
Minimum slab thickness is governed by span length, column capital size, and load intensity. For slabs without dropped panels using 2,000 psi concrete, the thickness t_1 is calculated as 0.038(1 - 1.44 c/l) l \sqrt{w'} + 1.5 inches (p. 196). Dropped panels must have a length or diameter of at least 0.35 of the panel length, and their depth cannot exceed 1.5 times the remainder of the slab (p. 194).
Reinforcement and Shear Requirements
Reinforcement in flat slabs must maintain a minimum ratio of 0.0025, with bar spacing not exceeding 1.5 times the slab thickness (p. 197). The code specifies precise bar lengths and extensions beyond points of inflection and into column heads for both two-way and four-way systems to adequately resist negative and positive moments (p. 197–198).
Shear stress is critical in flat slab design. The shearing unit stress at a section 1.5 inches from the column capital edge is limited based on the percentage of negative reinforcement passing directly over the capital, ranging from 0.025f'_c to 0.03f'_c (p. 191). Additional shear-reinforcement may be required if these limits are approached.
Special Conditions

Wall and Irregular Panels: Discontinuous edges increase maximum positive moments by 25%, and negative moments at wall edges are adjusted based on strip location (p. 198).
Marginal Beams: Beams along panel edges with depths greater than 1.5 times the minimum slab thickness must be designed to carry at least one-fourth of the adjacent panel’s total live and dead load (p. 198–199).
Openings: Openings are permitted in middle strips if total moment capacity is maintained, but are strictly limited in column strips to a maximum dimension of 1/20 of the span length (p. 199).

Flat slab construction represents a significant advancement in reinforced-concrete-standards, offering clear-span interiors while relying on rigorous column-design and moment distribution rules to manage structural stresses.
Related Documents

concepts/floor-and-roof-standards.title: floor-and-roof-standards
concepts/floor-and-roof-standards.content:
Floor and roof construction standards define the structural, material, and safety requirements for horizontal building elements, ensuring load-bearing capacity, fire resistance, and proper drainage. Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, these standards are detailed across multiple chapters and amendments, covering everything from joist spacing and cellular steel systems to roof drainage and metal coverings 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p225_2441935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p325_344.
Floor Construction Requirements
Floor systems must be integrally tied to the building’s framework and supporting walls, with beams and girders secured using ties or bridging to prevent separation (p. 240). Requirements vary by material:

Concrete Floors: Solid concrete floors must be at least four inches thick, while rib slab floors require a minimum of two and a half inches (p. 240). Concrete joists spanning over twenty feet must include lateral bridging (p. 240).
Steel Joisted Floors: These require a reinforced concrete or gypsum slab of at least two and a half inches on top, along with a fire-resistive ceiling underneath to protect the joists (p. 240). Joists must be cross-bridged at intervals not exceeding eight feet, and the lateral unsupported length of the top chord cannot exceed forty times the width of the compression flange (p. 240).
Cellular Steel Floors: Amendments introduce integrated systems of parallel steel beams that function as both load-bearing members and continuous decks. The steel must meet american-society-for-testing-materials standards (Grades A, B, or C) and be no thinner than U.S. Standard Gauge No. 18. Flexural stress is capped at 60% of the steel’s yield point, and deflection under full live load must not exceed 1/360 of the span. In fire-resistive-construction, these floors require a minimum of two inches of concrete fill and a suspended ceiling meeting national-board-fire-underwriters ratings (p. 331).
Wood and Mill Construction: Mill floors consist of at least three-inch nominal spliced plank covered with one-inch flooring laid crosswise or diagonally, leaving a half-inch gap at walls for swelling (p. 241). Wood joisted floors are framed and anchored according to wood-framing-standards, with fire protection required only when mandated by occupancy or construction type (p. 241).
Special Floors: Public toilet floors must use impervious materials like asphalt, marble, or vitrified tile (p. 241). Public garages and service stations require approved grease drains or traps (p. 241).
Joist Span Tables: Updated structural tables define maximum allowable spans for floor and roof joists based on a fiber stress of 1200 psi and specific live load categories (e.g., 50# for second floors or tile roofs, 40# for first floors or flat roofs, and 30# for ceilings or attics) (p. 337).

Roof Construction and Framing
Roofs must be securely tied into the structural framework, with specific attention to trusses and rafters. Roof trusses require well-fitted joints, tightened tension members, and diagonal/sway bracing (p. 242). Rafters must be anchored and braced according to specified sections, and flat-deck roofs require an eighteen-inch ventilation air space between ceiling joists and roof rafters (p. 242). Sheathing requirements depend on construction type, with Type II buildings requiring at least two and a half inches of nominal thickness, while precast concrete or gypsum roof slabs must be at least two inches thick (p. 242). The aforementioned joist span tables also govern roof framing, dictating maximum spans based on load types and joist dimensions (p. 337).
Roof Coverings, Drainage, and Access
Roof coverings are classified based on occupancy, location, and construction type to ensure adequate fire resistance and durability (p. 242). Drainage systems must include metal downspouts connected to storm sewers or adequately sized sumps (minimum three by three feet and four feet deep) (p. 242). Downspout diameter must be at least one inch per 150 square feet of roof surface, with a minimum of four square inches total, and increased by one inch for basin-style roofs (p. 242). Parapet-enclosed roofs require weep holes equal in area to the downspouts for overflow (p. 242).
Access to roofs and attic spaces is mandatory, typically via stairways, permanent ladders, or scuttles. Openings must be at least two feet by three feet and located in hallways or corridors, with securely fastened scuttles covered in incombustible material for Class A or B roofing (p. 242). One- and two-story Type III or V buildings may use eighteen-inch square scuttles (p. 242).
Metal Roofing and Siding: Detailed standards govern the use of steel and aluminum roofing. Steel sheets require a minimum gauge of No. 26 on wood sheathing or No. 24 when spanning supports, with corrugations at least 1/2” high. Aluminum requires a minimum thickness of .024” on sheathing or .027” for spanning applications. Fastening mandates galvanized nails with lead or neoprene washers, and side laps must be bolted or riveted. To prevent galvanic corrosion, aluminum sheets cannot contact steel members without a protective bitumastic or asphalt-asbestos paint barrier (p. 339).
These standards integrate closely with fire-resistive-construction, steel-joist-design, and reinforced-concrete-standards to ensure structural integrity and life safety across diverse building types.

concepts/foundation-construction-require.title: foundation-construction-requirements
concepts/foundation-construction-require.content:
Foundation Construction Requirements
Foundation construction requirements establish the structural, material, and environmental standards necessary to ensure the stability and longevity of building bases. These regulations govern excavation, soil bearing, wood preservation, ventilation, concrete placement, and pile foundations to protect against moisture, decay, and structural failure. Amendments to the code further refine these requirements, specifically addressing curing times for masonry and structural details for low-height walls and concrete columns.
Excavation and Soil Bearing
Excavations must be protected against danger to life and property, utilizing retaining walls of masonry, reinforced concrete, or interlocking steel piling for permanent cuts (p. 219). Excavations cannot extend within one foot of the angle of repose under existing footings unless properly under-pinned (p. 219). Footings and foundations must be constructed of masonry, reinforced concrete, or structural steel encased in concrete, with masonry units laid in Portland cement mortar (p. 219).
Soil bearing capacities are strictly regulated: solid rock allows up to 5 tons per square foot, rock with cleaned pot holes filled with concrete allows 3 tons, natural bed sand allows 2.5 tons, and filled land allows only 0.5 tons (p. 219–220). When soil bearing capacity is uncertain, load tests are mandatory, requiring an area of at least two square feet to sustain twice the desired bearing capacity for 48 hours without additional settlement (p. 220). Foundations should rest on natural solid ground; filled land or organic soil is restricted to single-story Type V construction (p. 220).
Pile Foundations
When natural ground is insufficient, pile foundations must be driven to a reasonably solid bearing without eccentric loading (p. 220).

Wood Piles: Must be free of decay, driven green, and meet specific taper and diameter requirements (minimum 6-inch point, 10-12 inch butt depending on length) (p. 220). Untreated piles must be cut below mean low water or groundwater levels. Creosoted piles (Douglas fir or Southern pine) meeting american-wood-preservers-association standards may be cut above ground if properly capped and treated (p. 220–221). Allowable loads are calculated via drop or steam hammer formulas, capped at 20 tons for untreated and 15 tons for creosoted piles (p. 221).
Concrete Piles: Must comply with material and reinforcement standards. Allowable working stress cannot exceed 12% of ultimate compressive strength, except for steel-tube cast piles which allow up to 25% plus steel tube stress, capped at 50 tons (p. 221–222). Precast piles require minimum 1.2% longitudinal reinforcement and specific hooping, with length limits tied to diameter (p. 222).
Load Testing: Piles may require load testing to twice the design load for 48 hours, with settlement limits of 1/100th of the test load in tons for continuous structures (p. 222).

Wood Foundation Standards
Wood components in contact with masonry or concrete must be isolated using eighty-five-pound slate roofing or twenty-four-gauge galvanized iron to prevent moisture transfer (p. 165). Approved lumber must possess physical properties equal to eighty-five percent Heart Long Leaf Southern Yellow Pine, No. 1 Common Tidewater Red Cypress, or other specified heartwoods and pressure-treated materials (p. 165). Brush treatment is prohibited except for end cuts (p. 165). Wood furring and foundation screen vents require preservative treatment on all four sides (p. 165).
Floor joists must maintain a minimum clearance of eighteen inches above leveled ground to facilitate airflow and prevent rot (p. 165). Wood posts or columns cannot rest directly on concrete floors; they require concrete floatings extending at least two inches above the finished floor or corrosion-resistant metal plates (p. 165). Timbers embedded in masonry walls below ground level must be enclosed in metal wall boxes and coated with coal tar creosote (p. 165).
Foundation Ventilation
Adequate airflow beneath structures is critical for moisture control. Solid foundation walls under first-floor joists must include ventilated openings covered with non-corrosive wire mesh of at least sixteen mesh per linear inch (p. 165–166). Ventilation area must total at least two square feet for every fifteen linear feet of exterior wall, distributed across a minimum of three sides to ensure cross-ventilation (p. 166). These requirements align with broader ventilation-requirements to prevent dampness and structural degradation.
Foundation Wall Specifications
Foundation walls must extend below the frost line and cannot be constructed of gypsum (p. 236). Solid masonry and concrete block or coursed stone foundation walls require a minimum thickness of ten inches, unless otherwise specified (p. 236). To support superimposed loads, foundation walls must provide at least a four-inch bearing surface for wood joists and eight inches for unit masonry, resulting in a general minimum thickness of twelve inches (p. 236). Exceptions allow for an eight-inch minimum in Type V construction or when exterior masonry walls rest on engineered reinforced concrete floor slabs supported by a reinforced concrete foundation wall (p. 236).
Cast-in-place concrete foundation walls must be at least as thick as the walls they support, with an absolute minimum of eight inches (p. 236). Rubble stone foundation walls require a minimum thickness of sixteen inches, with height restrictions applied based on construction quality: rough or random rubble without bonding or level beds is prohibited for walls exceeding thirty-five feet, while coursed bonded rubble is limited to seventy-five feet (p. 236). Foundations for hollow clay tile, concrete block, or hollow brick walls must match the thickness requirements for solid masonry, with tile foundation walls requiring a minimum of twelve inches (p. 236).
If earth pressure and building loads exceed the maximum allowable working stresses for the chosen materials, wall thickness must be increased accordingly (p. 236). Materials must equal the quality required for exterior bearing walls, utilizing cement mortar for below-grade sections (p. 236). Additionally, masonry unit foundations must be sealed below any woodwork with a cement wash or equally effective seal to prevent moisture migration (p. 236).
Code amendments specify that all foundations for masonry construction must be allowed to cure for 12 hours before any masonry elements are laid (p. 334). For specific low-height structures, such as fences, planting box walls, or walls up to four feet in height, foundations must be at least ten inches wide and eight inches deep, reinforced with 2.5-inch rods (p. 334). U-Type Beam Blocks are permitted with an unsupported maximum span of seven feet, requiring reinforcement of two 7/8-inch rods placed at the top and bottom of the beam (p. 336).
Concrete Foundations, Pedestals, and Reinforcement
Concrete foundations and footings must adhere to strict reinforced-concrete-standards. Later updates to the code explicitly adopt the Building Code Requirements for Reinforced Concrete ACI 318-47 as the standard for reinforced concrete work (p. 336). Metal reinforcement in footings and principal structural members contacting the ground requires a minimum concrete cover of three inches (p. 181). Other surfaces exposed to weather or ground require at least two inches of cover (p. 181). Formwork must be removed only after concrete achieves sufficient strength, and all wood forms within the ground or less than eighteen inches above it must be removed before occupancy (p. 166, 180).
Concrete placement demands clean excavations, proper mixing, and continuous pouring to minimize construction joints where possible (p. 178, 181). Curing requires exposed surfaces to remain moist for at least seven days, with additional precautions in hot or freezing weather (p. 179). Quality control involves field testing, compression tests, and adherence to specified water-cement ratio limits to guarantee structural integrity (p. 173–174).
Pedestals and footings require careful load distribution analysis, including bending moment calculations for trapezoidal load areas (p. 205). Reinforcement spacing and pitch must be maintained with secure spacers, and lateral reinforcement ratios are capped at 0.015 (p. 206). Unreinforced pedestals are limited to a maximum compressive stress of 0.25f'_c and strict depth-to-width ratios, with projections limited to half the depth (p. 206).
Concrete columns are required at all corners and at specified intervals. These columns must be at least eight inches thick and twelve inches wide, reinforced with four 5/8-inch continuous vertical rods and 1/4-inch ties spaced on 12-inch centers. U-Type Blocks are explicitly prohibited for reinforced concrete column use (p. 336). Structural integrity at gable ends is ensured by an eight-inch by eight-inch reinforced concrete tie beam constructed similarly to a coping beam, required unless the rake and height are less than 24 inches (p. 338).
Inspection and Compliance
All foundation work is subject to building-inspection protocols. Inspectors verify material quality, reinforcement placement, concrete curing, and adherence to clearance and ventilation mandates (p. 170, 178). Contractors must maintain detailed records of material tests, mixing proportions, and placement dates, which remain on file for two years post-acceptance (p. 170, 179). Pile inspections may require certification from approved testing laboratories to verify conformity with creosote treatment and driving methods (p. 221). These regulations are detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p165_184, 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224, and 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p325_344, and form a critical component of the 1935-miami-dade-building-code.

concepts/heating-and-chimney-safety.title: heating-and-chimney-safety
concepts/heating-and-chimney-safety.content:
Heating and Chimney Safety
heating-and-chimney-safety encompasses the structural, material, and installation standards for chimneys, flues, smokestacks, gas vents, fireplaces, furnaces, and heating appliances. Governed primarily by Chapter 37 of the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, these regulations prioritize fire prevention, proper draft, and safe clearances from combustible materials.
Chimney Construction and Flue Linings
Chimneys must conform to standards recommended by the national-board-fire-underwriters and be designed according to local engineering requirements (p. 256). Walls must be constructed of brick, concrete, stone, or hollow tile, with a minimum thickness of eight inches (p. 259). Flue linings are mandatory, requiring fire clay or refractory materials with a softening point of at least 1994°F and a thickness of no less than five-eighths of an inch (p. 256). All joints between masonry and lining must be thoroughly slushed and grouted (p. 256). Chimneys must extend at least three feet above flat roofs or two feet above the nearest roof ridge (p. 259).
Smokestacks and Industrial Vents
Masonry and steel smokestacks used for high-temperature industrial applications must withstand wind stresses and conform to recognized engineering standards (p. 260). Steel stacks require a minimum metal thickness of one-quarter inch and must be lined with fire brick for at least twenty-five feet from the intake (p. 260). Stacks exceeding a height ten times their diameter must be properly guyed using stranded wire and turn buckles (p. 260). Industrial smokestacks mounted on boilers must maintain a six-inch clearance, with inflammable materials within twelve inches protected by asbestos and sheet metal (p. 260).
Gas Vents and Patent Chimneys
Gas appliances require vents constructed of unglazed fire clay or concrete tile pipe at least one-half inch thick, featuring sleeves or flanges at every joint to ensure secure connections and air spacing (p. 261). Vents must maintain a rise of at least one inch per foot and extend two feet above any roof portion within a fifteen-foot radius (p. 261). ventilation-requirements for gas vents mandate that vents be continuous, vertical, and clear of combustibles, with metal vent connections maintained at least six inches away from unprotected wood (p. 261). Patent chimneys, permitted for stoves but not fireplaces, require a fire clay flue lining enclosed in a metal casing with a one-inch air space, built plumb from the floor level and braced every six feet (p. 262).
Fireplaces, Furnaces, and Boilers
Fireplace walls must be at least eight inches thick, with faces exposed to fire lined with fire brick, soap stone, or cast iron (p. 263). Hearths must be supported by trimmer arches or reinforced concrete slabs, extending at least twenty inches from the chimney breast and twelve inches wider than the opening on each side (p. 263). Warm air furnaces burning solid or liquid fuel must be encased in a double metal shield with an air space, protected with three inches of sand on top, and installed on masonry floors (p. 263). Combustible partitions must be kept at least seven feet from the front and four feet from the sides of the furnace, or protected with sheet metal and asbestos paper (p. 263). Adequate combustion air supply is required, with outdoor intake openings providing a net area of at least 400 square inches (p. 264).
Low-pressure steam heating plants (up to 15 pounds pressure) and hot water heaters using solid or liquid fuel must rest upon masonry or reinforced concrete floors and be protected on the outside by asbestos (p. 265). Large boilers for power or high-pressure steam require significant clearance: no combustible material less than five feet from the top or sides, or ten feet from the front (p. 265). Combustible material closer than these distances must be protected with at least four inches of concrete, brick, or similar incombustible material, and the area must be ventilated to keep temperatures below 125°F (p. 265).
Stoves and Gas Ranges
Domestic stoves used for heating, cooking, or laundry must have combustible partitions behind and beside them protected by fire-resistive-construction (p. 265). Stoves must be supported at least twelve inches above wood floors by metal supports and placed on a metal and asbestos pad extending six inches beyond the stove on each side and twelve inches in front (p. 265). Stoves must not be placed nearer than six inches to any combustible partition (p. 265).
Gas ranges and domestic water heaters must be supported at least six inches above wood floors or combustible materials (p. 265). Where burners lack a bottom shield, wood materials must be protected with a double metal shield and a one-inch air space, or a half-inch metal and asbestos pad (p. 265). Combustible partitions within six inches of these appliances require asbestos and metal covering or one-hour fire-resistive protection (p. 266). Wood ceilings must be at least three feet above gas installations (p. 266).
Warm Air Ducts
Warm air ducts and appurtenances must be constructed of bright tin or galvanized iron (p. 267). For gravity systems, leader heat pipes shall not exceed twenty feet in horizontal length without a booster fan (p. 267). Ducts must be insulated with asbestos paper or double-walled with an air space, and horizontal pipes must be kept three inches from combustibles or protected with an asbestos shield (p. 267). Joists and studs must provide at least fourteen inches of clear space for continuous duct runs (p. 268).
Incinerators and Solar Heaters
Built-in incinerators require enclosing walls of solid masonry or reinforced concrete, at least eight inches thick for small chambers and twelve inches for larger ones (p. 268). The inner four inches of combustion chamber walls must be lined with fire brick laid in fire clay or cement mortar (p. 268).
solar water heaters must be constructed of galvanized iron or equal rust-resistant material, anchored to galvanized angle iron supports, and designed to operate under at least 100 pounds of pressure (p. 268). Tanks must be insulated with at least five inches of ground cork to prevent heat loss (p. 269).
Oil Burners
Oil burning apparatus must be constructed and erected similarly to solid fuel apparatus (p. 266). Installation must comply with regulations from the national-board-fire-underwriters and the national-fire-protection-association regarding the construction, installation, and storage of oil fuels (p. 266).

concepts/masonry-standards.title: masonry-standards
concepts/masonry-standards.content:
Masonry Standards
Masonry standards define the quality, testing, and structural design requirements for materials used in masonry construction within the 1935-miami-dade-building-code. The code classifies masonry to include brick, plain concrete, concrete blocks, gypsum, hollow tile, and stone, all of which must meet strict performance thresholds to ensure structural integrity and fire resistance.
Material Quality and Testing
All masonry materials must conform to specifications set by the american-society-for-testing-materials (ASTM). The Building Inspector retains the authority to mandate structural and fire-resistive tests if material quality is in question, with costs borne by the owner or contractor. Specific requirements include:

Brick: Burned clay, sand-lime, and concrete bricks must pass a five-hour immersion test with an average water absorption of no more than 12% and a minimum flatwise modulus of rupture of 450 psi.
Hollow Concrete Block: Units for exterior or party walls must meet gross cross-sectional compressive strength thresholds (700–1,000 psi) and absorb ≤10% water in a 24-hour test. Certificates from underwriters-laboratories may substitute for physical testing.
Gypsum: When used structurally in floors or roofs, gypsum must achieve compressive strengths ranging from 500 to 1,800 psi depending on whether it is neat, fiber-reinforced, or mixed with coarse aggregates.
Glass & Veneer Materials: Glass building units must not carry loads beyond their own weight, and decorative glass veneer must be at least 11/32” thick with strict limits on panel size (max 10 sq ft below 15’, max 6 sq ft above) (p. 227, p. 232). Stone, terra cotta, and cast stone veneers have minimum thickness requirements ranging from 1¼” to 3” (p. 231).

Mortar Specifications
Mortar composition is strictly regulated based on the building’s exposure to moisture. Only lime-cement or cement mortar is permitted for unit masonry. Cement mortar is mandatory for isolated piers, footings, exterior foundation walls, and any masonry below grade (p. 225, p. 236). The code provides a specific formula to calculate the allowable working stress for mortars with intermediate proportions of cement and lime. For hollow masonry, all contact surfaces must be solidly embedded in mortar with a full bond, and inner/outer courses must bond at vertical intervals not exceeding three courses (p. 228).
Structural Design and Stresses
Design must account for structural-loads, including eccentric loads and lateral forces. Concentrated loads are permitted to exceed standard limits by up to 25% if properly distributed. Maximum allowable-working-stress values for compression are determined by the unit’s material strength and the type of mortar used. For example, brick masonry laid in Portland cement mortar can withstand higher compressive stresses than the same brick laid in lime mortar. Similar stress tables apply to concrete blocks, load-bearing clay tile, and various stone types like granite, limestone, and coral rock.
Wall thickness scales with building height to manage compressive stresses. For solid masonry, the uppermost 35 feet must be at least 12 inches thick, increasing by 4 inches per successive 35 feet downward (p. 224). Hollow walls follow a similar progression but require a minimum of 12 inches for the top 25 feet, increasing by 4 inches per 25 feet downward (p. 227). Reinforced concrete bearing walls must be at least 1/25 of the unsupported height, with specific thickness tables provided for bearing and non-bearing applications (p. 228-230). Stone rubble walls must be 4 inches thicker than solid brick walls of the same height, never less than 16 inches (p. 230).
Wall Construction, Bonding, and Lateral Support
Solid masonry walls require robust lateral support to resist wind and seismic forces. Wall panels must be supported at right angles at intervals ensuring no panel exceeds 256 sq. ft., with vertical supports no more than 20 feet apart (p. 223). Lateral support is achieved through masonry cross walls, concrete piers, columns, buttresses, floors, roofs, or concrete tie beams (p. 223). Reinforced concrete tie-beams are mandatory below each tier of floor or ceiling joists, over main interior horizontal frames, and atop walls as coping. These tie-beams must be at least 8 inches wide and 12 inches high, reinforced with four 5/8-inch rods (two top, two bottom) (p. 223). Coping beams (minimum 8x8 inches) reinforced with two 1/2-inch rods must be anchored to the tie-beam below using 8x12 inch concrete struts reinforced with four 1/2-inch rods (p. 223). Additional tie-beams are required on the rake at the end of gables (p. 223).
Bonding requirements ensure structural continuity. At least every sixth course on both sides of a solid masonry wall must be a header course, or there must be one full header per 72 sq in of wall surface (p. 225). Piers of solid unit masonry must be laid in cement mortar, with unsupported height not exceeding ten times their least dimension (p. 225). Chases and recesses are strictly limited: no chases in walls ≤12” thick, max depth of 1/3 wall thickness, and aggregate area of recesses/chases per story cannot exceed 1/4 of the wall face area (p. 226).
Veneered and faced walls require specific attachment methods. Brick, tile, or stone veneer must be tied into the backing with headers or non-corrosive metal ties spaced 1 ft vertically and 2 ft horizontally (p. 231). Glass veneer requires a rigid, incombustible backing, approved mastic cement, and metal shelf angles for support above sidewalk level (p. 232). Faced walls bonded to the backing can have the facing counted toward wall thickness and bearing strength (p. 234).
All walls must be securely anchored at intersections and skeleton frame abutments, using 2”x3/8” metal anchors or cross pins spaced 3 ft apart vertically (p. 237). Existing walls reused in renovations must meet current code thickness or be strengthened with an 8-inch cement mortar addition or gunite lining, thoroughly bonded via toothings (p. 237).
Fire Resistance, Partitions, and Enclosures
Masonry construction plays a critical role in fire-resistive-construction and fire-separation. Solid masonry and reinforced concrete fire walls must match the thickness of exterior bearing walls of corresponding height, never less than 12 inches (solid) or 8 inches (reinforced concrete) (p. 234). Fire walls must be continuous from foundation to 18 inches above the roof level (p. 234). Hollow masonry fire walls are prohibited in Fire Zone No. 1 and must be at least 16 inches thick (12 inches for Group H/I buildings) (p. 235).
Interior bearing partitions spanning only one story must be at least 8 inches thick for solid masonry, or 1/18 of the floor height for hollow units (p. 236). Non-bearing partitions of brick, hollow tile, or reinforced plaster have thickness limits based on unsupported height, ranging from 2 inches (max 8 ft height) to 8 inches (max 25 ft height) (p. 236).
Vertical openings, including stairways, ramps, elevators, and utility shafts, require enclosed, fire-resistive construction. Stairway and elevator enclosures must be at least two-hour fire-resistive, with self-closing doors and wire glass (p. 238-239). Elevator shafts are limited to two elevators per enclosure, with one-hour fire walls between separate banks (p. 238). Foundation walls must extend below frost action, be at least 10 inches thick for solid masonry, and use cement mortar below grade (p. 236-237).

concepts/moment-coefficients.title: moment-coefficients
concepts/moment-coefficients.content:
Moment coefficients are empirical factors used in structural engineering to calculate bending moments in beams and slabs under uniform loading, simplifying complex indeterminate structural analysis. In the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, these coefficients provide standardized formulas for designing reinforced-concrete-standards based on support conditions and span continuity.
Freely Supported or Slightly Restrained Members
For beams and slabs of approximately equal spans with partial end restraint or masonry wall supports, the code specifies the following moment coefficients (where w is load per unit length and l is span length) 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204:

One span: Positive moment near center = wl^2/8
Two continuous spans: Positive moment = wl^2/10; Negative moment over interior support = wl^2/8
More than two spans: Positive/negative moments for interior spans = wl^2/12; Positive/negative moments for end spans = wl^2/10
End supports: Negative moment = wl^2/24

Fully Restrained Continuous Members
For integrally built continuous beams and slabs with rigid support connections, the coefficients adjust to account for higher restraint 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204:

Interior spans: Negative moment at supports = wl^2/12; Positive moment near center = wl^2/16
End spans: Positive moment and negative moment at the first interior support = wl^2/12 (subject to moment of inertia ratios)

Two-Way Slabs and Flat Slabs
In two-way reinforced floors, these coefficients are applied to distribute loads across short and long panel dimensions based on aspect ratios 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204. For flat-slabs, the total static moment M_o for interior panels is calculated using the formula M_o = 0.09Wl(1 - 2c/3l)^2, where W is the total load, l is the clear span, and c is the column capital dimension. This total moment is then distributed between column strips and middle strips using specified ratios for negative and positive moments. Wall panels and discontinuous edges require a 25% increase in positive moments to account for reduced continuity 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204.
These empirical coefficients streamline the design process while ensuring structural integrity under structural-loads, reducing the need for detailed elastic analysis in standard construction scenarios.
Related Documents

concepts/municipal-boundary-definitions.title: municipal-boundary-definitions
concepts/municipal-boundary-definitions.content:
Municipal Boundary Definitions
Municipal boundary definitions in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code establish the precise geographic jurisdictions for regulatory enforcement, particularly concerning fire-zones and construction standards. These boundaries are delineated through exhaustive legal descriptions that trace routes along major arterial roads, subdivision plats, and natural or man-made landmarks (p. 85).
Geographic Tracing and Landmarks
The code defines limits by following the centerlines or property lines of key thoroughfares such as ne-2nd-avenue, north-miami-avenue, nw-7th-avenue, sw-17th-avenue, and west-flagler-street (p. 86). Major infrastructure and geographic features serve as critical reference points, including the florida-east-coast-railroad right-of-way, federal-highway, seybold-canal, and south-bayshore-drive (p. 90). The descriptions frequently reference specific lots, blocks, and historical subdivisions (e.g., Miami Shores, Biscayne Shores, ashbury-park) to pinpoint exact jurisdictional edges (p. 85).
Later amendments further refine these boundaries through detailed legal descriptions. For instance, the expansion of Fire Zone Number I traces a complex perimeter along Northwest Fourteenth Avenue, Northwest Twentieth Street, and Northwest Seventh Avenue, extending through numerous subdivisions like Greenwood Park, Highland Park, Spring Garden Subdivisions, and johnson-and-waddell-addition (pp. 341–343). The boundary description also utilizes natural waterways and bridges, following the seybold-canal to the miami-river, crossing the Northwest Fifth Street Bridge, and proceeding along various terraces and boulevards before looping back to the starting point (pp. 341–344).
Inter-Municipal Boundaries
The definitions carefully navigate the interfaces between neighboring jurisdictions, notably the city-of-miami and coral-gables city limits. The text explicitly tracks boundary lines as they cross municipal borders, ensuring clear regulatory handoffs and consistent enforcement across adjacent communities (p. 87).
Regulatory Application and Fire Zone Expansion
These precise boundary definitions are foundational for applying zoning and safety regulations. By establishing exact limits, the code enables the enforcement of specific construction requirements, such as the restrictions on Type V and Type IV construction within designated Fire Zone No. 1 (p. 104). This systematic approach to municipal-boundary-definitions ensures that building codes, fire-resistive-construction mandates, and building-permits are applied consistently within legally defined areas, as detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104.
Subsequent ordinances utilize these boundary mechanisms to expand safety zones. Ordinance No. 2471, for example, adds a substantial area to Fire Zone Number I specifically to prevent the erection of new frame structures and reduce fire hazards in the downtown area and its outskirts (p. 341). The ordinance mandates strict compliance, with violations punishable by fines or imprisonment, underscoring the legal weight of these geographic delineations (p. 344).

concepts/occupancy-classification.title: occupancy-classification
concepts/occupancy-classification.content:
Occupancy classification is a foundational principle in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code that categorizes buildings and structures based on their intended use, which directly dictates their safety, construction, and maintenance requirements (p. 5). The city-of-miami code organizes all structures into distinct groups, ranging from Group A to Group J, each with specific regulations tailored to the unique hazards and needs of that use (p. 65). The building-inspection authority is responsible for classifying every building according to its use or character of occupancy (p. 41).
Classification Groups and Requirements
The code divides occupancies into groups covering assembly, business, residential, industrial, and institutional facilities. Once a building’s occupancy is determined, it dictates allowable construction types, maximum height and area, stair and exit configurations, light and ventilation standards, sanitation provisions, and required fire extinguishing apparatus (p. 67). This systematic approach ensures that buildings are designed to mitigate risks specific to their function.
Specific groups include:

Group A (Large Assembly): Theaters, auditoriums, and similar venues with 1,000+ seats require Type 1 Construction with no height or area limits (p. 45). This group also encompasses churches, libraries, museums, schools, and gymnasiums (p. 368-371). emergency-egress mandates exits on three sides, a main foyer, and smokeproof towers for multiple balconies (p. 46).
Group B (Medium Assembly): Venues with 300–1,000 seats allow Types I–III construction with height and area limits; foyers are waived and balconies restricted to Type I buildings (p. 51).
Group C (Small Assembly): Spaces with fewer than 300 seats permit Types I–V construction, with floor areas determined by street frontage (p. 54). Classrooms may have exits on one side if travel distance is under 35 feet (p. 57).
Group D (Institutional): Detention facilities require Type I construction, while hospitals mandate ramps with a 1:6 slope for bedridden patients (p. 60). This group also includes asylums, jails, nurseries, orphanages, prisons, and reformatories (p. 371-372).
Group E (Hazardous): High-risk uses like aircraft hangars, storage of flammable liquids, and public garages are restricted. This group additionally covers bus stations, dry cleaning plants, mattress factories, and planing mills (p. 368, 370-372). The code mandates at least two emergency-egress per story, with specific rules for automobile ramps and smokeproof towers (p. 65). Mechanical ventilation must supply 30 cubic feet of air per minute per occupant, and separate toilet facilities are required based on sex and race (p. 65). Dry cleaning plants require Type I construction, four-hour fire-resistive-construction partitions, and power-driven exhaust systems (p. 66). Public garages cannot be Type V construction, face strict height limits, must use incombustible floor materials, and gasoline filling stations require incombustible exterior wall coverings (p. 67). Service stations require approved grease drains (p. 67).
Group F (Moderately Hazardous): Includes wholesale/retail stores, offices, factories, and storage for combustible goods. This group also regulates police stations and undertaking parlors (p. 372-375). Maximum allowable floor areas are determined by construction type, building height, and street frontage, allowing a 100% area increase for complete sprinkler-systems (p. 68). Draft stops are required in rooms exceeding 30,000 square feet (p. 68). Exterior walls within four feet of property lines must be masonry or reinforced concrete (p. 68). Storage of volatile inflammable liquids is prohibited unless compliant with the 1930 National Board of Fire Underwriters ordinance (p. 69).
Group G (Non-Hazardous): Covers industrial and commercial occupancies with low fire and life hazards, such as ice plants, power plants, pumping plants, creameries, and storage of incombustible materials (p. 369, 371). Similar area/height tables apply, capping Type V construction at three stories and 38 feet (p. 71). Unlike higher-hazard groups, vertical openings do not require enclosure except as specified in Chapter 33 (p. 72), and under-roof fireproofing may be omitted (p. 72).
Group H (Residential/Hotels): Regulates hotels, apartment houses, dormitories, rooming houses, convents, monasteries, and old peoples homes (p. 369, 372). These buildings must comply with the florida-hotel-state-commission rules (p. 73). Dimensional limits are stricter, restricting Type V construction to two stories plus an attic (p. 74). Living and sleeping rooms must feature windows comprising at least 15% of the floor area (p. 75). All stairs and exits must open directly to a street, alley, or approved yard/court (p. 75). Boiler rooms require “Special fire-separation” from the rest of the building (p. 75).
Group I (Dwellings & Small Institutions): Applies to dwellings, garage apartments for up to two families, and small hospitals/sanitariums (p. 370, 373). Floor areas are capped at 10,000 sq ft for Types III/IV and 7,500 sq ft for Type V (p. 76). It specifies a minimum ceiling height of 8 feet 4 inches (p. 78). Strict fire-resistive-construction separation is required for attached private garages; the ceiling and walls must have a one-hour fire resistance, openings are restricted to a single doorway protected by a gravity-closing solid wood or metal door without glass, and dedicated exhaust ventilation openings are required near the floor (p. 77, 139-140).
Group J (Accessory & Amusement): Covers private garages, sheds, stadiums, amusement park structures, tents, towers, and reviewing stands (p. 368, 375). Spectator venues require specific stair dimensions, exit widths (one 7-foot-wide exit per 2,000 persons), and aisle spacing (every 20 seats) to ensure safe emergency-egress (p. 79). Private garages attached to residential buildings need fixed louvered or exhaust ventilation, and direct openings into living/sleeping rooms are banned (p. 80). Garages storing over three cars require chemical extinguishers (p. 80).

Vertical Transportation and Special Apparatus
Occupancy classification dictates the requirements for elevators, escalators, dumbwaiters, and hoists, which are critical for multi-story buildings across various groups. The code mandates that all such lifting apparatus undergo regular inspection and receive a certificate of operation from the Building Inspector (p. 365-366). Ordinance No. 2149 establishes that inspection reports must be filed by either city employees or approved insurance/casualty company inspectors (p. 365). Fees for these inspections are set at $2.50 plus 50 cents for the certificate, though the inspection fee is waived for approved private inspectors after July 1, 1939 (p. 366). These regulations ensure that vertical-transportation-safety is maintained regardless of whether the apparatus serves a public assembly, a hospital, or a commercial warehouse (p. 369).
Structural Load Requirements by Occupancy
Occupancy classification directly governs the structural-loads a building must support. The code establishes minimum unit live loads in pounds per square foot based on the intended use, ensuring structural adequacy for varying occupancies. For example, dwellings and hospital wards require 40 psf, offices require 50 psf, retail stores require 75 psf, and manufacturing or heavy storage facilities require 125 psf or more (p. 141-142). Additionally, allowable floor areas for construction-types like Type IV and Type V buildings are explicitly capped based on their assigned occupancy group and location within the city (p. 126, 128).
Common Standards and Site Requirements
Regardless of the specific occupancy group, the code enforces baseline standards for sanitation, ventilation, and site placement. ventilation-requirements dictate that all habitable spaces receive natural light/air via windows or skylights covering at least 1/8 of the floor area, or a mechanical system providing a specified air exchange rate (p. 69, 375). Buildings employing more than four people must provide toilets appropriate for their occupancy group, with specific requirements listed for Groups A through J (p. 375). Furthermore, every occupancy group is subject to mandatory set-back regulations to ensure adequate spacing from property lines and public ways (p. 373). sprinkler-systems and standpipes are consistently referenced across groups to mitigate fire risks (p. 66). Across all groups, the code mandates independent central meter rooms for multiple-occupancy buildings (p. 67, 70, 73, 76, 78).
Mixed Occupancies and Fire Separation
Buildings housing multiple uses are subject to strict regulations regarding mixed occupancies. When different portions of a building are placed in different occupancy Groups, a fire-separation must be provided to entirely segregate each group per Section 503 (p. 42, 67, 70, 73, 76, 81). These separations are classified as:

Absolute: 4-hour resistance, no openings allowed.
Special: 3-hour resistance, restricted openings with self-closing doors.
Ordinary: 1-hour resistance, openings protected by self-closing metal-clad doors (p. 42).
A detailed tabulation specifies the required separation levels between various groups to prevent fire spread (p. 44).

Changes in Use
Changing the occupancy of an existing building triggers significant compliance obligations. No change in the character of occupancy shall be made without a certificate-of-occupancy permitting the new use (p. 41). If a use changes to one that would not be permitted in a newly erected building, the entire structure must be brought up to code, unless the new use is segregated or remains within the same occupancy group (p. 19). Furthermore, any change in occupancy requires a revised occupancy permit certifying that the building’s floors are structurally suitable for the live loads characteristic of the proposed use (p. 144).
Integration with Building Permits & Fire Zones
Determining the correct occupancy classification is a critical step in the building-permits process. Applicants must specify the intended use for all parts of a building when filing plans, and this classification works alongside fire-zones to establish the complete regulatory framework for a project (p. 5). The code divides the entire city into three fire zones: Zone 1 covers the densest commercial and residential areas near the miami-river and biscayne-bay (p. 81-82), while Zone 2 extends north along major arteries like federal-highway and biscayne-boulevard (p. 83-84). Stricter construction and relocation standards apply within these zones, particularly for Types IV and V buildings. As detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p5_24, proper classification ensures that structural, fire, and life-safety standards align with the building’s operational risks.

concepts/pile-foundation-engineering.title: pile-foundation-engineering
concepts/pile-foundation-engineering.content:
Pile Foundation Engineering
Pile foundation engineering involves the design, installation, and testing of piles to transfer structural loads to competent soil or rock strata. According to the 1935 code, all piles must be driven to a reasonably solid bearing without eccentric loading (p. 220).
Wood Piles
Wood piles, typically Dade County pine, must be driven green, free of decay or sharp bends, and maintain a uniform taper (p. 220). Minimum diameters are 6 inches at the point and 10 to 12 inches at the butt depending on length. Piles must be cut below the mean low water or lowest groundwater level, unless treated with creosote. Creosoted Douglas fir or Southern pine piles must meet retention standards set by the american-wood-preservers-association and may be cut above water if properly capped and inspected (p. 220-221). Spacing must be at least 2 feet 6 inches center-to-center. Allowable loads are calculated using hammer weight, drop height, and penetration formulas, capped at 20 tons for untreated and 15 tons for creosoted piles (p. 221).
Concrete and Steel Piles
Concrete piles must comply with material standards, with allowable working stress limited to 12% of the concrete’s ultimate compressive strength (p. 221). Steel-tube concrete piles can carry up to 50 tons. Cast-in-place piles are limited to 30 times their average diameter in length, while precast piles require 1.2% longitudinal reinforcement and specific hooping, with length limits of 20 to 40 times their diameter (p. 222). Rolled structural steel piles must have a minimum metal thickness of 3/8 inch and follow allowable load formulas or pass a 1.5x test load (p. 222).
Load Testing and Settlement
When required by the Building Inspector, pile capacity is verified through load tests. Test piles are loaded to twice the design load and sustained for 48 hours until settlement stabilizes (p. 222). Total settlement must not exceed 1/100th of the test load in tons for continuous spans, or double that amount for non-continuous spans. These requirements ensure foundation-construction-requirements are met for structures subjected to significant structural-loads.
See also: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224

concepts/reinforced-concrete-standards.title: reinforced-concrete-standards
concepts/reinforced-concrete-standards.content:
The reinforced-concrete-standards established by the 1935-miami-dade-building-code provide a comprehensive framework for the design, material quality, and construction of concrete structures. These regulations require the submission of detailed drawings and calculations prior to issuing building-permits and mandate continuous building-inspection to ensure compliance (p. 166, 170). In 1949, the code was updated to formally adopt the Building Code Requirements for Reinforced Concrete ACI 318-47, aligning municipal standards with the american-concrete-institute specifications (p. 336).
Materials and Testing
All concrete materials, including Portland cement, aggregates, and metal reinforcement, must adhere to standards set by the american-society-for-testing-materials (p. 170–172). The code identifies the water-cement ratio as the critical factor for determining concrete quality, prescribing specific ratios to achieve 28-day compressive strengths between 1,500 and 3,000 psi (p. 173). Quality control requires field testing using both control cylinders, cured under ideal conditions, and job cylinders, which are cured on-site to reflect actual structural conditions (p. 174).
Design and Stresses
Structural design relies on allowable-working-stress principles, with unit stresses for flexure, shear, and compression derived from the concrete’s minimum ultimate 28-day strength (f'_c) (p. 176). The code defines a modular ratio (n) based on concrete strength to account for the difference in elasticity between steel and concrete (p. 182). For structures designed to withstand wind-pressure-design loads, allowable unit stresses may be increased by 50% (p. 184).
Structural Member Design
The code provides detailed empirical rules for beams, slabs, and continuous systems. The effective depth of a beam or slab is measured from the centroid of tensile reinforcement to the top surface, and for symmetrically loaded equal spans, the point of inflection is assumed at the fifth point of the span (p. 185). T-beam and ribbed floor constructions require integral slab-beam formation, with effective flange widths and rib spacing strictly capped to ensure composite action (p. 185–186). moment-coefficients are explicitly provided for uniformly loaded continuous beams and slabs, differentiating between freely supported/slightly restrained systems and fully restrained continuous systems (p. 186–188). For two-way reinforced floors, load distribution depends on panel aspect ratios, with minimum shrinkage reinforcement ratios specified based on bar type (p. 189).
Shear and diagonal tension requirements dictate when shear-reinforcement such as vertical stirrups or bent-up bars must be provided, with spacing limited based on stress levels (p. 189–190). Bond stress computations govern anchorage lengths, requiring sufficient embedment beyond support faces and points of inflection to develop full tensile capacity (p. 191–192). Special anchorage rules apply for high shear stresses or unusual members, mandating personal supervision by the designing engineer (p. 192–193).
flat-slabs are defined as beamless slabs reinforced in two or four directions, with standard design provisions limited to panels with length-to-width ratios not exceeding 1.33 (p. 193–194). Moment distribution is divided between column strips and middle strips, with specific coefficients provided for slabs with or without dropped panels, and minimum thickness calculated using span length, column capital size, and load intensity (p. 195–196). column-design limits unsupported lengths and lateral dimensions, with spiral columns permitting higher axial loads based on core area and reinforcement ratio, while tied columns use gross area calculations (p. 200–201). Composite steel-concrete columns and long columns include explicit reduction factors for buckling based on slenderness ratios (p. 202–204).
Footings are proportioned for total column load plus self-weight, with critical bending sections located at column faces (p. 204). For trapezoidal load areas, bending moments are calculated assuming corner triangle loads act at a distance equal to six-tenths of the footing projection, while rectangular portions act at their center of gravity (p. 205). In sloped or stepped footings, the effective area may be calculated as the lower base of a frustum with 1:2 vertical-to-horizontal slopes (p. 206). Unreinforced pedestals are restricted to a maximum compressive stress of 0.25f'_c and strict depth-to-width ratios (p. 206). When footings are supported directly on piles, a mat of reinforcing bars (minimum 0.20 sq. in. per ft. in each direction) must be placed 3 inches above the pile tops (p. 206). Lateral reinforcement spacing in pedestals and piers must maintain a pitch between 2 and 5 inches, secured by spacers, with a maximum reinforcement ratio of 0.015 (p. 206).
Construction Practices and Masonry Integration
Concrete placement must be continuous to avoid construction joints; where joints are necessary, surfaces must be roughened and cleaned before resuming work (p. 179, 181). The code mandates minimum concrete cover for reinforcement to ensure durability and fire resistance. In fire-resistive-construction, reinforcement must be covered by at least 1 inch of concrete in slabs and 1.5 inches in beams and columns, potentially supplemented by metal mesh (p. 181). Curing requires exposed surfaces to remain moist for at least seven days, with strict temperature controls required for cold-weather placement (p. 179). To ensure long-term durability, the code mandates specific corrosion protection for embedded steel. Structural steel intended for concrete encasement must not be painted, and any steel rendered inaccessible after erection must be thoroughly cleaned and encased in Portland cement concrete before concealment (p. 218).
Amendments also address the integration of concrete with masonry and block construction. All foundations for masonry construction must cure for a minimum of 12 hours before laying masonry elements (p. 334). Concrete columns are required at wall corners and specified intervals, with a minimum thickness of 8 inches and width of 12 inches. These columns must be reinforced with four 5/8-inch continuous vertical rods and 1/4-inch ties spaced on 12-inch centers, with U-Type Blocks explicitly prohibited for column use (p. 336). Additionally, U-Type beam blocks are limited to a maximum unsupported span of 7 feet and require a minimum of two 7/8-inch reinforcing rods, placed at the top and bottom (p. 336). Gable ends must feature an 8x8 inch reinforced concrete tie beam, constructed similarly to coping beams, unless the rake height is under 24 inches (p. 338).

concepts/roofing-standards.title: roofing-standards
concepts/roofing-standards.content:
Roofing Standards
Roofing standards in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code establish strict requirements for material composition, layering, installation methods, and fire resistance to ensure structural durability and public safety. These regulations categorize roof coverings into “Fire Retardant” and “Ordinary” classes, each with specific performance benchmarks and testing protocols. Recent amendments have expanded these standards to include detailed specifications for metal roofing and siding.
Classification of Roof Coverings
The code divides roofing materials into two primary categories:

Fire Retardant Roofings: Designed to meet Class A or B specifications of the underwriters-laboratories. These typically require multiple layers of saturated felt, asphalt, or mineral surfacing. Installation mandates thorough mopping between layers with bituminous compounds (air-refined asphalt or coal tar pitch) to ensure no unmapped layers touch. The top layer must be No. 82 mineral-surfaced roofing or entirely covered with a flowing bituminous coat and embedded gravel or crushed rock 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304.
Ordinary Roofings: Meets Class C specifications or permits fewer layers of specific felt combinations, asphalt shingles, or approved wood shingles. Wood shingles must be 100% heartwood from non-resinous species (e.g., red cedar, cypress, redwood) and maintain uniform thickness and vertical grain standards, with specific exposure limits based on roof pitch 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304.

Metal Roofing and Siding
Amendments to the code introduce comprehensive standards for metal roofing and siding, including corrugated, deck, standing seam, V crimp, and flat types 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p325_344.

Steel: Steel roofing and siding must have a protective coating. If not exposed to weather, paint is sufficient. Exposed sheets require a zinc or other non-corrosive metal coating, or may be coated with asphalt and asphalt-impregnated felt. Stainless steel is approved without a protective coating.
Aluminum: Aluminum roofing and siding may be of the same types as steel but are subject to specific gauge and fastener requirements. Aluminum sheets are not permitted in direct contact with steel members; a barrier such as bitumastic paint, aluminum asphalt asbestos combination paint, or an equal material must be provided.

Material Specifications and Physical Properties
Roofing felts and membranes are standardized by weight, saturation percentage, and fiber composition (vegetable/animal vs. asbestos). Common designations include No. 15, No. 18, No. 20, No. 30, No. 32, and No. 82, each with precise dry felt weights and bituminous saturation rates. Physical properties are rigorously defined:

Pliability: Tested via mandrel bending at 77°F to ensure materials do not crack under installation stress.
Heat Resistance: Roll roofing heated to 176°F for two hours must not lose more than 1.8% volatile matter or show surface sagging/blistering. Felt membranes heated to 221°F for five hours must retain at least 96% of their weight.
Surface Integrity: Mineral surfacing must be uniformly embedded and resistant to dusting or sliding when suspended vertically 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304.

Metal Gauge Requirements:

Steel: Minimum gauge No. 26 when used on solid wood sheathing. When spanning between supports without sheathing, minimum gauge is No. 24 with corrugations at least 1/2” high.
Aluminum: Minimum gauge .024 (24 gauge) on solid wood sheathing. Minimum gauge .027 when decking is eliminated, with corrugations at least 1/2” high. An exception exists for 2 1/2”x1/2”x.027 corrugated sheets, which are limited to spans of 30” 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p325_344.

Installation and Testing Requirements
All roofing must be securely fastened to solid sheathing using non-corrosive nails with minimum penetration depths (typically ¾ inch). Exposed wood edges and gravel roof perimeters require non-corrosive metal flashing to prevent weather damage and material displacement. Testing methodologies adhere to american-society-for-testing-materials Designation D 146-27, ensuring standardized evaluation of bituminous-saturated fabrics 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304.
Metal Installation Specifics:

Fastening: Steel sheets on sheathing are fastened with approved galvanized roofing nails and minimum 3/8” diameter lead or neoprene washers, spaced 12” centers at side laps. Nailing must be done through high corrugations, standing seams, or V crimps.
Spanning Without Sheathing: When metal sheets span supports (purlins, rafters, girts) without sheathing, load carrying capacity must be established by manufacturer’s load tables or mathematical computations. Steel uses a fiber stress of 18,000 lbs per square inch; aluminum uses a factor of safety of 2 and a fiber stress not exceeding 12,000 lbs per square inch. Fastening may involve bolts, rivets, or heavy aluminum/asphalt-coated steel straps 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p325_344.
Aluminum Fasteners: Aluminum, hot-dipped galvanized, or cadmium-plated nails, bolts, screws, or rivets must be used. Washers may be aluminum, lead, or neoprene.

Roofing standards intersect with broader fire-resistive-construction and building-inspection protocols, ensuring that roof assemblies contribute to the overall fire rating and structural resilience of the building. Proper drainage, parapet wall integration, and edge protection are also mandated to maintain long-term performance and compliance with municipal safety codes.
