concepts/shear-reinforcement.title: shear-reinforcement
concepts/shear-reinforcement.content:
Shear Reinforcement
Shear reinforcement, commonly referred to as web reinforcement, is a critical component of reinforced-concrete-standards used to resist diagonal tension and shearing forces when the concrete web alone is insufficient. As detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204, when computed shearing unit stress exceeds the permitted limit for the concrete, web reinforcement must be provided to carry the excess (p. 190).
Types of Reinforcement
The code identifies three acceptable forms of web reinforcement (p. 190):

Vertical stirrups or web reinforcing bars.
Inclined stirrups or bars forming an angle of at least 30 degrees with the axis of the beam.
Longitudinal bars bent up at an angle of at least 15 degrees with the axis of the beam.

Design and Spacing Requirements
The area of steel required is calculated based on the excess shear stress using specific formulas for stirrups and bent-up bars. Spacing is strictly regulated to ensure structural integrity. Where the shearing stress does not exceed 0.06f'_c, the distance between successive stirrups must not exceed three-quarters of the effective depth (d). If the unit shearing stress exceeds this threshold, the maximum spacing is reduced to three-eighths of d (p. 190).
Anchorage
Effective web reinforcement requires secure anchorage at both ends to prevent pull-out. This can be achieved through continuity with main longitudinal reinforcement, bending around the longitudinal bar, or using hooks with a bend radius of at least four times the bar diameter. For high shearing unit stresses exceeding 0.08f'_c, only specific anchorage methods meeting rigorous standards are permitted (p. 193).
Application in Slabs and Footings
In flat-slabs, shear stress limits near column capitals depend on the distribution of negative reinforcement; higher allowable stress is permitted if at least 50% of negative reinforcement passes directly over the capital (p. 191). Similarly, column-design interacts with footing shear requirements, where critical sections for diagonal tension are defined relative to the column face (p. 191).

concepts/smokeproof-towers.title: smokeproof-towers
concepts/smokeproof-towers.content:
Smokeproof Towers
smokeproof-towers are specialized vertical evacuation shafts designed to prevent smoke infiltration and ensure safe occupant evacuation in multi-story structures. Mandated by the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, these towers serve as a critical component of emergency-egress systems, particularly for high-occupancy or tall buildings.
Construction and Fire Resistance
Smokeproof towers require robust fire-resistive-construction to isolate occupants from fire and smoke. The enclosure must consist of masonry walls with a minimum four-hour fire-resistive rating, complemented by floors and ceilings rated for at least two hours (p. 250). Throughout the tower, all structural components—including stairways, landings, platforms, and balconies—must be built entirely of incombustible materials, with the sole exception of handrails which may be wood (p. 250). The enclosure extends from street level to a penthouse on the roof, which must also be roofed with incombustible materials (p. 250).
Access and Egress Design
The defining feature of a smokeproof tower is its exterior access system, which prevents smoke from entering the stairwell during a fire. Access to the tower from each floor must be provided through vestibules open to the exterior or balconies that overhang an exterior wall (p. 250). These vestibules or balconies must have an unobstructed length at least equal to the combined width of the exit doors opening onto them, and they must open directly to a street, alley, yard, or an enclosed court measuring at least 15 feet wide and 600 square feet (p. 250).
Doorways providing access to the vestibules and the tower enclosure must be at least 30 inches wide and 75 inches in clear height (p. 250). These openings require self-closing fire doors with a one-hour fire-resistive rating that swing in the direction of exit travel (p. 250). Up to 720 square inches of clear wire glass is permitted in the enclosure access doors (p. 250-251). Any locks or latches installed must be of an approved pressure-release type to allow immediate egress (p. 250-251).
Location and Continuous Egress
Smokeproof towers must be strategically located to provide the most efficient evacuation route for building occupants (p. 251). Access to the tower must be routed through a public room, hall, or passageway with a minimum clear width of 36 inches, and it is strictly prohibited to route access through private rooms, apartments, or offices (p. 251). The stairways within the tower must provide continuous, uniform egress from the roof and all stories down to street grade (p. 251). Ground-level exit must lead directly outside or through a protected passageway (44 inches wide, 8 feet high) with four-hour fire-resistive walls and unpierced construction to a public way at least 10 feet wide (p. 251).
Applicability
The 1935-miami-dade-building-code requires smokeproof towers in all Group D, E, F, G, and H occupancies that are three or more stories in height (p. 250). Specific requirements for Group A, B, and C buildings are detailed in their respective chapters (p. 250).
For further details on stairway dimensions, enclosure requirements, and related egress standards, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p245_264.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p245_264

concepts/sprinkler-systems.title: sprinkler-systems
concepts/sprinkler-systems.content:
Sprinkler Systems
In the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, sprinkler-systems serve as a critical fire protection measure, often mandated for high-risk occupancies and used to leverage increased allowable building areas. The code references Chapter 38 for detailed installation specifications, placing mechanical operation and testing under the jurisdiction of the Plumbing Inspector (p. 269).
Mandatory Installation
Sprinkler requirements are stringent across various occupancy-classification groups. For Group A buildings, such as large assembly spaces, automatic sprinklers are strictly required (p. 50). Groups B, C, D, and E buildings must also install systems as specified in Chapter 38 (pp. 53, 58, 62, 66).
Chapter 38 expands these mandates to specific high-risk areas. Automatic sprinklers are required in the cellars or basements of all buildings in Fire Zone No. 1, unless separated by a 4-inch concrete slab with fire doors, and in all fire zones if the basement stores inflammable materials (pp. 269-270). They are also mandated in enclosed occupied spaces above or below stairways in Groups B and C (unless Type I construction) and in basements used for storage or maintenance (p. 270). Additionally, Groups D and H (Division 2) buildings two or more stories high require sprinklers (p. 270). Groups F and G also fall under the Chapter 38 mandates for automatic sprinkler, standpipe, and basement pipe inlet installations as conditions require (pp. 69, 72).
Installation Standards & Water Supply
Every automatic sprinkler system must comply with the 1931 regulations of the national-board-fire-underwriters, unless contradicted by the code (p. 270). A single water supply equal to the primary requirement is acceptable, and connections to city water mains must be at least four inches in diameter (p. 270).
Exceptions allow sprinklers protecting stairway enclosures to be supplied by the domestic water system, requiring only compliance with pipe sizing and head spacing. If domestic pressure falls below 25 psi, an approved automatic chemical extinguisher may substitute for the sprinkler (pp. 270-271).
Area Increase Incentives
The code incentivizes fire suppression by allowing developers to bypass strict floor area limitations. For Groups C, D, and E, the maximum allowable floor area can increase by 100% if the entire area is protected by an automatic sprinkler installation (pp. 55, 61, 64). This incentive is consistently applied across other occupancy groups as well: Groups F, G, and H all permit a 100% increase in maximum allowable floor area provided the building is entirely protected by an automatic sprinkler system per Chapter 38 specifications (pp. 68, 71, 74).
Specialized Applications
Certain high-hazard and theatrical structures face unique mandates. Theatrical spaces in Groups A, B, and C require sprinklers in motion picture booths, under gridirons and stage floors, in fly galleries, dressing rooms, workshops, and passageways behind the proscenium wall, with a line of heads in the proscenium arch (p. 270).
Industrial mandates cover planing mills, box factories, woodworking establishments with over two power machines, mattress factories using combustible stuffing, and film exchanges (p. 270). Aircraft hangars (Group E) are restricted to Type I and II construction but may receive a 100% area increase if equipped with sprinkling or deluge systems, aligning with standards from the national-fire-protection-association (p. 64).
Sprinkler systems complement fire-separation measures, such as draft stops in large rooms, to compartmentalize fire and smoke, thereby enhancing overall building safety and emergency-egress viability.

concepts/standpipe-systems.title: standpipe-systems
concepts/standpipe-systems.content:
Standpipe Systems
Standpipe systems are interior vertical piping networks designed to deliver water for firefighting operations within buildings. The 1935 code mandates their installation in specific occupancy types and building heights to ensure rapid fire suppression access.
Requirements and Installation
Interior standpipes are required in every Group A, B, and C building of any height, and in Group D through H buildings that are four or more stories tall (p. 271). During the construction of buildings five stories or higher, standpipes must be installed and kept ready for service up to one story below the active floor construction (p. 272).
Standpipes must be constructed of wrought iron or galvanized steel, capable of withstanding operational pressure without leaking, and must be fully drainable (p. 271). They are typically located within stairway enclosures or immediately adjacent to exit routes to ensure accessibility (p. 272).
Sizing and Spacing
Pipe sizing is determined by building height and fire department delivery requirements. Standpipes must be capable of delivering 250 gallons per minute from any three outlets simultaneously. Minimum diameters are four inches for buildings up to six stories (or 75 feet) and six inches for taller structures, aligning with national-board-fire-underwriters standards (p. 271). The number of standpipes scales with floor area, requiring one unit per 10,000 square feet or fraction thereof for floors above the second level (p. 272). Spacing must ensure that any part of the building is reachable with a 75-foot hose (p. 273).
Water Supply and Equipment
Standpipes must connect to a street water main of at least four inches in diameter. If municipal pressure is insufficient to maintain 30 psi at the highest outlet, the system must utilize pressure tanks, gravity tanks, or automatic fire pumps capable of delivering 250 GPM for at least ten minutes (pp. 272-273).
Fire department access is facilitated through Siamese connections located on the street front, one to four feet above grade. The number of inlets scales with height: two for up to five stories, three for six to ten stories, and four for taller buildings (p. 272). All outlets are equipped with gate valves, and a 2.5-inch outlet is provided at each story and on the roof (p. 272). Each outlet includes a 1.5-inch hose with a brass nozzle, mounted on a recessed wall rack or cabinet (p. 273).
Testing and Compliance
Installation and hydrostatic testing are conducted under the supervision of the Plumbing Inspector and Fire Department representatives. Owners or contractors are responsible for any damage occurring during testing and must drain the system afterward (p. 271). These systems often work in tandem with sprinkler-systems to provide comprehensive fire protection, particularly in high-hazard or large-scale occupancies.
See also: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p265_284
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p265_284

concepts/steel-joist-design.title: steel-joist-design
concepts/steel-joist-design.content:
Steel Joist Design
Steel joists are secondary structural members used to support floor and roof loads, transferring them to primary beams, girders, or trusses. According to the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, steel joists may be fabricated as rolled structural sections, built-up sections, spot-welded strip or sheet steel forming an I-beam contour, or determinate truss designs with fusion-welded members (p. 215).
Classification and Usage Limits
Steel joists are classified as secondary members that do not contribute to the vertical rigidity of the structural frame but are required to transmit horizontal forces to the primary lateral force-resisting system (p. 215). Non-solid-web joists are strictly prohibited in floor construction for buildings exceeding eight stories in height (p. 215).
Design Criteria and Deflection
Joists must be designed to safely carry all dead, live, and construction loads without exceeding the allowable-working-stress limits specified for structural steel (p. 215). The maximum permissible deflection under calculated load is limited to one three-hundred-sixtieth (1/360) of the span (p. 215). Strip or sheet steel joists must have a minimum thickness of .0072 inches, and the flange width cannot exceed half the joist’s depth (p. 216).
Bridging and Lateral Stability
Permanent and temporary bridging is mandatory to prevent lateral movement of the top chord or flange during construction and service. Bridging systems may consist of solid concrete sections, structural steel shapes, portal bridging, or diagonal rods (p. 216). Each row of bridging must be capable of transferring 500 pounds of lateral force from each joist to adjacent members (p. 216).
Connections and Fabrication
Connections to supporting beams, girders, or masonry/reinforced-concrete-standards elements must be secure. For Type I construction, connections may utilize fusion welding, riveting, bolting, or rigid connecting methods (p. 216). Welded connections require beads on both sides of the bearing, with a minimum length of 1 inch and a cross-section of 1/4 inch (p. 216). In buildings over eight stories, connections must comply with standard angles and power-driven rivets or unfinished bolts per the american-institute-of-steel-construction handbook (p. 216).
For trussed steel joists, all member forces must intersect at a single point to avoid eccentricity. Joints are made via rivets or fusion welds, with welds required to be symmetrical, at least twice the diameter of the smallest connected member in length, and possessing a cross-sectional area of at least one-fourth of the smallest member (p. 216-217). The code also grants the Building Inspector authority to mandate load testing of welded joist connections at twice the designed load to verify structural integrity (p. 217).
See also: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224, structural-steel-connections, structural-loads.
Related Documents

concepts/storm-shutter-requirements.title: storm-shutter-requirements
concepts/storm-shutter-requirements.content:
Overview
storm-shutter-requirements establish mandatory protections for building openings to safeguard occupants and structures against high winds and flying debris. As detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p245_264, these regulations reflect early recognition of tropical weather hazards and the need for resilient urban infrastructure in the city-of-miami region.
Regulatory Standards
The code mandates that all buildings, both existing and newly erected, must install approved storm shutters for openings facing public streets or other public places (p. 252). These shutters must be capable of withstanding a wind pressure of at least thirty (30) pounds per square foot, aligning with broader wind-pressure-design principles to ensure structural integrity during severe weather events (p. 252). The construction and anchorage methods must receive explicit approval from the building-inspection authority (p. 252). Additionally, code amendments specify that removable metal storm shutters and their fasteners must conform to the strength requirements established for steel, sheet metal, or aluminum siding (p. 338).
Operational and Storage Requirements
To ensure rapid deployment, the code requires suitable storage for shutters either on the premises or at an easily accessible location, allowing for transport to the site within a reasonable time after notification of an impending windstorm (p. 252). This operational readiness underscores the practical application of emergency preparedness in building management.
Exceptions and Scope
The regulations carve out specific exceptions to avoid undue burden on certain structures. Minor window openings, solid doors equipped with substantial bolting, and standard windows located in high, inaccessible positions are exempt from strict shutter mandates (p. 253). However, large windows, plate glass, and openings in business areas where the public gathers are explicitly prioritized for protection due to their critical role in public safety and commerce (p. 253). While residences classified under occupancy-classification Group I and minor buildings outside business centers are not strictly required to have shutters, their installation is strongly recommended as a preservation measure and safeguard against flying debris (p. 253).
Integration with Building Safety
These requirements complement broader fire-resistive-construction and structural resilience goals, ensuring that building envelopes remain intact during extreme weather. By standardizing shutter performance, material strength, and deployment logistics, the code establishes a foundational framework for hazard mitigation in coastal and tropical urban environments.

concepts/structural-fireproofing.title: structural-fireproofing
concepts/structural-fireproofing.content:
Structural Fireproofing Requirements
The 1935-miami-dade-building-code establishes strict mandates for protecting structural frameworks to ensure varying degrees of public safety and resistance to fire and windstorms (p. 108). The extent of required structural-fireproofing depends heavily on the designated construction-types and building-height-and-area-limits.
Type I: Fire-Resistive Construction
Buildings classified as fire-resistive-construction require the most rigorous protection. All structural steel or iron members must be thoroughly fireproofed (p. 110). The duration of the fire-resistive protection scales with building height:

Over 8 stories or 85 feet: Columns, beams, and girders require a 4-hour rating, while floors require a 3-hour rating (p. 110).
8 stories or 85 feet and under: Columns, beams, and girders require a 3-hour rating, while floors require a 2-hour rating (p. 110).
Reinforced concrete structural members follow identical fireproofing duration requirements (p. 111).

Type II: Heavy Timber Construction
In heavy timber construction, structural steel or iron members generally require 3-hour protection for columns and 2-hour protection for beams, girders, and floor systems (p. 116). However, this fireproofing may be entirely omitted if the building does not exceed one story or 65 feet in height (p. 116). Wood structural members are explicitly exempt from fireproofing requirements (p. 116). Reinforced concrete elements in these buildings require 3-hour protection for columns and 2-hour for joists, beams, girders, and slabs (p. 116-117).
Type III: Ordinary Masonry Construction
For ordinary masonry construction, fireproofing of steel, iron, or wood structural members is generally not required unless specifically mandated by fire-zones or occupancy-classification (p. 123). The primary exception applies to steel members (other than exterior lintels) that support masonry, which must have at least a 1-hour fire-resistive rating (p. 123).
Insulating Materials and Specifications
The code provides detailed tables for minimum protection thicknesses based on fire-resistive periods (1 to 4 hours) and the specific insulating materials used (p. 285). Common materials include concrete, gunite, brick, clay tile, gypsum blocks, and plaster. Concrete is strictly categorized into two grades:

Grade A Concrete: Contains coarse aggregates with no more than 65% siliceous material (e.g., limestone, pumice, slag, coral rock) (p. 286).
Grade B Concrete: Uses coarse aggregates outside the Grade A specifications, generally requiring thicker protective layers to achieve equivalent fire ratings (p. 286).

Plaster protections exceeding 1 inch in thickness must incorporate an additional layer of galvanized metal lath, wire, or mesh embedded no more than ¾ inch from the surface and securely tied to supporting members (p. 286). All concrete, gypsum, and gunite protections require embedded galvanized wire or expanded metal mesh, with specific weight and gauge standards mandated for ties and bases (p. 286-287). Gypsum plaster board (minimum 3/8 inch thick) may substitute for metal lath in 2-hour or less ratings, provided it is reinforced with galvanized mesh and contains no more than 15% combustible material (p. 287).
Floor, Ceiling, and Joist Protection
Floor and ceiling protections are integrated into the floor-and-roof-standards to ensure comprehensive structural integrity. Flat ceilings where protection is suspended to create a free-air space of at least 1 inch between the structural member and the protection may reduce the required thickness by ½ inch, though no thickness may fall below ¾ inch (p. 286, 291).
Steel joists require ceiling protection using galvanized metal/wire lath with plaster or gunite, with thickness varying by the required fire-resistive period (p. 292). Wood joisted construction is permitted for 1-hour ratings if it features a double wood floor (minimum 1¼ inches total thickness) and a securely fastened fire-resistive ceiling underneath (p. 291). All reinforcing steel, tie rods, and supporting members in concrete or masonry floor systems must be protected according to their respective fire-resistive classifications (p. 291).
Exceptions and Modifications
The code provides specific exceptions to balance safety with practical construction needs:

Fireproofing thickness on the outer edge of column lugs or brackets may be reduced to a minimum of 1 inch (p. 286).
Steel plates or angles supporting masonry over window openings do not require underside fireproofing if supported by a fully fireproofed beam or girder (p. 110).
Roof structural frameworks located 25 feet or more above any floor or balcony are exempt from fireproofing requirements (p. 110). If located between 18 and 25 feet above a floor, a 1-hour fire-resistive suspended ceiling must be installed at least 6 inches below the roof construction (p. 110).
For steel or iron members, protection thickness is measured outside the extreme edges of the structural shapes. For reinforced-concrete-standards, thickness is measured outside the reinforcement (p. 286).
Protection materials are not considered load-bearing for design purposes, except as permitted for tied columns (p. 286).

These requirements ensure that critical load-bearing elements maintain integrity during a fire, supporting the overall fire-separation and life safety goals of the municipality.

concepts/structural-loads.title: structural-loads
concepts/structural-loads.content:
Structural loads encompass the forces and weights that building components must safely support, categorized into dead loads and live loads. The 1935-miami-dade-building-code establishes strict engineering standards to ensure structural integrity across various building types and uses.
Dead and Live Loads
Dead load refers to the weight of permanent stationary construction, including walls and fixed structures (p. 140). Live load includes all other imposed forces, such as occupants, furniture, and equipment (p. 140). Buildings must be designed to support both without exceeding allowable material stresses, with special considerations for impact loads (p. 140).
Occupancy-Specific Live Loads
Minimum unit live loads are prescribed based on occupancy-classification:

Residential (Apartments, Dwellings, Hotels): 40 pounds per square foot (psf) for rooms, up to 100 psf for corridors and public areas (p. 141).
Commercial & Assembly (Dance Halls, Garages, Retail Stores): 75 to 100 psf, with heavy storage requiring up to 250 psf (p. 141-142).
Institutional & Educational (Hospitals, Schools, Libraries): 40 to 125 psf, depending on whether the space is a ward, corridor, or stack room (p. 141).
Industrial & Manufacturing: 75 to 150 psf, with actual machinery loads dictating design where they exceed standards (p. 142).

Load Reductions and Foundation Design
While slabs, joists, and beams must be designed for full live loads, reductions are permitted for girders (5–15%) and columns based on tributary floor area and story level (p. 142). Reductions scale with height, allowing up to 50% for columns in high-rise non-storage buildings, but are capped at 20% for warehouses (p. 143). Footing base areas are calculated by dividing total load by allowable soil pressure, ensuring combined dead, live, and wind loads never exceed the soil’s safe sustaining power (p. 143).
Roof and Wind Loads
Roof design depends on pitch: slopes of four inches or less per foot require a 30 psf vertical live load, while steeper roofs require loads calculated normal to the slope (p. 142). Wind pressure standards scale with building height, ranging from 25 psf for structures under 25 feet to 45 psf for those over 75 feet, with specific allowances for combined stress increases of 33⅓% (p. 143). For more on aerodynamic forces, see wind-pressure-design.
Posting and Permit Compliance
Owners must conspicuously post designed live loads and maximum seating capacities on durable metal signs (p. 144). building-permits and certificate-of-occupancy issuance requires verification that floor load signs are installed and that proposed occupancies align with structural capacities (p. 144).
Further details on load calculations and structural requirements are documented in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p125_144.

concepts/structural-steel-connections.title: structural-steel-connections
concepts/structural-steel-connections.content:
Structural Steel Connections
Structural steel connections ensure the transfer of forces between beams, columns, girders, and trusses while maintaining the overall stability of the framework. The 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code establishes rigorous requirements for riveted, bolted, and welded joints to ensure they safely transmit structural-loads without exceeding allowable-working-stress limits.
Riveted and Bolted Joints
Connections carrying calculated stresses must utilize a minimum of two rivets, or three for field connections (p. 211). To minimize eccentricity, the lines of gravity for members meeting at a joint should intersect at a single point; if not, the design must account for the resulting bending moments (p. 212). Loose fillers between connected pieces require an increased number of rivets, whereas tight-fitting fillers are preferred (p. 212).
Rivets are mandatory for main members subject to live loads, impact, or stress reversal (p. 213). Finished bolts in reamed holes may substitute for rivets in shop or field work when power-driven riveting is impractical, provided washers are used to ensure full grip (p. 213). Unfinished bolts are restricted to secondary members like purlins, girts, and bracing (p. 213).
Spacing and Pitch Requirements
The code specifies strict geometric limits for fastener placement:

Minimum Spacing: The distance between rivet centers must be at least three times the rivet diameter, with specific minimums listed in Table A (p. 212).
Edge Distance: The minimum distance from a rivet center to a plate edge is defined in Table B, while the maximum distance cannot exceed 12 times the plate thickness (capped at 6 inches) (p. 212).
Maximum Pitch: In compression members, rivet pitch along the stress line is limited to 16 times the thinnest outside plate or 20 times the thinnest enclosed plate, with a 12-inch maximum (p. 213).

Welded Connections
Fusion welding is permitted as an alternative to riveting or bolting, provided joint stresses remain within allowable limits: 13,000 psi for tension in butt welds, 11,300 psi for shear in fillet welds, and 15,000 psi for compression (p. 213). Electrode wire must conform to American Welding Society Specifications E No. 1-A or 1-B (p. 213-214).
Welders must demonstrate competency by producing sample butt welds with an average tensile strength of at least 45,000 psi, with no individual sample falling below 40,000 psi (p. 214). For electric spot or resistance welding, base metals must be thoroughly pickled in acid to remove rust and scale before welding (p. 214).
Workmanship and Fabrication
Holes for rivets may be punched 1/16 inch larger than the nominal rivet diameter if the metal thickness does not exceed the rivet diameter plus 1/8 inch; thicker materials require drilling or reaming (p. 217). Drifting to enlarge unfair holes is strictly prohibited (p. 217).
Rivets must be driven hot, with a maximum temperature of 1950°F (light yellow). Air hammers are prohibited once the temperature drops below 1000°F (blood red) (p. 217). Compression joints relying on contact bearing must be truly faced after riveting (p. 217). Gas cutting is permitted for preparing weld edges or correcting minor fabricating errors, but cannot replace milling of bearing surfaces or be performed on stressed members (p. 217-218).
Surfaces of riveted work contacting each other must receive two coats of paint before assembly to prevent corrosion (p. 218). These connection standards integrate with broader requirements for steel-joist-design and overall framework stability (p. 214).
For the complete regulatory text, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224

concepts/temporary-canvas-structures.title: temporary-canvas-structures
concepts/temporary-canvas-structures.content:
Temporary Canvas Structures
Temporary canvas structures encompass a variety of movable and detachable shelters designed to provide protection from sun and weather. In the city-of-miami municipal code, these structures are strictly regulated to ensure public safety, structural integrity, and proper use of public spaces 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324.
Classification and Definitions
The code distinguishes between several types of canvas and cloth protections based on their construction and support mechanisms (p. 315):

Awnings: Temporary, movable structures on folding metal frames supported entirely by building walls, operated via ropes, pulleys, or rollers.
Awning Shutters: Movable, rigid structures (wood or metal) that function as sun protection or storm shutters.
Roller Curtains: Detachable canvas protections supported by a roller attached to the lower edge, raised and lowered mechanically.
Stationary Awnings: Detachable canvas on rigid metal frames fixed to building walls.
Canopies: Temporary detachable structures on rigid metal frames supported by posts attached to the ground or building.
Tents: Canvas shelters supported by poles, stakes, and ropes, completely detached from any building.

Permitting and Usage Restrictions
Installation, removal, repair, or alteration of these structures over public property requires a building permit from the Building Inspector (p. 315). Tents may only be pitched within fire zones for a specified period under strict division rules (p. 315).
Usage is heavily restricted: no business transactions or merchandise storage/display are permitted under these structures without a special permit (p. 316). While small awnings and canopies may shade a single automobile or seating area on private property, they are prohibited in areas storing inflammables and cannot shelter motor vehicles within the First and Second Fire Zones (p. 316). Temporary structures over public sidewalks must be quickly collapsible and removed immediately when the associated premises are vacated (p. 318).
Construction and Safety Standards
Structural integrity is paramount, with all frames required to withstand standard wind pressure without relying on the canvas for support (p. 316). Metal frames are mandatory within Fire Zones 1 and 2, and pipe/frame dimensions are strictly graded by span width (p. 316). Fastenings must use approved lag screws or anchor bolts; wooden or fiber plugs are prohibited (p. 317).
For structures projecting over sidewalks, metal components must maintain a minimum clearance of 7’6”, while canvas must stay at least 6’6” above the walkway (p. 318). Projections are limited to nine feet from the building wall. Slide rods are restricted unless automated, and all overhangs must be equipped with supporting chains or fireproof cables to prevent accidental collapse if the canvas fails (p. 318). Awning-shutters require permanent construction with hinges secured by bolts or rivets, never screws or nails (p. 317).
These regulations ensure that temporary canvas structures enhance urban environments without compromising pedestrian safety or building security.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324

concepts/theatrical-stage-safety.title: theatrical-stage-safety
concepts/theatrical-stage-safety.content:
Overview
Theatrical stage safety encompasses structural requirements, fire separation, ventilation, and specialized equipment for assembly occupancies to prevent fire spread and ensure occupant safety. Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, stages are heavily regulated due to the high combustibility of curtains, scenery, and lighting equipment.
Stage Construction and Fire Separation
Stages must be completely isolated from the auditorium using a proscenium wall constructed of solid masonry with a four-hour fire-resistive-construction rating, or metal studs with cement plaster for Group C occupancies (p. 275). Openings in the proscenium wall are strictly limited and must be protected by one-hour fire doors (p. 276). Stage floors require Type I construction or steel/heavy timber capable of supporting 125 pounds per square foot (p. 276). Gridirons, fly galleries, and pin-rails must be built from incombustible materials and designed for a 75 psf load (p. 275). Dressing rooms, workshops, and storerooms must be located on the stage side of the proscenium and separated by fire-separation (p. 275).
Ventilation and Fire Control
ventilation-requirements for stages mandate metal ventilators above the highest part of the stage, covering at least 5% of the floor area. These ventilators must open automatically via fusible links at 160°F or manually via stage-side chains (p. 274). Air circulation blowers serving the auditorium must include automatic cut-off devices to stop airflow during a fire (p. 276). Proscenium curtains are critical fire barriers, constructed from metal-reinforced asbestos cloth and mineral paint. They must be capable of lowering automatically via counterweights and fusible links within 30 seconds, intercepting flames and smoke within five minutes (pp. 279-280).
Motion Picture Booths and Film Storage
Projection booths housing inflammable nitrocellulose films require a masonry floor, one-hour fire-resistive walls, and self-closing fire doors (p. 277). Viewing ports are restricted in size and number, and must be covered with sliding metal shutters held open by 160°F fusible links (p. 278). Booths must be ventilated to change air every ten minutes, with vents routed directly to the roof or through masonry flues (p. 278). All films not in use must be stored in metal cabinets with tight self-closing doors, adhering to national-board-fire-underwriters guidelines (p. 279).
Egress and Safety Equipment
Stage exits must be at least 2 feet 6 inches wide, providing direct access to streets or exit courts (p. 276). Fly galleries require dedicated exit stairs, and each tier of dressing rooms needs two means of egress (p. 276). The stage switchboard must be protected by a hood, and sprinkler-systems are mandated under the stage floor, gridiron, and in all backstage areas (p. 269). For further details on these regulations, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p265_284.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p265_284

concepts/ventilation-requirements.title: ventilation-requirements
concepts/ventilation-requirements.content:
Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, ventilation requirements ensure adequate air quality, structural preservation, and safety across various occupancy-classifications and construction-types. The code mandates specific standards for natural airflow, mechanical air exchange, and structural ventilation depending on the building’s use and framing.
Natural and Mechanical Ventilation for Occupancies
As detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84, the code mandates specific standards for natural airflow through windows and mechanical air exchange systems. For general occupancies like Group E and Group F, spaces customarily used by humans must have windows or skylights covering one-eighth of the total floor area, or utilize a mechanical ventilation system (p. 65, p. 69). Group G buildings, covering non-hazardous industrial occupancies, also require light and ventilation for human use areas (p. 72). Group H buildings, such as hotels and apartments, require even larger windows covering fifteen percent of the floor area for living and sleeping rooms (p. 75). Group I living spaces follow the one-eighth floor area window requirement (p. 77).
When natural ventilation is insufficient, mechanical systems must meet strict air exchange rates. Group E requires thirty cubic feet of pure air per minute per occupant, running continuously while occupied (p. 65). Group F mandates at least four air changes per hour (p. 69). In Group I, bathroom and toilet rooms must be ventilated by a wall or ceiling vent of at least two square feet net area if a window is not provided (p. 77).
Foundation and Subfloor Ventilation
To prevent moisture accumulation and decay in wood-frame and other structures, the code requires ventilated openings in solid foundation walls under the first floor joists. For Type IV and Type V buildings, these openings are mandated unless the space is occupied by a basement or cellar (p. 126, p. 130). Similarly, 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p165_184 extends this requirement to all Type II buildings, mandating ventilated openings for the same purpose (p. 165). Across these construction types, openings must be covered with non-corrosive wire mesh of at least sixteen mesh per linear inch. The required ventilation area is proportioned at not less than two square feet for every fifteen linear feet of exterior foundation wall, distributed across at least three sides to ensure adequate cross-ventilation in at least one direction (p. 126, p. 131, p. 165–166).
Roof and Attic Ventilation
For flat deck type roof construction in wood-frame buildings, an air space of not less than eighteen inches is required between the top of the ceiling joists and the bottom of the roof rafter to ensure proper ventilation (p. 138). This requirement helps regulate temperature and moisture levels within the roof assembly.
Hazardous Materials and Automobile Exhaust
Special exhaust ventilation is required for hazardous environments to prevent the buildup of fumes. Areas handling automobiles or inflammable liquids need one complete air change every fifteen minutes, drawn from near the floor level (p. 65). Dry cleaning plants require an even faster rate of one complete air change every three minutes via a power-driven fan system (p. 66).
Private garages attached to Group H or I buildings must have exhaust openings or fixed louvered or screened openings located within six feet of the floor. The clear area of these openings must be at least sixty square inches per car stored in the garage. Under no circumstances shall a private garage have any opening into a living or sleeping room (p. 80). Amusement park structures with enclosed public spaces must also maintain sufficient natural or artificial ventilation for safe and healthful conditions (p. 80).

concepts/vertical-transportation-safety.title: vertical-transportation-safety
concepts/vertical-transportation-safety.content:
Vertical Transportation Safety encompasses the regulations governing the design, installation, operation, and maintenance of elevators, escalators, and dumbwaiters within the city-of-miami municipal code 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324. These rules prioritize passenger and freight security, fire resilience, and emergency access. Recent amendments via Ordinance No. 2149 (1939) further clarify inspection reporting and fee structures 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p365_375.
Regulatory Standards
All vertical transportation systems must adhere to the “Safety Code for Elevators” recommended by the american-standards-association (1925 Edition) and its amendments (p. 311). Electrical installations require compliance with the 1935 “National Electrical Code” and approval from the city’s Electrical Inspector (p. 311). The code defines elevators as devices for passengers or freight, while dumbwaiters are restricted to a maximum horizontal section of six square feet and four feet in height for small packages (p. 311).
Installation and Operational Controls
Before installation or alteration, owners must submit detailed plans to the Building Inspector for approval (pp. 311-312). Operational safety mandates that non-automatic elevators be operated by competent personnel aged 18 or older with verified training and a certificate of competency (p. 312). Carrying capacity must be clearly posted, with a minimum requirement of 100 pounds per square foot for passenger elevators (p. 312). Door controls feature mechanical interlocks that prevent car movement when shaft doors are open and restrict door access when the car is not present (p. 312). Combination passenger and freight elevators are strictly prohibited (p. 313). Additionally, the code requires that no passenger elevator, freight elevator, dumbwaiter, escalator, or building hoist may be lawfully operated without a valid certificate of operation conspicuously posted in the car or platform (p. 366).
Structural and Mechanical Requirements
Elevator cars and machinery enclosures must utilize incombustible materials, though hardwood flooring is permitted (p. 313). Guide rails must be steel and rigidly bolted, with hardwood alternatives allowed only for slow-moving systems under 100 feet per minute (p. 313). Counterweights require bolted sections with a minimum three-foot clearance from overhead beams, and continuous forged straps are banned (p. 312). Substantial buffer springs are mandatory at the top and bottom of shafts, and supporting beams must be engineered to withstand double the anticipated live loads (pp. 313, 314). Shaft clearances are strictly regulated based on elevator speed, ensuring safe operation during travel (p. 313).
Emergency and Fire Safety
The code integrates vertical transportation with broader fire safety protocols. Banks of elevators require at least one-hour fire-resistive-construction between them (p. 311). Every passenger elevator must include an emergency-egress mechanism, such as a roof trap door or side exit for dual-car shafts (p. 314). In high-rise structures exceeding 100 feet, at least one passenger elevator must remain operational and ready for fire department use around the clock (p. 314). Speed governors and safety devices are legally required for elevators exceeding their rated speed by 40% or reaching 850 feet per minute (p. 314).
Inspections, Certification, and Enforcement
Ongoing safety is maintained through quarterly building-inspection protocols conducted by the Building Inspector (p. 314). Inspectors hold the authority to halt operations immediately if hazards are identified and mandate prompt repairs (p. 314). Under Ordinance No. 2149, every elevator inspector, whether employed by the city-of-miami or by an insurance or casualty company, must file a full report of each inspection with the Building Inspector (p. 365-366). If the report indicates safe operating conditions, the Building Inspector issues a certificate of operation valid for three months for passenger elevators, freight elevators, escalators, and building hoists, and six months for dumbwaiters and hand-power freight elevators (p. 366). The inspection fee is set at $2.50 and the certificate at 50 cents, though the inspection fee is waived after July 1, 1939, for approved private inspectors from insurance or casualty companies (p. 366). All collected fees are directed to the city-of-miami Tax Collector rather than a special elevator fund (p. 367). These comprehensive measures ensure that vertical transportation systems meet rigorous standards for public safety and structural integrity.

concepts/wall-veneer-and-facings.title: wall-veneer-and-facings
concepts/wall-veneer-and-facings.content:
Wall Veneer and Facings
Wall veneer and facings refer to non-structural or semi-structural exterior coverings applied to masonry or reinforced concrete backing walls to provide weather protection, aesthetic finish, or additional bearing capacity depending on bonding methods. The 1935 code distinguishes strictly between veneered walls, which do not contribute to structural strength, and faced walls, which may be integrated into the load-bearing system.
Material Specifications and Minimum Thickness
Veneering materials must meet specific quality standards and minimum thickness requirements to ensure durability and weather resistance (p. 231). Approved materials and their minimum thicknesses include:

Stone and cellular architectural terra cotta: not less than 3 inches
Slab terra cotta: not less than 1¼ inches
Brick (clay, concrete, or sand-lime) and hollow tile: not less than 2 inches
Cast stone and other approved masonry: not less than 2 to 3 inches

Glass or vitreous materials require special approval from the Building Inspector regarding thickness and anchorage (p. 231).
Attachment and Bonding Requirements
Veneers must be securely tied to the backing wall to resist wind pressure and prevent detachment. The code mandates either a masonry header for every 300 square inches of surface or non-corrosive metal wall ties spaced no more than 1 foot vertically and 2 feet horizontally (p. 231). Headers must project at least 3¾ inches into the backing. Thin tile veneers (≤1 inch thick, ≤200 sq. in. area) with corrugated backs may be cemented solidly to the backing instead of mechanically tied (p. 231-232).
Glass Veneer Construction
Glass veneer installations require rigorous structural backing and specialized setting techniques. The backing must be a rigid, incombustible, plumb surface, typically cement mortar on wire lath over studs (p. 232). Minimum glass thickness is 11/32”, with panel sizes limited to 10 sq. ft. below 15 feet elevation and 6 sq. ft. above (p. 232). Installation involves a bond coat and approved mastic cement applied to a bulk thickness of ¼” to ⅝”, ensuring at least 50% of the panel area is bonded. Metal shelf angles (18 U.S. Gauge) and mechanical fastenings are required at specific intervals, particularly above second-story sills, to support the veneer independently of the mastic (p. 232-233). Expansion joints of at least ¼” are mandatory at terminations to accommodate thermal movement (p. 233).
Faced Walls vs. Veneered Walls
Unlike veneered walls, faced walls integrate the outer layer into the structural system. When properly bonded, the facing may be included in calculations for allowable-working-stress and wall thickness (p. 234). Bonding requirements for faced walls are stringent: solid masonry or terra cotta facings require one header course per six courses, while stone ashlar facings must include bond units comprising at least 15% of the superficial area, extending 3¾ inches deeper than the rest of the facing (p. 234). These requirements ensure composite action between the facing and the backing, aligning with broader masonry-standards.
Height and Structural Limits
Veneer applied to masonry or reinforced concrete walls is generally limited to 40 feet above foundations unless specific anchorage and grouting methods are used for slab terra cotta or flat tile (p. 233). The structural backing must independently support all loads, as veneering is never considered part of the bearing wall’s strength or required thickness (p. 231). These provisions work in conjunction with wind-pressure-design and reinforced-concrete-standards to ensure exterior cladding remains secure under environmental loads.
For detailed regulatory text and tables governing these installations, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p225_244.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p225_244

concepts/wind-pressure-design.title: wind-pressure-design
concepts/wind-pressure-design.content:
Wind pressure design establishes the minimum lateral load requirements for buildings and structures to ensure stability against wind forces. According to the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, normal wind pressure is applied to all vertical plane surfaces and scales with building height (p. 143). Structures up to 25 feet require 25 pounds per square foot (psf), those up to 75 feet require 35 psf, and buildings over 75 feet must withstand 45 psf (p. 143).
Several exceptions modify these baseline requirements. Buildings whose height does not exceed 1.5 times their least width are exempt from tabulated wind loads, unless the width represents a single structural span or the interior framing does not provide lateral rigidity (p. 143-144). Exposed structures like sprinkler tanks and sky signs must be designed for a minimum of 50 psf on their gross area, while circular tanks and towers calculate pressure on 60% of their projected area (p. 144). Additionally, the overturning moment caused by wind must never exceed 50% of the dead load resisting moment (p. 144).
When calculating combined stresses from wind and other structural-loads, allowable unit stresses may increase by 33 1/3%. However, this increase is prohibited if the wind stress constitutes 50% or more of the total stress in a member, and the structural section must never be smaller than required if wind loads were ignored (p. 144). These engineering regulations ensure that lateral forces are properly integrated into the overall construction-types and foundation design.
For further details on load calculations and structural engineering standards, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p125_144.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p125_144

concepts/wood-framing-standards.title: wood-framing-standards
concepts/wood-framing-standards.content:
Wood framing standards dictate the quality, sizing, stress limits, and construction details for structural timber used in buildings. According to the 1935 code, all wood (except finish and millwork) must conform to American Lumber Standards established by the united-states-department-of-commerce (p. 153). Lumber is classified into Structural, Yard, and Other grades, with Structural lumber requiring permanent grade-marks and association certificates (p. 154). Allowable unit working stresses are determined by grade and species, heavily referencing data and formulas from the united-states-forest-products-laboratory (p. 157).
Lumber Quality and Preservation
Lumber permitted in contact with masonry or concrete must possess physical properties equal to 85% Heart Long Leaf Southern Yellow Pine, No. 1 Common Tidewater Red Cypress, or equivalent heartwood grades like Redwood; alternatively, it must be thoroughly treated with an approved preservative (p. 165). Brush treatment is prohibited except for end cuts on timber that has been otherwise properly treated (p. 165). All wood furring and foundation screen vents contacting masonry must be treated on all four sides (p. 165).
Foundations, Sills, and Clearance
Key structural requirements for the building base include:

Clearance: Floor joists must maintain an 18-inch clearance from the ground, which must be leveled to prevent uneven surfaces (p. 165).
Sills: Wood sills and plates contacting masonry or concrete must be isolated using 85-pound slate roofing, 24-gauge galvanized iron, or approved equivalents (p. 165).
Columns & Posts: Wood posts cannot extend through or sit directly on concrete floors; they must rest on concrete floatings extending at least 2 inches above the floor or on corrosion-resistant metal plates (p. 165). Posts must be squared, anchored with metal base plates, and supported on footings projecting at least 1 inch above finished floors (p. 160). Wood preservatives are required for damp locations, and posts cannot serve as foundations below ground (p. 160).

Horizontal Members
Beams and girders bearing on masonry require a minimum 4-inch bearing protected by asphalt, felt, or a metal plate, plus preservative treatment. Joists and rafters must also bear at least 4 inches and be anchored at masonry walls (p. 160-161). The minimum permissible thickness for joists, rafters, and studs is 1⅝ inches (p. 161).
Stud Walls & Partitions
Bearing partitions require doubled top plates lapped at intersections. Studs must be at least 2x4 inches (nominal), increasing to 2x6 or 3x4 where supporting heavy loads (p. 162). Openings require double headers and studs.
Roof & Trusses
Valley rafters must be at least 1⅝”x5½”, with roof sheathing minimum 25/32” thick. Flat-deck roofs require an 18-inch ventilation air space above ceiling joists (p. 163).
Ventilation
Solid foundation walls under the first floor of Type II buildings must include ventilated openings covered with 16-mesh non-corrosive wire. These openings must provide at least 2 square feet of area per 15 lineal feet of exterior wall, distributed across at least three sides to ensure cross-ventilation (p. 165-166), supporting broader ventilation-requirements.
Fire Safety and Pest Control
Fire safety and pest control are integrated into framing rules. fire-separation is enforced through mandatory fire stops at all wall/floor/ceiling intersections, limiting concealed air spaces to 7 feet in any dimension (p. 163-164). Termite prevention provisions mandate removing stumps to 12 inches below grade, placing wood on masonry or concrete foundations, and keeping foundation tops at least 6 inches above final grade (p. 163-164). Additionally, loose wood must not be stored in direct contact with the ground under any building (p. 166).
These standards ensure structural integrity by aligning material grading with allowable-working-stress calculations and enforcing rigorous detailing for load transfer, fire blocking, and environmental protection.

entities/1935-miami-dade-building-code.title: 1935-miami-dade-building-code
entities/1935-miami-dade-building-code.content:
1935 Miami-Dade Building Code
The 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code (formally Ordinance No. 1554) is a comprehensive municipal regulation adopted by the city-of-miami to establish minimum safety, design, and construction standards for all buildings and structures within the city limits (p. 17). Passed and adopted on August 4, 1936, the code governs the erection, alteration, repair, occupancy, and maintenance of buildings, as well as related structures like signs, awnings, and elevators (p. 17).
Scope and Administration
The code is organized into fifteen parts covering administrative procedures, definitions, occupancy classifications, fire zone restrictions, construction types, engineering regulations, and detailed building requirements (pp. 6–16). It primarily applies to new construction but includes specific thresholds for existing buildings, such as mandating full compliance or demolition if major repairs exceed 50% of a building’s value within a twelve-month period (p. 18). All structures must be maintained in a safe condition, with existing safety devices requiring inspector approval for removal (p. 19).
The code classifies buildings into Groups A through J based on their occupancy-classification and hazard level (p. 41). It also provides extensive definitions for key terms, including “Grade” (elevation relative to streets), “Story,” “Fire Wall” (continuous from foundation to roof), and “Fire Division Wall” (sub-divides building but not necessarily continuous) (p. 36, 40).
Permitting and Compliance
To ensure adherence to safety standards, the code requires building-permits for any construction or alteration work exceeding 25 in cost (p. 20). Projects with an estimated cost of 5,000 or more must have plans prepared or approved by a registered architect or structural engineer (p. 21). The compliance process involves verifying zoning districts, identifying applicable fire-zones, determining occupancy classification, and securing multi-departmental approvals for plumbing, electrical, and health requirements (p. 5, 20).
Fees are calculated based on a detailed table correlating occupancy groups with construction types (I-V) and cost per cubic foot (p. 25). Work commenced without a permit incurs double fees (p. 25). The building-inspection process requires permits to be posted and mandates sequential inspections for foundations, framing, stucco, and final completion (p. 26). For Type I buildings or complex designs, a “Registered Inspector” must be employed, bonded at $5,000, and present during construction until a Certificate of Compliance is issued (p. 27). A certificate-of-occupancy is required before any building can be used (p. 29).
Construction Types and Foundation Requirements
The code categorizes buildings into five construction-types, with specific regulations for Type IV (Metal Frame) and Type V (Wood Frame) structures. Type IV buildings feature steel, iron, masonry, or reinforced concrete frameworks with metal or incombustible exterior walls and roofs (p. 125). Their height is limited to forty-five feet, extending to fifty-five feet on sloping ground, with allowable areas dictated by occupancy and location (p. 126). Fireproofing of structural members is not required, and wood is permitted only where specifically allowed (p. 127).
Type V buildings utilize wood or wood-combination construction for walls, partitions, floors, and roofs, except where prohibited by fire zones or occupancy rules (p. 127). Maximum height is thirty-eight feet, or forty-five feet on slopes, with foundations required to rest on continuous masonry or reinforced concrete piers (p. 128). Exterior walls must use 2x4 studs spaced sixteen inches on centers, with thicker studs required for three-story buildings (p. 130). Detailed specifications govern exterior coverings, including weatherboarding, shingles, stucco over metal lath, and masonry veneer (pp. 132–135). Floor and roof joist spans are tabulated based on wood grade and spacing, with mandatory fire stopping every seven feet to prevent concealed air spaces (pp. 131, 135–138). Attached private garages require one-hour fire-resistive-construction for walls and ceilings, gravity-closing doors, and dedicated ventilation-requirements (pp. 139–140).
Foundations must comply with foundation-construction-requirements, mandating an 18-inch clearance between floor joists and the ground (p. 165). Wood sills and plates contacting masonry must be isolated with slate roofing or galvanized iron, while furring and vents require preservative treatment on all sides (p. 165). Approved lumber must meet specific heartwood or pressure-treated standards, explicitly excluding brush treatment except for end cuts (p. 165). Solid foundation walls require ventilated openings covered with 16-mesh non-corrosive wire, proportioned at two square feet per 15 lineal feet and distributed across at least three sides to ensure cross-ventilation (p. 165–166).
Reinforced Concrete and Engineering Standards
Chapter 26 establishes comprehensive reinforced-concrete-standards, requiring detailed drawings, reinforcement layouts, and load calculations prior to permit issuance (p. 166). Special concrete systems not covered by standard rules may be evaluated by the board-of-examiners-for-special-construction (p. 166). All materials and testing must comply with american-society-for-testing-materials standards, with the Building Inspector overseeing aggregate quality, water-cement ratios, and 28-day compressive strength verification (p. 170–173). Field testing mandates both moist-cured control cylinders and on-site job cylinders to verify mix proportions (p. 174).
The code defines allowable-working-stress limits for flexure, shear, bond, and axial compression based on concrete strength (f'_c) and reinforcement type, with spiral columns permitting higher stresses based on reinforcement ratios (p. 176–177). Concrete operations require dry excavations, continuous mixing for at least one minute, thorough compaction around reinforcement, and a minimum of seven days of moist curing, with strict cold-weather temperature controls between 50°F and 120°F (p. 178–179). Forms must be tightly sealed and braced, removable only after concrete achieves sufficient strength (p. 179–180). Reinforcement must be cleaned, bent cold without heating, and spaced with minimum concrete cover ranging from 3 inches for ground-contact footings to 1 inch for structural-fireproofing applications (p. 180–181). Construction joints must be strategically placed near mid-spans, roughened, and cleaned before resuming pours (p. 181).
Structural design relies on elastic flexure theory, assuming steel carries all tensile stress, utilizing a modular ratio of n = 30,000/f'_c, and permitting a 150% increase in allowable stresses for wind-pressure-design calculations (p. 182, 184). The code specifies detailed design rules for beams, slabs, and T-beams, defining effective depth, points of inflection, and effective flange widths (p. 185). Ribbed floor systems mandate specific rib spacing and minimum slab thicknesses (p. 186). It provides explicit moment-coefficients for uniformly loaded continuous beams and slabs, differentiating between freely supported and fully restrained systems (pp. 186–188). For two-way reinforced floors, load distribution depends on panel aspect ratios, with minimum shrinkage reinforcement ratios specified by bar type (p. 189).
Shear and diagonal tension calculations dictate when shear-reinforcement such as vertical stirrups or bent-up bars must be provided, with spacing limits tied to effective depth and stress levels (pp. 189–190). Bond stress computations govern anchorage lengths, requiring sufficient embedment beyond support faces and points of inflection (pp. 191–192). Special anchorage rules apply for high shear stresses, mandating personal supervision by the designing engineer (p. 193).
flat-slabs are defined as beamless slabs reinforced in two or four directions, with standard design provisions limited to panels with length-to-width ratios not exceeding 1.33 (pp. 193–194). Moment distribution is divided between column strips and middle strips, with specific coefficients provided for slabs with or without dropped panels (p. 195). Minimum slab thickness is calculated using a formula incorporating span length, column capital size, and load intensity (p. 196). Detailed reinforcement layout rules dictate bar extensions beyond points of inflection and column heads for both two-way and four-way systems (pp. 197–198). Additional provisions address wall panels, marginal beams, and permissible openings within middle and column strips (pp. 198–199).
column-design limits unsupported lengths and lateral dimensions, with principal columns requiring a minimum 12-inch diameter or thickness (p. 200). Spiral columns permit higher axial loads based on core area and reinforcement ratio, while tied columns use gross area calculations with stricter longitudinal steel limits (pp. 200–201). Composite steel-concrete columns and long columns (slenderness exceeding 40R or 50R) include explicit reduction factors for buckling (pp. 202–204). Footings are proportioned for total column load plus self-weight, with critical bending sections located at column faces or base centers, and sloped or stepped designs permitted if shear requirements are met (p. 204).
The code also establishes comprehensive standards for structural-loads, defining dead loads as permanent structural weight and live loads as all other imposed forces (p. 140). Minimum unit live loads vary by occupancy, with reductions permitted for girders and columns based on tributary area (pp. 141–143). Owners must post designed live loads and seating capacities, and occupancy permits require verification of floor suitability (p. 144).
Structural and Operational Regulations
Beyond initial construction, the code regulates ongoing building operations and structural modifications. It establishes strict protocols for moving buildings within or into city limits, requiring performance bonds, cash bonds for street damage, continuous city inspector accompaniment during transit, and mandatory warning lights (pp. 21–22).
Enforcement powers are vested in the Building Inspector, who holds police authority to halt unsafe work, condemn dangerous structures, and mandate repairs or demolition, with costs liened against the property (p. 29). The code includes specific provisions for termite extermination (p. 31). Disputes regarding alternate materials or code interpretations may be appealed to the board-of-examiners-and-appeals, composed of an architect, builder, and structural engineer (p. 32).
For mixed-occupancy buildings, the code mandates fire-separation to prevent fire spread. Separations are classified as Absolute (4-hour resistance, no openings), Special (3-hour resistance, restricted openings), or Ordinary (1-hour resistance, self-closing doors) (p. 42).
Detailed Occupancy Standards
The code specifies rigorous requirements for assembly, institutional, and hazardous occupancies (Groups A–E), emphasizing emergency-egress, ventilation, and fire protection:

Group A (Large Assembly): Covers theaters and auditoriums with 1,000+ or 3,500+ seats. Mandates Type 1 construction with no height or area limits. Requires exits on three sides of the auditorium, a main foyer sized at one square foot per seat, aisles at least three feet wide, and fixed seating. Buildings with multiple balconies must include smokeproof towers. Automatic sprinkler-systems and mechanical ventilation supplying 30 cubic feet of air per minute per occupant are mandatory (pp. 45–50).
Group B (Medium Assembly): Applies to venues with 300–3,500 seats. Allows Types I–III construction with varying height limits. Egress rules mirror Group A but waive the foyer requirement and prohibit balconies except in Type I buildings (pp. 51–54).
Group C (Small Assembly): Covers spaces with fewer than 750 seats. Permits Types I–V construction with floor areas strictly tied to street frontage. Classrooms and small rooms have adapted exit rules, and schools face restrictions on placing younger grades on upper floors. Rooms over 30,000 square feet require draft stops to subdivide attic spaces (pp. 54–60).
Group D (Institutional): Regulates detention facilities, hospitals, and care centers. Detention buildings require Type I construction, while multi-story care facilities need one-hour fire-resistive floors and partitions. Mandates 1:6 slope ramps for bedridden patients and self-releasing panic devices on exit doors (pp. 60–63).
Group E (Hazardous Industrial): Governs high-risk facilities like paint storage, mills, and aircraft hangars. Imposes strict area and height caps based on construction type. Aircraft hangars are limited to 30,000 sq ft (Type I) or 15,000 sq ft (Type II), with a 100% area increase permitted if fully sprinklered (pp. 63–64).

Across these groups, the code enforces fire-separation for boiler and heating rooms, prohibits the storage of inflammable liquids outside controlled laboratory settings, and requires independent meter rooms for multi-occupancy structures. Historical sanitation provisions also mandate segregated toilet facilities based on race and gender (pp. 50, 53, 58, 62).
Means of Egress, Doors, and Windows
The code establishes strict dimensional and safety standards for stairways to ensure reliable occupant evacuation. Stairways must be at least 44 inches wide, with a maximum rise of 7½ inches and a minimum tread depth of 10 inches (p. 247). Landings at the top and bottom of every flight must be level, at least 44 inches long, and match the width of the connected stair flight (p. 247). Interior stairways require 2-hour fire-resistive-construction enclosures, while passageways serving exits must maintain a minimum clear height of 7 feet and be protected by 1-hour fire doors (p. 245). For buildings three or more stories in height, the code requires smokeproof-towers featuring 4-hour fire-resistive walls, exterior access vestibules, and continuous uniform egress to street grade (pp. 250–251). Outside stairways are permitted for buildings up to three stories or 55 feet high but may not constitute more than 50% of the required exit capacity (p. 251).
Exit enclosure doors must be self-closing, normally closed, and constructed to 1-hour fire-resistive standards, swinging in the direction of travel without obstructing stair width (pp. 245–246). First-floor plate glass windows facing public streets must be at least ¼ inch thick, limited to 91 square feet without division bars, and secured with non-corrosive metal settings (p. 252). The code mandates storm-shutter-requirements capable of withstanding 30 pounds per square foot of wind pressure for public-facing openings to protect against flying debris (p. 252). Skylights angled less than 45 degrees above the first story must be set at least one foot above the roof, and wire glass panels are restricted to 720 square inches or 48 inches in any single dimension (p. 253).
Chimneys, Heating, and Roof Structures
Exterior balconies and bays require incombustible supports, railings at least 3 feet 6 inches high, and structural design to carry a 100 psf live load (p. 254). Penthouses and roof structures housing mechanical equipment, water tanks, or stair enclosures are excluded from building height calculations but are strictly limited to 20% of the total roof area and a maximum projection of 12 to 28 feet above the roof (p. 255). Walls of roof structures within 4 feet of exterior walls must match the fire-resistive rating of the story below and project 2 feet above the roofline (p. 255).
Chimney construction requires ⅝-inch thick fire clay linings with a softening point of at least 1994°F, with all joints thoroughly slushed and grouted (p. 256). Steel smokestacks must include 25 feet of fire brick lining from the intake and external masonry or ventilated metal shielding up through the roof (p. 260). Gas vents must utilize unglazed fire clay or concrete tile pipe with sleeves at every joint, maintaining a minimum 1-inch rise per foot and strict clearances from combustibles (p. 261). Fireplaces require 8-inch thick walls, fire brick facing, and properly supported hearths extending at least 20 inches from the chimney breast (p. 263). Warm air furnaces must be double metal-shielded, installed on masonry floors, and maintain protected clearances of at least 24 inches from overhead wood framing (pp. 263–264).

entities/american-concrete-institute.title: american-concrete-institute
entities/american-concrete-institute.content:
American Concrete Institute
The American Concrete Institute is a professional engineering organization referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code for its authoritative specifications and testing standards governing masonry and concrete construction.
Role in the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code
Within Chapter 24 on masonry-standards, the code mandates that tests for cast stone specimens must be conducted in accordance with the tentative specifications for cast stone, Serial Designation P-3-A-29T, issued by the American Concrete Institute (p. 150). This ensures that cast stone used in masonry construction meets rigorous structural and quality benchmarks.
Additionally, the code incorporates the American Concrete Institute’s standards for reinforced-concrete-standards. A note in the regulations explicitly adopts the “Building code requirements for reinforced concrete A.C.I. (American Concrete Institute) 318-47 DTD. Sept., 1947” as part of the building code, directing readers to page 311 for full details (p. 175).
In 1949, Ordinance No. 3703 further amended the code to harmonize with surrounding municipalities, formally adopting the “Building Code Requirements for Reinforced Concrete ACI (American Concrete Institute) 318-47 dated September, 1947” into Chapter 26 (p. 336). These references establish the A.C.I. as a primary authority for material testing and structural design calculations within municipal construction regulations.
Related Documents

entities/american-institute-of-steel-con.title: american-institute-of-steel-construction
entities/american-institute-of-steel-con.content:
The American Institute of Steel Construction (AISC) is a professional organization referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code for its authoritative engineering manuals and standards. Dated January 1934, its Steel Construction handbook is cited as the required source for approved standard connection angles, power-driven rivets, and unfinished bolts used in steel joisted floor construction for buildings exceeding eight stories in height (p. 216). The code mandates that connections between steel joists and primary structural members comply with these specifications to ensure reliable structural-steel-connections within steel-joist-design frameworks.
Additionally, the code recognizes the AISC’s “standard specifications for fire-proofing structural steel buildings,” dated October 8, 1927, and its subsequent amendments. For buildings with a structural steel frame, these AISC specifications are accepted as an alternative to the general ASTM C19-33 testing standards when determining structural-fireproofing and fire-resistive-construction ratings (p. 283). This provision allows engineers to utilize specialized industry standards tailored to steel structures for fire protection compliance.
In a 1949 amendment to the building code (Ordinance No. 3703), the american-institute-of-steel-construction’s Steel Construction Manual, 5th Edition, was formally adopted as the standard for allowable factors governing structural steel requirements (p. 336). This update further solidified the organization’s role in defining the structural integrity and design parameters for steel and iron construction within the municipality.
