entities/american-public-health-associat.title: american-public-health-association
entities/american-public-health-associat.content:
American Public Health Association
The American Public Health Association is a professional organization dedicated to advancing public health and disease prevention. In municipal regulations for the city-of-miami, the association’s established appraisal techniques for measuring housing quality are formally adopted to evaluate dangerous-buildings (p. 353).
Specifically, Ordinance No. 3963 utilizes these standards to identify basic structural and sanitary deficiencies, such as inadequate plumbing, lack of electricity, and severe overcrowding, which render a property unfit for human habitation (p. 353). These metrics provide objective criteria for the chief-building-inspector and the building-board-of-appeals when determining whether a structure must be repaired, vacated, or demolished to protect community welfare.
See also: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364, dangerous-buildings, city-of-miami.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364

entities/american-society-for-testing-ma.title: american-society-for-testing-materials
entities/american-society-for-testing-ma.content:
The American Society for Testing Materials (ASTM) serves as the primary authority for material testing standards and specifications within the 1935-miami-dade-building-code. The code mandates that all material tests and specifications conform to ASTM standards in effect on the date of adoption, including subsequent amendments (p. 153, p. 170).
Masonry and General Materials
In Chapter 24, ASTM designations govern the quality and testing of masonry components:

Brick: Burned clay, sand-lime, and concrete bricks must meet ASTM standards for absorption and modulus of rupture (e.g., Designations C62-30, C73-30, C55-33T) (p. 146-147).
Concrete & Gypsum: Hollow concrete units and gypsum partition tiles must comply with ASTM tentative or standard specifications (e.g., C90-34T, C52-33) (p. 147, p. 149).
Mortars: Cements and limes used in mortars must adhere to ASTM specifications (e.g., C5-26, C6-31, C9-30) (p. 150).

Reinforced Concrete and Structural Metals
Chapter 26 extensively utilizes ASTM specifications to regulate reinforced-concrete-standards and structural metals:

Concrete Testing: Compression tests for concrete must follow the “Standard Method of Making Compression Tests of Concrete” (Designations C 39-27 and C 39-33) (p. 170, p. 173, p. 174).
Cement & Aggregates: Portland cement must conform to “Standard Specifications and Tests for Portland Cement” (C9-30), while organic impurities in sands are tested per “Standard Method of Test for Organic Impurities in Sands for Concrete” (C 40-27) (p. 171).
Metal Reinforcement: Billet-steel and rail-steel reinforcement bars must meet specifications A 15-35 and A 16-35, respectively. Cold-drawn steel wire for reinforcement follows specification A 82-34 (p. 171).
Structural Metals (Ch. 26): Structural steel for buildings must comply with specification A 9-24, and cast-iron sections for composite columns must meet specification A 44-04 (p. 172).

Chapter 27 further expands ASTM requirements for structural steel and iron quality:

Structural Steel: Must conform to Standard Specifications for Structural Steel for Buildings, A. S. T. M., Designation A9-33 (p. 206).
Cast Steel: Must conform with Standard Specifications for Steel Castings, A. S. T. M., Designation A27-24 (p. 206).
Cast Iron: Must conform with Tentative Specifications for Gray Iron Castings, A. S. T. M., Designation A48-32T (p. 206).

Fire-Resistive Construction Standards
In Part VIII, ASTM standards govern the classification of fire-resistive-construction. Materials, systems, units, and forms of construction must meet the requirements of “Standard Specifications for Fire Tests of Building Construction and Materials,” A. S. T. M. Designation C19-33, to be officially classified as one-hour, two-hour, three-hour, or four-hour fire-resistive construction (p. 283). Authoritative tests conducted in accordance with these specifications are required to verify fire resistance ratings. Additionally, load-bearing hollow clay tiles must conform to A. S. T. M. standards to be used in rated wall assemblies (p. 289).
Roofing and Waterproofing Materials
The code applies ASTM testing methodologies to roofing-standards and waterproofing materials. Physical properties for roll roofing, roofing felt, and felt membrane—including pliability, heating resistance, saturation percentage, and surface integrity—must be evaluated using the “Standard Methods of Testing Felted and Woven Fabrics Saturated With Bituminous Substances For Use in Waterproofing and Roofing,” A. S. T. M. Designation D 146-27, and its subsequent editions (p. 301). These tests ensure that composition roofings meet the strict durability and fire-retardant requirements mandated by the code.
Inspection and Competency
The Building Inspector retains the authority to judge the competency of testing personnel based on training and experience, and may disapprove those with records of technical incompetency (p. 170). All test results must be maintained on file for two years post-acceptance, ensuring ongoing compliance with building-inspection protocols (p. 170). By establishing these rigorous testing benchmarks, ASTM provides the technical foundation for material quality across masonry, concrete, steel, fire-resistive construction, and roofing systems.

entities/american-standards-association.title: american-standards-association
entities/american-standards-association.content:
The American Standards Association is an organization referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code for its development of safety standards. Specifically, Chapter 48 of the code mandates that the construction, alteration, use, and maintenance of all elevators, escalators, and dumbwaiters must comply with the requirements of the “Safety Code for Elevators” recommended by the American Standards Association, 1925 Edition, along with its subsequent editions and amendments (p. 311). This requirement ensures that vertical-transportation-safety standards are met within the city. See 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324

entities/american-welding-society.title: american-welding-society
entities/american-welding-society.content:
The American Welding Society is a standards organization referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code to establish technical requirements for fusion welding in structural steel and wrought iron construction.
In the context of structural steel design, the code mandates that electrode wire used for structural-steel-connections must conform to the American Welding Society Specifications E No. 1-A or E No. 1-B, as published in December 1921 in the American Welding Society Bulletin No. 2, along with any subsequent amendments (p. 213). The code also stipulates allowable unit working stresses for static loads in weld metal, such as 13,000 lbs per sq. in. for tension in butt welds and 11,300 lbs per sq. in. for shear in fillet welds, aligning with established engineering practices of the era 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224

entities/american-wood-preservers-associ.title: american-wood-preservers-association
entities/american-wood-preservers-associ.content:
The American Wood Preservers Association is referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code regarding the chemical treatment of creosoted wood piles used for structural support (p. 220). The code mandates that creosoted piles made of Douglas fir or Southern pine must comply with Specification No. 41-a or No. 39-a established by the association (p. 220). These specifications require a full-cell creosote treatment applied under pressure to achieve a minimum final retention of 16 pounds per cubic foot for Douglas fir and 20 pounds per cubic foot for Southern pine (p. 220-221).
This standard ensures the longevity and structural integrity of timber foundations, particularly when piles are cut off above the mean low water level or ground water table (p. 220-221). The association’s specifications are integral to the pile-foundation-engineering and foundation-construction-requirements outlined in the code, providing verified methods for protecting wood against decay and environmental exposure. See 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224 for further details on foundation standards.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224

entities/ashbury-park.title: ashbury-park
entities/ashbury-park.content:
Ashbury Park is a residential subdivision located in city-of-miami, Florida, referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code as a geographic marker for municipal zoning boundaries.
In the code’s definition of fire-zones, specifically the boundaries for Fire Zone No. 2 (Secondary Fire District), the district line runs through specific lots within Ashbury Park. The boundary traverses Lots 19, 18, 17, 16, 20, 11, 10, 9, 8, and 7 of the subdivision (p. 84). This section of the boundary runs parallel to and 100 feet distant from the East line of federal-highway (p. 84).
This designation places the subdivision within the city’s regulated fire districts, subjecting structures there to specific construction-types and building-height-and-area-limits depending on their exact location relative to the zone lines (p. 84).
See also: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/bay-ridge.title: bay-ridge
entities/bay-ridge.content:
Bay Ridge is a residential subdivision and geographic area in Miami, Florida, referenced in the 1935 building code as part of the legal boundary definition for fire-zones. Specifically, on page 84 of the code, the boundary line for Fire Zone No. 2 (Secondary Fire District) traverses through “Bay Ridge amended,” noting the south line of Block 4, the southeast corner of Lot 5 in Block 4, and the west lines of Lot 6 and Block 3 (p. 84). The boundary route connects Bay Ridge along the dixie-highway to adjacent subdivisions such as biscayne-shores and vista-biscayne (p. 84). As a designated area within the city’s fire district mapping, properties in Bay Ridge were subject to specific construction and fire safety regulations mandated by the city-of-miami (p. 81). See 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84 for full code details.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/bayshore.title: bayshore
entities/bayshore.content:
Bayshore is a subdivided area in Miami, Florida, cited in the 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84 for defining municipal fire district boundaries. In the 1935 code, specific lots and blocks within Bayshore (including Blocks 12, 16, 23, 24, and 6) are used as geographic markers to trace the northern limits of fire-zones No. 2 (the Secondary Fire District) (p. 84). The boundary line runs north along the eastern sides of these lots, situated roughly 150 feet east of and parallel to the center line of biscayne-boulevard (p. 84). This precise platting was essential for establishing construction and fire-resistive-construction requirements within the city’s regulated zones.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/biscayne-bay.title: biscayne-bay
entities/biscayne-bay.content:
Biscayne Bay is a coastal body of water that serves as a key geographic boundary marker within the 1935-miami-dade-building-code. Specifically, the harbor line of Biscayne Bay defines portions of the perimeter for fire-zones within the city-of-miami (p. 81, 83).
The legal boundary for Fire Zone No. 1 begins at the mouth of the miami-river, extends north along the harbor line of Biscayne Bay to the center line of N. E. 13th Street, and later returns south along the same harbor line back to the river’s mouth (p. 81, 83). This natural feature anchors the geographic description of the primary Fire District, establishing where stricter construction and building relocation regulations apply. See 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84 for full context.
Related Documents
See also: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364

entities/biscayne-boulevard.title: biscayne-boulevard
entities/biscayne-boulevard.content:
Biscayne Boulevard is a major thoroughfare in the city-of-miami that is subject to specific municipal regulations regarding public space and visual aesthetics. Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, the boulevard is heavily restricted to protect its character and ensure traffic safety.
Specifically, the code prohibits the erection, placement, or maintenance of any new signs, signboards, billboards, or similar advertising devices along Biscayne Boulevard between Northeast Thirteenth Street and Northeast Fifty-fourth Street (p. 306). This restriction extends to any property abutting the boulevard, preventing structures from extending into, over, or above the street, public walks, or parkways within this designated zone. However, the regulation includes a grandfather clause that preserves any advertising devices already in place at the time of the code’s adoption (p. 306).
Violations of these advertising restrictions are treated as daily offenses, with the mere existence of an unauthorized sign serving as prima facie evidence of a violation by the property possessor (p. 306). These rules are part of the broader advertising-sign-standards enforced by the city to maintain orderly urban development, as detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324

entities/biscayne-shores.title: biscayne-shores
entities/biscayne-shores.content:
Biscayne Shores is a residential subdivision referenced in the 1935 1935-miami-dade-building-code as a key geographic marker for establishing municipal fire district boundaries within the city-of-miami. Specifically, the legal description for fire-zones No. 2 (Secondary Fire District) traces its limits through specific blocks and lots in Biscayne Shores Unit No. 2 and Unit No. 3 (p. 84). The boundary line runs parallel to and 100 feet east of the federal-highway, passing southward along the dixie-highway corridor before cutting through the subdivision’s blocks to define the northern extent of the secondary fire zone (p. 84). This zoning designation dictated stricter construction standards and fire safety regulations for buildings located within its limits. For additional context on Miami’s fire district mapping and building requirements, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/board-of-examiners-and-appeals.title: board-of-examiners-and-appeals
entities/board-of-examiners-and-appeals.content:
The Board of Examiners and Appeals is a three-member body established by the 1935-miami-dade-building-code to evaluate the suitability of alternate materials and construction methods, and to provide reasonable interpretations of code provisions (p. 32). Its primary function is to hear appeals from applicants whose requests for building permits using non-standard construction types or design methods have been refused by the building-inspection authority (p. 32).
Composition and Appointment
The Board consists of three qualified members: a licensed and practicing Architect, a competent Builder of Structures on land, and a licensed and practicing Structural Engineer (p. 32). Each member must possess at least ten years of professional experience (p. 32). They are appointed by the City Manager of the city-of-miami and serve one-year terms (p. 32). The Building Inspector serves as an ex-officio member and Secretary but is prohibited from voting on any questions before the Board (p. 32).
Powers and Procedures

Appeals Process: Individuals may appeal to the Board by submitting a written notice to the Building Inspector and paying a $10.00 fee, which is refunded if the appeal is granted but retained if denied (p. 32).
Interpretations: The Board may interpret code provisions for special cases by unanimous vote if the existing rules do not cover the issue or would cause manifest injustice (p. 32).
Material Approvals: Decisions regarding the use of alternate materials or construction types require a majority vote (p. 32). If approved, the usage only becomes effective upon authorization by an amendment to the Code by the City Commission (p. 32-33).
Reporting: All decisions and findings must be rendered in writing to both the Building Inspector and the appellant. The Board may also recommend new legislation consistent with its findings to the City Commission (p. 32).

For more details on enforcement and appeals, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p25_44.

entities/board-of-examiners-for-special-.title: board-of-examiners-for-special-construction
entities/board-of-examiners-for-special-.content:
The Board of Examiners for Special Construction is a regulatory body established under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code to evaluate alternative construction methods that fall outside standard code provisions. Specifically tasked with reviewing special systems of reinforced-concrete-standards, the board is composed of competent engineers, architects, and builders (p. 166).
Sponsors of reinforced concrete systems that conflict with or are not covered by the standard code regulations may present test data and performance records to this board for review. The board holds the authority to investigate submitted data and issue rulings that govern the design and construction of these specialized systems. These rulings carry the same legal force and effect as the official building code itself (p. 166). The specific designation and appointment procedures for the board members are outlined in other sections of the code.
See also: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p165_184
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p165_184

entities/chief-building-inspector.title: chief-building-inspector
entities/chief-building-inspector.content:
The Chief Building Inspector is a municipal official in the city-of-miami responsible for enforcing building codes, inspecting structures, and managing permit-related financial guarantees. Under various ordinances, the Chief Building Inspector holds significant authority over building-permits, construction site oversight, and the identification of dangerous-buildings.
Permit Bonds and Deposits
For construction, excavating, or grading permits, the Chief Building Inspector determines the required performance bond or cash deposit (minimum 50) based on the value of public property at the site (p. 348). The inspector issues receipts for deposits and returns them after final inspection if no damage or debris remains. If the owner fails to correct site conditions within five days, the inspector is authorized to use the funds for repairs or cleanup (p. 348). Similarly, for carnivals and circuses, the inspector sets cash deposits (minimum 100 and $500, respectively) and oversees site cleanup compliance (p. 363).
Inspections and Dangerous Buildings
The Chief Building Inspector conducts semi-annual inspections of public assembly buildings and annual inspections of all city structures to identify safety hazards (p. 354). The inspector investigates complaints and reports from the department-of-public-safety divisions, places official notices on unsafe structures, and issues written orders for repair, vacation, or demolition via registered mail (p. 354, p. 355). In emergency cases posing immediate danger, the inspector can order immediate action (p. 358). The inspector also testifies at hearings before the building-board-of-appeals and reports non-compliance to the City Attorney (p. 355, p. 357).
Elevator and Lifting Apparatus Oversight
Under Ordinance No. 2149, the Building Inspector receives and reviews all inspection reports for elevators, escalators, dumbwaiters, hoists, and other lifting apparatus, whether conducted by city employees or private insurance and casualty companies (p. 365-366). Based on these reports, the inspector issues certificates of operation for safe apparatus, valid for three months for passenger and freight elevators and escalators, and six months for dumbwaiters (p. 366). The inspector also holds approval authority over private company inspectors, a prerequisite for waiving city inspection fees after July 1, 1939 (p. 366). Additionally, the Building Code Index outlines the inspector’s broad jurisdiction, including authority to order load tests, approve building use changes, and oversee the removal of safety devices (p. 368, p. 375).
Administrative Duties
Decisions regarding bond amounts can be appealed by owners to the department-of-public-service (p. 348). The Chief Building Inspector operates under the framework of the 1935-miami-dade-building-code and related municipal ordinances to protect public welfare and property (p. 345, p. 352).
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p365_375

entities/city-commission.title: city-commission
entities/city-commission.content:
The City Commission is the legislative governing body of the city-of-miami responsible for enacting municipal ordinances, including amendments to the local building code. In the context of the regulations documented in the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code, the Commission holds several key administrative and oversight powers:

Ordinance Enactment: The Commission passes and adopts all building-related ordinances, such as those expanding fire-zones, mandating performance bonds for construction, and regulating permit applications (p. 345, p. 347, p. 350).
Elevator Inspection Regulations: The Commission enacted Ordinance No. 2149 (August 1939) to oversee vertical-transportation-safety, mandating that all elevator, escalator, and hoist inspection reports be filed with the Building Inspector. It adjusted inspection fees, authorized waivers for approved insurance company inspectors, and redirected collected fees to the City Tax Collector rather than a special elevator fund (p. 365-367).
Board Appointments: The Commission appoints members to the building-board-of-appeals through official resolutions. Appointments are made to fill vacancies or succeed outgoing members, with each appointee serving a two-year term (p. 356).
Emergency Extensions: When a property owner fails to complete required repairs, vacation, or demolition of dangerous-buildings within the standard 60-day period, the Commission may pass a resolution to grant additional time for completion (p. 356).
Emergency Measures: For ordinances declared as emergency measures due to urgent public need for peace, health, and safety, the Commission may dispense with the standard requirement of reading the ordinance on two separate days, provided it passes by a vote of at least four-fifths (4/5) of its members (p. 359, p. 367).

The Commission works in conjunction with executive officials such as the chief-building-inspector and the Mayor to enforce municipal safety and construction standards.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364
1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p365_375

entities/city-of-miami.title: city-of-miami
entities/city-of-miami.content:
The City of Miami is a municipality in Florida subject to the regulatory framework established by the 1935-miami-dade-building-code. The code is enforced by the city-commission and administered through various municipal departments and officials, including the chief-building-inspector, City Manager, Zoning Department, Fire Department, Health Department, and the department-of-public-safety (pp. 20–21, 22). The Building Inspector is granted the power of a Police Officer to enforce code provisions, enter premises for inspection, and halt unsafe work (p. 29). Specific municipal departments retain jurisdiction over specialized requirements; for instance, the Plumbing Department of the City of Miami mandates approved grease drains or traps for public garages and service stations (p. 67), and the Fire Department of the City of Miami dictates fire extinguishing apparatus requirements for amusement and private garage structures (p. 80).
The city is divided into three distinct fire-zones, each with specific construction and relocation restrictions (p. 5, 22, 81). The entire city is established as a Fire District, with Fire Zone No. 1 boundaries recommended by the planning-board and adopted by the city-commission (p. 82). Fire Zone No. 2 extends along major thoroughfares such as biscayne-boulevard and Federal Highway, while Zone 3 encompasses all remaining territory (pp. 81, 83–84). Subsequent legislation, such as Ordinance 2568 (1941), further expanded the secondary fire zone into coconut-grove to restrict frame construction (pp. 322–323). Additionally, Ordinance 2471 (1941) significantly expanded Fire Zone No. I to prevent new frame structures and reduce fire hazards, incorporating areas around Greenwood Park, Highland Park, Spring Garden Subdivisions, and lands bordering the miami-river and seybold-canal (pp. 341–344). Property development and structural modifications within the city limits require building-permits, which involve multi-departmental plan approvals for zoning, plumbing, electrical, and health compliance (p. 20). Permit fees are calculated based on the estimated cost of construction using detailed cost-per-cubic-foot schedules categorized by building group and type, with rates updated in 1945 and 1950 to reflect actual market increases (pp. 325–330). The municipal government also strictly regulates the relocation of structures, mandating performance bonds, cash bonds for street damage, and continuous inspector accompaniment during transit (pp. 21–22). Notably, the City of Miami, along with Dade County, the State of Florida, and the United States government, is exempt from paying building permit fees, though it must still obtain permits for municipal work (p. 24).
The city maintains a Zoning Map and Code to determine permitted land uses, and all structures must be maintained in safe condition under municipal oversight (p. 5, 19). The Building Inspector has the authority to order work stopped, condemn dangerous buildings, and mandate repairs or demolition, with expenses charged against the property owner (p. 30). The city also enforces termite provisions, requiring extermination within 60 days of notice (p. 31). Amendments to the code further clarified permit thresholds, requiring permits for any repairs valued at Fifty ($50.00) Dollars or more, and mandated a 12-hour curing period for all masonry foundations before elements are laid (pp. 333–335).
For complex structures, the code requires the employment of a “Registered Inspector” who must be bonded to the City of Miami for $5,000 (p. 27). Appeals regarding code interpretations or alternate materials are handled by the board-of-examiners-and-appeals, appointed by the City Manager (p. 32). Ordinance 1678 (1937) further formalized this process by establishing a dedicated Board of Appeals to review building condemnations, with hearing expenses borne by the municipality (p. 321). The city has also formally adopted external engineering standards to ensure structural integrity, including the american-concrete-institute ACI 318-47 for reinforced-concrete-standards and the american-institute-of-steel-construction 5th Edition Manual for structural steel requirements (p. 336). Specific material standards were introduced for cellular steel floors, mandating minimum gauge thicknesses and fire-resistive construction (p. 331), while detailed span tables were added for floor and roof joists based on fiber stress and live loads (pp. 335–337). Additionally, comprehensive standards for metal roofing and siding were established, specifying minimum gauges, fastening methods, and corrosion protection for steel and aluminum (pp. 339–340).
Buildings are categorized by occupancy-classification into Groups A through J, dictating requirements for fire-separation in mixed-use buildings (p. 41). City administration buildings are explicitly included in these classifications, falling under Group A, B, or C depending on their seating capacity and stage configuration (pp. 45, 51, 54). All such municipal and assembly structures must front directly upon a public street at least twenty feet wide (pp. 45, 51, 54) and comply with strict emergency-egress and sprinkler-systems mandates (pp. 45–64). Multi-occupancy municipal buildings connected to the public service line are required to house an independent, ventilated central meter room for main switches and disconnecting equipment (pp. 51, 54, 60, 63, 67, 70, 73, 76, 78). The code explicitly preserves the enforcement of specific city ordinances, including Ordinances No. 873, No. 1109, and No. 1156 covering dry cleaning plants, alongside regulations for awnings, zoning, contractors, electrical work, plumbing, and gas systems (pp. 34, 66).
The municipality strictly regulates construction-site-regulations to protect public rights-of-way. The department-of-public-safety grants temporary permission for street and sidewalk occupation during construction, limiting material storage to one-third of the street width and prohibiting obstruction of fire hydrants or drainage (pp. 301–302). In Fire Zone No. 1 or for buildings exceeding two stories, owners must construct a covered walkway with specific load-bearing and lighting standards until exterior work is complete (p. 302). Permanent projections over public property, such as marquees, balconies, and porches, are heavily restricted and require revocable permits from the Building Inspector, mandating fire-resistive materials, structural anchorage, and minimum ten-foot sidewalk clearances (pp. 303–304). Furthermore, the city explicitly prohibits the construction of vaults, excavations, or cellar-ways beneath any sidewalk to preserve public safety and infrastructure integrity (p. 304).
The city strictly regulates outdoor advertising and temporary-canvas-structures. Billboards are prohibited in R, B-1, and B-2 zoning districts and face specific bans along biscayne-boulevard between NE 13th and NE 54th Streets (p. 306). Sign permits require engineering drawings to ensure wind resistance and structural safety, mandating metal or fireproof materials for large installations and strict clearances from electrical conductors (pp. 307–310). All signs must display the manufacturer’s imprint (p. 311). Similarly, awnings and canopies require permits, with metal frames mandatory in Fire Zones 1 and 2 (pp. 315–316). Sidewalk projections are limited to nine feet with minimum clearance heights of 7’6” for metal and 6’6” for canvas, and storage of merchandise under these structures is prohibited without special approval (pp. 316, 318).
Municipal oversight extends to vertical-transportation-safety, requiring elevators, escalators, and dumbwaiters to comply with the 1925 American Standards Association code and the 1935 National Electrical Code (p. 311). Regulations mandate minimum carrying capacities, operator competency certifications, and mechanical door interlocks (pp. 312–313). Structural requirements include incombustible car materials, steel guide rails, substantial buffer springs, and emergency roof exits (pp. 313–314). Buildings exceeding 100 feet must maintain a fire department-ready elevator around the clock, and the Building Inspector conducts quarterly inspections to enforce safety devices for speed limits (p. 314). The city further refined these protocols through Ordinance No. 2149, passed on August 2, 1939, which mandated that all elevator, escalator, dumbwaiter, and hoist inspection reports—whether conducted by municipal employees or private insurance and casualty companies—be filed directly with the Building Inspector (p. 365–366). This ordinance adjusted the fee structure to $2.50 for inspections and 50 cents for operation certificates, while authorizing a complete waiver of the inspection fee after July 1, 1939, for approved private inspectors (p. 366). Additionally, it redirected all collected vertical transportation fees to the City Tax Collector rather than a dedicated elevator fund, declaring the changes an emergency measure to preserve public peace, health, and safety (p. 367).
The code is declared an emergency measure for public safety, establishing penalties of up to $500 or 60 days imprisonment for violations, with each day constituting a separate offense (pp. 319–320). The municipality has continuously updated its regulatory framework through subsequent ordinances, such as Ordinance 2972 (1945) which revised building permit fees (p. 324), Ordinance 4049 (1950) which adjusted cost schedules to match market rates (p. 330), Ordinance 3334 (1947) which detailed cellular steel floor requirements (p. 331), and Ordinance 3703 (1949) which harmonized foundation, joist, and roofing standards with surrounding municipalities (pp. 333–340). The city has also formally adopted various ASTM standards for construction materials like brick, concrete, and gypsum (pp. 320–321). The comprehensive nature of these regulations is reinforced by an alphabetical building code index that systematically organizes municipal requirements across construction types, fire zones, occupancy groups, and structural standards (p. 368–375).

entities/coconut-grove.title: coconut-grove
entities/coconut-grove.content:
Coconut Grove is a district within the city-of-miami subject to specific municipal building regulations. In 1941, the planning-board recommended placing a defined area within Coconut Grove into the secondary fire-zones to prohibit the construction of new frame structures and align with the general city plan (p. 322). Ordinance No. 2568, adopted in October 1941, formally amended the building code to include several local subdivisions—such as Percival & Sanford Revised, McFarlane Homestead Plat, St. Alban’s Park, Frow Homestead Amended, and DeHedowville—within this restricted zone (p. 322). This regulatory update was declared an emergency measure for public safety and property preservation, carrying fines or imprisonment for violations (p. 323). For further details on the code amendments and zoning classifications, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324

entities/coral-gables.title: coral-gables
entities/coral-gables.content:
Coral Gables is a municipality adjacent to the city-of-miami, frequently cited in the 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104 as a critical reference point for establishing municipal-boundary-definitions. The 1935 code uses the city limits of Coral Gables to demarcate the jurisdictional boundaries for various thoroughfares and regulatory zones. For example, the southern extent of sw-17th-avenue and its associated fire limits are defined relative to the Coral Gables city line and “Coral Gables Craft’s Section” (p. 95). Similarly, the boundary definitions for west-flagler-street trace along the city limits of Coral Gables to delineate areas subject to specific building regulations (p. 98). The code also references the “West City Limit line of Coral Gables” and “North Coral Gables” when mapping out routes for SW 8th Street (p. 100-101). These references establish where Miami’s fire-zones and building codes cease and municipal authority shifts, highlighting the importance of precise geographic demarcations in early 20th-century urban planning.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104

entities/coral-park.title: coral-park
entities/coral-park.content:
Coral Park is a Miami subdivision referenced in the 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84 as part of the boundary definition for fire-zones. Specifically, the code outlines the perimeter of Fire Zone No. 2 (Secondary Fire District), tracing the boundary along the east lines of Lots 10 and 9-A within the Coral Park subdivision (p. 83). This geographic designation placed properties within Coral Park under specific construction and fire safety regulations mandated by the city-of-miami to mitigate fire hazards in densely developed areas.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/department-of-public-safety.title: department-of-public-safety
entities/department-of-public-safety.content:
The department-of-public-safety is a municipal agency referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code responsible for granting temporary permissions for the use of public streets and sidewalks during construction projects (p. 301). The department oversees compliance with construction-site-regulations to ensure that building materials and temporary structures, such as covered walk-ways, do not obstruct pedestrian traffic, fire hydrants, or drainage systems. Its authorization is required before contractors can occupy public rights-of-way, aligning with broader building-permits and municipal safety standards. See 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304

entities/department-of-public-service.title: department-of-public-service
entities/department-of-public-service.content:
The Department of Public Service is a municipal agency within the city-of-miami responsible for regulating the use of public property and issuing special permits for specific construction and event activities. Under the 1935 Building Code, the department oversees the excavation and construction of areaways, cellar openings, underground vaults, or other substructures located beneath sidewalks or in front of buildings (p. 305). Owners must obtain a special permit from the department and strictly adhere to its prescribed plans, specifications, and regulations before undertaking such work (p. 305). Additionally, the department controls the permissible projection distance of structures or solid iron doors extending from a building onto adjacent public walkways (p. 305). The occupation of public space under these permits remains revocable by the city at any time, with the permit holder liable for associated costs (p. 305). Chapter 46 of the code is dedicated to the department’s rules and regulations pertaining to public property, though the specific provisions in this edition are marked as reserved (p. 305).
Subsequent ordinances expanded the department’s role in protecting public infrastructure from construction and temporary events. The Director of the Department of Public Service recommends and oversees the requirement for performance bonds or cash deposits prior to issuing permits for construction, excavating, clearing, filling, or grading land (p. 347). These deposits, determined by the chief-building-inspector, protect the city against damage to pavements, trees, and other public property, with appeals regarding deposit amounts directed to the Director (p. 348). Similarly, for temporary events such as carnivals and circuses, the department mandates cash deposits to cover potential site damage and cleanup costs (p. 362). After such events vacate, the Department of Public Service conducts final inspections of the site and travel routes; if no damage or debris remains, the department authorizes the refund of the deposit (p. 363).
For broader context on municipal oversight, see building-permits and construction-site-regulations.
Related Documents

entities/dixie-highway.title: dixie-highway
entities/dixie-highway.content:
Dixie Highway is a major roadway referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code as a geographical marker for municipal zoning boundaries (p. 84). Within the code, it serves as a key boundary line for fire-zones, specifically delineating the northern extent of Fire Zone No. 2 (the Secondary Fire District) in the city-of-miami (p. 84). The legal description traces the zone’s limits along the highway before transitioning to other local streets and subdivisions such as bay-ridge (p. 84). For additional context on municipal zoning and fire district regulations, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/elwood-court.title: elwood-court
entities/elwood-court.content:
Elwood Court is a residential subdivision or land tract in city-of-miami referenced in the 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84. It is specifically cited in the legal boundary description for fire-zones, marking the perimeter of Fire Zone No. 2 (Secondary Fire District) (p. 83). The municipal boundary line traces through “Lot 3, Block 3 and Block 2. Elwood Court” before proceeding to Beverly Terrace (p. 83). As part of the officially designated fire district, properties located within Elwood Court were subject to stricter construction-types and fire-resistive-construction mandates enforced by the city’s building regulations.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/federal-highway.title: federal-highway
entities/federal-highway.content:
Federal Highway is a major roadway in Miami-Dade County that served as a critical geographic reference in the 1935 building code.
In the context of the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code, Federal Highway is primarily utilized to define the eastern boundaries of fire-zones, specifically delineating the limits of Fire Zone No. 2 (the Secondary Fire District) (p. 83). The code establishes property lines and district boundaries by referencing points “parallel with and 100 feet distant from the East line of Federal Highway” (p. 83). This alignment runs through several subdivisions and plats, including miami-shores and biscayne-shores, serving as a consistent north-south axis for municipal zoning and fire district regulations (p. 84). As a defined thoroughfare, it anchors the municipal-boundary-definitions used by the city-of-miami to regulate construction and fire safety compliance across its expanding northern territories (p. 81).
See also: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/florida-east-coast-railroad.title: florida-east-coast-railroad
entities/florida-east-coast-railroad.content:
Florida East Coast Railroad
The florida-east-coast-railroad (often abbreviated as F.E.C. Railroad or Railway) served as a major geographic reference point and boundary line for municipal zoning and street definitions in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code.
Role in Municipal Boundaries
In the 1935 code excerpts, the railroad’s right-of-way is repeatedly cited to demarcate limits for various streets and districts:

North Miami Avenue: The northern boundary begins at the West right-of-way line of the F.E.C. Railroad (p. 87).
Little River District: The western limit is defined by the West Right-of-way line of the F.E.C. Railroad, tracing routes near N.E. 76th and N.E. 82nd Streets (p. 103).
Buena Vista District: Boundaries for N. 36th Street and N. 41st Street utilize the West line of the F.E.C. Railroad Company Right-of-Way (p. 104).
Fire Limits and Street Routes: The East right-of-way line is referenced in tracing routes along ne-2nd-avenue and defining municipal-boundary-definitions for subdivisions like West Broadmoor (p. 86).

These references highlight how the railroad infrastructure functioned as a fixed landmark for municipal-boundary-definitions in early 20th-century Miami urban planning, as detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104

entities/florida-hotel-state-commission.title: florida-hotel-state-commission
entities/florida-hotel-state-commission.content:
The Florida Hotel State Commission was a state regulatory agency responsible for establishing and enforcing construction standards for lodging and food service establishments in Florida (p. 73). Under the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, the Commission promulgated the “Rules Governing Construction of Hotels, Apartment Houses, Rooming Houses and Restaurants,” which applied to occupancy-classification Group H buildings (p. 73). The local code mandated that all structures under the Commission’s jurisdiction conform to its current rules, with the stipulation that the Miami-Dade Code would supersede state regulations if they were less restrictive (p. 73). The Commission’s rules dictated specific requirements for toilet facilities and room construction within these occupancies (p. 75), and references to the State Hotel Code noted restrictions on one-exit apartment types (p. 74). Additionally, the Commission’s regulations imposed strict limits on vertical egress, prohibiting more than thirteen risers without a platform or landing in stairways serving these occupancies (p. 247).

entities/johnson-waddell-addition.title: johnson-waddell-addition
entities/johnson-waddell-addition.content:
The Johnson & Waddell Addition is a residential subdivision in the city-of-miami referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code to establish municipal zoning boundaries. Specifically, it serves as a geographic marker for the northern and western limits of fire-zones (Fire Zone No. 1) (p. 81). The code traces the district boundary along the center lines of Blocks 20, 21, 30, 40, and 41 within the addition, connecting northeast-second-avenue (via N.E. 20th Street) to N.W. 14th Street (p. 81). This subdivision’s layout was utilized to demarcate areas subject to stricter construction and safety regulations. See 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84 for full boundary descriptions.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/lawrence-estate-land-company-su.title: lawrence-estate-land-company-subdivision
entities/lawrence-estate-land-company-su.content:
The Lawrence Estate Land Company Subdivision is a residential development in city-of-miami referenced in the 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84. It serves as a key geographic landmark for defining the boundaries of fire-zones, specifically Fire Zone No. 1. The code’s boundary description traces the zone limits west through Blocks 13 and 14 of the subdivision to the center line of N. W. 12th Avenue, then south to Block 18, and east along Blocks 18 and 17 (p. 83). Its inclusion highlights the reliance on established local land plats for municipal regulatory zoning in 1935.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/miami-river.title: miami-river
entities/miami-river.content:
The Miami River is a prominent waterway that serves as a primary geographic boundary for fire-zones within the city-of-miami (p. 81). In the 1935 building code, the river’s channel lines and mouth are used to delineate the limits of Fire Zone No. 1, also known as the “Fire District” (p. 81). The boundary description begins at the mouth of the river where it meets biscayne-bay and traces along the waterway’s edges (p. 81, 83). The river’s channel lines intersect with various streets, including the North line of N. W. 1st Street, and connect to the seybold-canal to define the western and northern limits of the district (p. 83).
See 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84 for the full legal description of the fire zone boundaries.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/miami-shores.title: miami-shores
entities/miami-shores.content:
Miami Shores is a subdivision and geographic area within the city-of-miami that plays a specific role in the municipal zoning regulations outlined in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code.
In the code, Miami Shores is referenced as a key landmark for establishing the boundaries of fire-zones, specifically Fire Zone No. 2 (the Secondary Fire District) (p. 84). The legal boundary description traces along the federal-highway and passes through specific lots and blocks within the area, notably citing “Block 64, Miami Shores Section No. 3” and “Block 66 of said subdivision” (p. 84).
As part of the designated fire district, structures and land uses within Miami Shores are subject to specific municipal building regulations, including restrictions on moving buildings and requirements for construction-types and fire-resistive-construction depending on the exact zone classification (p. 81). For further details on the zone boundaries and regulations, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/national-board-fire-underwriter.title: national-board-fire-underwriters
entities/national-board-fire-underwriter.content:
The National Board of Fire Underwriters is a prominent organization referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code for establishing critical safety standards across various industrial, commercial, and accessory occupancies. The code repeatedly cites the organization’s external guidelines to regulate hazardous materials, high-risk structures, mechanical installations, and fire suppression systems.
Inflammable Liquids & Oil Burners
The code mandates that the storage, handling, use, and sale of gasoline, fuel oil, and other inflammable liquids must comply with the “Suggested Fire Prevention Ordinance, Edition of 1930, recommended by the National Board of Fire Underwriters,” along with its subsequent amendments. This requirement applies across multiple occupancy classifications:

Group E: Buildings handling automobiles or inflammable liquids must adhere to these standards for storage and sales operations (p. 66).
Group F: Moderately hazardous commercial and industrial buildings are prohibited from storing volatile inflammable liquids unless handling complies with the ordinance (p. 69).
Group G: Non-hazardous industrial and commercial buildings face the same compliance requirements for any storage or handling of inflammable liquids (p. 72).
Group J: Private garages and accessory structures must follow these guidelines for any storage or handling of inflammable liquids (p. 80).

Additionally, oil burning apparatus using commercial fuel oil, furnace oil, diesel oil, or other inflammable liquids must be constructed and installed in compliance with the regulations of the National Board of Fire Underwriters for Oil Burning Equipment and fuel storage, as recommended by the national-fire-protection-association (1928 edition) (p. 266).
Airplane Hangars
The code cites the organization’s Recommended Requirements National Board Fire Underwriters for the Construction and Protection of Airplane Hangars (edition effective September 1, 1930, amended November 15, 1931) as the governing standard for the design and fire protection of aircraft hangars classified under occupancy-classification Group E (p. 64). These external guidelines inform maximum area limits and sprinkler-systems or deluge system mandates for hangar facilities.
Chimney Construction and Heating Apparatus
The code relies on the organization’s standards for heating-and-chimney-safety and mechanical venting. All chimneys must be constructed in conformity with “A Standard Ordinance for Chimney Construction” recommended by the National Board of Fire Underwriters, Fifth Edition, revised 1935, or as subsequently revised (p. 256). Furthermore, asbestos flue pipes used in place of unglazed fire clay or concrete tile for gas vents must be approved by the National Board of Fire Underwriters, with specific requirements for tapered joints and approved flue pipe cement (p. 261).
Fire Doors & Openings
The code mandates that all required fire doors must be installed according to the “Regulations of the National Board of Fire Underwriters for the Protection of Openings in Walls and Partitions Against Fire,” recommended by the national-fire-protection-association (Edition effective October 15, 1930, corrected to May 1, 1935, or subsequent amendments) (p. 294). This ensures doors fit tightly against walls or frames to effectively stop fire and smoke, complementing the broader requirements for fire-rated-openings.
Fire Suppression & Standpipe Systems
The organization’s codes form the baseline for building fire suppression infrastructure:

Automatic Sprinklers: Every required system must comply in all respects with the National Board of Fire Underwriters regulations governing the installation of automatic sprinkler equipment (1931 edition), with limited exceptions for single water supplies and domestic system connections (p. 270).
Standpipes: Sizing requirements for standpipe systems in buildings exceeding four stories in height follow the National Board of Fire Underwriters’ Code of 1928 and its subsequent amendments, dictating minimum diameters based on building height and pressure drop calculations (p. 271).

Incinerators & Nitrocellulose Films

Incinerators: Any incinerator constructed and installed in compliance with the recommended rules and regulations of the National Board of Fire Underwriters for Incinerators (January 1, 1935 edition) or the Fifth Edition of the National Board of Underwriters Building Code is accepted as meeting code requirements (p. 268).
Film Storage: All regulations for the storage and handling of photographic and X-ray nitrocellulose films must conform to the National Board of Fire Underwriters regulations, as recommended by the national-fire-protection-association (July 15, 1931 edition), including strict compartmentalization and fire-resistive cabinet standards (p. 279).

entities/national-fire-protection-associ.title: national-fire-protection-association
entities/national-fire-protection-associ.content:
National Fire Protection Association
The National Fire Protection Association is cited in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code as the author of key safety standards referenced for regulating hazardous materials, oil burning equipment, film storage, and the installation of fire-resistive openings.
Inflammable Liquids
Specifically, the code mandates that the handling of volatile inflammable liquids in quantities exceeding one gallon within occupancy-classification Group D buildings must comply with an ordinance adopted by the association in May 1926 (p. 62). Titled “Regulating the Use, Handling, Storage and Sale of Inflammable Liquids and the Products Thereof,” this ordinance and its subsequent amendments establish the baseline for safe storage and usage protocols (p. 62).
Similarly, for occupancy-classification Group H buildings (hotels, apartment houses, dormitories, and rooming houses), the storage and handling of gasoline, fuel oil, and other inflammable liquids are prohibited unless they comply with the same suggested ordinance adopted by the association in May 1926 (p. 76). These requirements work in tandem with the code’s broader fire-separation rules to mitigate explosion and fire risks in institutional, care, and residential facilities.
Oil Burning Equipment
Regarding heating-and-chimney-safety, oil burning apparatus using commercial fuel oil, furnace oil, diesel oil, or other inflammable liquids must be constructed and installed in compliance with regulations recommended by the National Fire Protection Association, Edition of 1928, and its subsequent amendments (p. 266). These regulations govern the construction and installation of oil burning equipment as well as the storage and use of oil fuels in connection therewith.
Film Storage
For the storage and handling of photographic and X-ray nitrocellulose films, regulations must conform to those recommended by the National Fire Protection Association, Edition of July 15, 1931, and its subsequent amendments (p. 279). This ensures safe handling of highly flammable materials used in motion picture and medical imaging contexts.
Fire-Resistive Openings
Section 4304 of the code mandates that all fire doors required by the code be installed in the manner prescribed in the “Regulations of the National Board of Fire Underwriters for the Protection of Openings in Walls and Partitions Against Fire,” which are recommended by the National Fire Protection Association (p. 294). The specific edition cited is effective October 15th, 1930, corrected to May 1st, 1935, including subsequent amendments (p. 294). These regulations govern the installation of fire-rated-openings in stairway enclosures, smoke-proof towers, corridors, and other critical egress paths to ensure they fit tightly and provide an effective stop for fire and smoke.

entities/ne-2nd-avenue.title: ne-2nd-avenue
entities/ne-2nd-avenue.content:
N.E. 2nd Avenue (also historically referenced as part of the Dixie Highway) is a major north-south thoroughfare in Miami used extensively in the 1935 building code to delineate municipal and regulatory boundaries (p. 86). The avenue serves as a primary reference line for defining the eastern limits of specific districts, with boundary markers often placed 100 feet east or west of its centerline (p. 86). Tracing its path from NW 41st Street in the north to NE 41st Street in the south, the avenue passes through or borders numerous historic subdivisions, including Elwood Place, Rosedale, Dixie Highway Park, El Portal, Miami Shores, and Shadowlawn (p. 86-87). It acts as a key geographic divider in the definition of municipal-boundary-definitions and fire-zones within the city-of-miami (p. 86, 87). Additionally, N.E. 2nd Avenue forms the eastern boundary of the Buena Vista district, intersecting with N. 36th Street, N. 41st Street, and north-miami-avenue (p. 104). For further details on its regulatory role, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104

entities/north-miami-avenue.title: north-miami-avenue
entities/north-miami-avenue.content:
North Miami Avenue is a major north-south thoroughfare in city-of-miami whose jurisdictional boundaries were meticulously defined in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code (p. 87-89). The official boundary description begins at the North line of ne-2nd-avenue (p. 87) and extends northward along the West right-of-way line of the florida-east-coast-railroad (p. 87). The route traverses numerous early 20th-century subdivisions, including Rosewin Park, Biltmore, Shadowlawn, Belmont Park, La Paloma, Phoenix Park, and Wynwood Park, before reaching the North City Limits of Miami (p. 88-89). The boundary then loops west and south through areas such as Hamilton Terrace, Everglades Commercial Addition, and the johnson-and-waddell-addition, eventually returning to NW 20th Street to close the district (p. 88-89). These precise municipal-boundary-definitions established the geographic scope for zoning, fire limits, and building regulations applicable to properties along the avenue. The avenue is also referenced in broader district definitions, such as the Buena Vista zone (p. 104). For further details on the boundary descriptions, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104

entities/northeast-second-avenue.title: northeast-second-avenue
entities/northeast-second-avenue.content:
Northeast Second Avenue is a major north-south thoroughfare in the city-of-miami. In municipal zoning and safety regulations, it serves as a primary boundary marker for designated fire districts.
According to Ordinance No. 3970, the center line of Northeast Second Avenue defines the eastern edge of the newly extended fire-zones No. 1. The designated zone begins at its intersection with northeast-thirteenth-street and extends northward along the avenue to Northeast Thirty-seventh Street (p. 345). This boundary placement dictates the application of stricter building and fire safety codes for properties located west of the avenue within the specified limits (p. 346).
For further details on the zoning changes, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364

entities/northeast-thirteenth-street.title: northeast-thirteenth-street
entities/northeast-thirteenth-street.content:
Northeast Thirteenth Street is a roadway in the city-of-miami that serves as a key geographic boundary for municipal zoning regulations. In the 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364 document, it is referenced in Ordinance No. 3970, which expands fire-zones within the city. The ordinance defines the southern and eastern limits of the newly added area for Fire Zone No. 1 along the center line of Northeast Thirteenth Street (p. 345). The boundary begins at its intersection with northeast-second-avenue and extends eastward until it meets the Westerly U.S. Harbor Line of biscayne-bay (p. 346). This street delineates the corporate limits subject to specific building and fire safety codes.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364

entities/nw-7th-avenue.title: nw-7th-avenue
entities/nw-7th-avenue.content:
N.W. 7th Avenue is a major north-south thoroughfare in Miami that serves as a key reference line for municipal boundaries and fire limits in the 1935 building code (p. 90). The legal description begins at the present City Fire Limits in Lot 10, Block 14, near the East line of seybold-canal (p. 90). The avenue’s boundary line runs northward along the canal and through numerous residential and commercial subdivisions, including Highland Park, Jefferson Park, Suwannee Park, Bay Vista Park, and Railroad Shops Addition (p. 90-92). It extends north to cross NW 81st Street and continues southward parallel to the center line of the avenue through areas like Rock Terrace, Northern Tract, and NW 7th Avenue Addition (p. 91-92). The boundary ultimately terminates at the present fire limits of the city-of-miami at the center line of Block 70 North (p. 92). This thoroughfare is instrumental in establishing the geographic scope for fire-zones and municipal jurisdiction as detailed in 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104

entities/planning-board.title: planning-board
entities/planning-board.content:
The planning-board of the city-of-miami is a municipal advisory body tasked with reviewing urban development proposals and recommending changes to local zoning and building regulations. Within the scope of the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, the board served as a key authority in updating municipal safety districts to align with evolving city plans.
On December 2, 1940, the board unanimously adopted Resolution Number 602, recommending an ordinance amendment to expand fire-zones by adding certain areas on the outskirts of downtown Miami to Fire Zone Number I (p. 341). This proposal aimed to prohibit the erection of new frame structures in the designated area, thereby reducing fire hazards and supporting the general city plan. The recommendation was subsequently enacted through Ordinance No. 2471, formally integrating the new boundaries into the city’s fire district regulations (p. 341).
Later, on September 2, 1941, the board unanimously adopted Resolution Number 771, recommending an amendment to expand the secondary fire zone into Coconut Grove (p. 322). This action further demonstrated the board’s role in bridging municipal planning objectives with the technical enforcement standards outlined in the building code.
Continuing its efforts to enhance public safety, the board met on December 12, 1949, and unanimously passed Resolution No. 2261. This resolution recommended the further extension of Fire Zone No. 1 to incorporate additional land areas within the corporate limits, as detailed in Ordinance No. 3970 (p. 345). This ongoing expansion of fire districts reflects the board’s commitment to adapting municipal regulations to the city’s growth and safety needs.

entities/seybold-canal.title: seybold-canal
entities/seybold-canal.content:
The Seybold Canal is a waterway in city-of-miami referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code as a key geographical marker for municipal zoning. Specifically, its channel lines are used to delineate the boundaries of fire-zones, forming part of the western and southern perimeter of fire-zones No. 1 (p. 81). The code describes traveling along the East Channel Line of the canal to reach the miami-river, establishing a clear demarcation for fire district regulations within the city. For further details on its role in zoning, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/south-bayshore-drive.title: south-bayshore-drive
entities/south-bayshore-drive.content:
South Bayshore Drive is a prominent roadway in the city-of-miami that serves as a key geographic reference and municipal boundary line in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code. The code explicitly defines its jurisdictional route from sw-17th-avenue to SW 27th Avenue and E. Ingraham Highway (p. 104). Its boundaries are delineated by tracing paths along the Pelican Canal, through the John T. Peacock Estate, past Dinner Key, and across multiple historic subdivisions such as New Biscayne Amended and Crystal Bluff Addition (p. 104).
The roadway functions as a critical surveyor’s baseline for establishing municipal-boundary-definitions and fire-zones within the county. For instance, the code uses the North line of South Bayshore Drive to demarcate the eastern limits of SW 17th Avenue’s jurisdiction (p. 92, p. 95). Additionally, boundary descriptions for NW and SW 27th Avenue converge along the North line of Bayshore Drive before looping back to their points of beginning (p. 94). These precise legal descriptions highlight the road’s role in early 20th-century urban planning and land subdivision records. For further details on its boundary definitions, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104

entities/sw-17th-avenue.title: sw-17th-avenue
entities/sw-17th-avenue.content:
S.W. 17th Avenue is a major thoroughfare in Miami-Dade County that serves as a key reference line for municipal boundaries and fire limit designations in the 1935 building code (p. 92). The document outlines a specific jurisdictional zone beginning at a point on the North line of south-bayshore-drive 110 feet east of the avenue’s East line, extending northward parallel to it through various tracts and subdivisions (p. 92). The boundary runs 150 feet west of the center line of S.W. 17th Avenue in certain sections before returning to South Bayshore Drive (p. 95). The avenue is also explicitly referenced in the definition of the south-bayshore-drive corridor, which extends from S.W. 17th Avenue to S.W. 27th Avenue (p. 95). These precise geographic measurements establish the scope of municipal-boundary-definitions and delineate fire-zones where specific construction regulations apply (p. 95). For further details on these boundary descriptions, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104

entities/underwriters-laboratories.title: underwriters-laboratories
entities/underwriters-laboratories.content:
Underwriters Laboratories
Underwriters Laboratories is a testing and certification organization referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code for verifying the quality, safety, and fire-resistance of construction materials, openings, and roofing systems.
Role in Building Codes
In the context of masonry-standards, the code permits the Building Inspector to accept certificates furnished by Underwriters Laboratories in lieu of conducting on-site tests for hollow concrete blocks (p. 147).
The organization established specific standards that govern the classification of certain materials:

Special Concrete Units: Hollow concrete block or tile that complies with the Underwriters Laboratories’ Standard for Hollow Concrete Building Units (dated September 26, 1930) and maintains a minimum compressive strength of 700 psi are classified as “Special” units (p. 148).
Certified Units: Any hollow concrete building units certified by Underwriters Laboratories are automatically recognized as meeting the requirements for “Special” units under the code (p. 148).

These provisions streamline the inspection process by relying on established third-party testing for allowable-working-stress and material durability, reducing the need for redundant physical testing on construction sites.
Fire Doors and Windows
Underwriters Laboratories standards are heavily referenced for fire-rated-openings and fire-resistive-construction. Tin-clad wood-core fire doors must be constructed in accordance with the “Underwriters’ Standard for Tin-Clad Fire-Doors and Shutters,” Edition of August 1935 (p. 292). Any fire door bearing the label of Underwriters Laboratories, Incorporated, is accepted as meeting the code’s requirements for fire-resistive doors (p. 293). Similarly, fire-resistive windows bearing the UL label are automatically accepted as meeting the one-hour fire-resistive window requirements (p. 295).
Roofing Standards
The code relies on Underwriters Laboratories classifications to categorize roofing-standards into “Fire Retardant” and “Ordinary” roofings. “Fire Retardant” roofings must meet Class A or B specifications established by Underwriters Laboratories, Incorporated (p. 295). Roof coverings bearing the UL label and installed according to their guidelines are accepted as compliant (p. 296). Additionally, composition fire retardant roofings require a top layer of mineral surfaced roofing bearing the Class C label of Underwriters Laboratories, Inc. (p. 296). “Ordinary” roofings are defined as any covering meeting the Class C Specifications of Underwriters Laboratories, Incorporated (p. 298).

entities/united-states-department-of-com.title: united-states-department-of-commerce
entities/united-states-department-of-com.content:
The United States Department of Commerce is referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code as the issuing authority for the American Lumber Standards (p. 153). The code specifically adopts the Department’s “Simplified Practice Recommendations, R16-29,” effective July 1, 1929, as the basis for determining minimum acceptable sizes and assigning allowable working stresses for structural lumber (p. 153). These recommendations are foundational to the wood-framing-standards and allowable-working-stress requirements detailed in Chapter 25 of the code (p. 153-154).
See 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p145_164 for more details.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p145_164

entities/united-states-forest-products-l.title: united-states-forest-products-laboratory
entities/united-states-forest-products-l.content:
United States Forest Products Laboratory
The United States Forest Products Laboratory is a research institution referenced in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code for its authoritative engineering data on structural wood properties.
Role in Building Code Standards
In Chapter 25, which outlines wood-framing-standards, the code adopts the laboratory’s recommendations to establish allowable-working-stress limits for “Yard Lumber” and “Other Lumber” (p. 157). Specifically, Table II in the code derives its stress values for extreme fiber bending, parallel and perpendicular compression, horizontal shear, and modulus of elasticity directly from the laboratory’s data for lumber conforming to the “Common” Structural Material grade example (p. 157).
Additionally, the laboratory’s fourth-power-parabolic-Euler formula is mandated for calculating allowable compressive stresses in intermediate and long timber columns where the unsupported length-to-least dimension ratio exceeds 11 (p. 158). These engineering standards ensure that wood structural members safely support imposed loads without exceeding material limits.

entities/vista-biscayne.title: vista-biscayne
entities/vista-biscayne.content:
Vista Biscayne is a residential subdivision or land tract referenced in the 1935 1935-miami-dade-building-code. It appears in the legal boundary description for fire-zones, specifically delineating the northern limits of the city-of-miami’s Fire Zone No. 2 (Secondary Fire District) (p. 84). The official boundary line runs along the west line of Lot 22, Block 1 of Vista Biscayne, situated adjacent to water-view-park and running parallel to the federal-highway (p. 84). This geographic designation places the area under specific municipal construction and fire safety regulations outlined in the code. For further details on the zone boundaries, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/water-view-park.title: water-view-park
entities/water-view-park.content:
Water View Park is a residential subdivision and land plat located within the city-of-miami jurisdiction, noted in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code for its role in defining municipal fire district boundaries.
In the code, Water View Park is specifically cited in the legal description of Fire Zone No. 2 (the Secondary Fire District) (p. 84). The boundary line for this zone traces through “Lot 4, Block 1 and 2, Water View Park” before proceeding parallel to the federal-highway (p. 84). This designation places properties within the subdivision under the specific construction and safety regulations mandated for Zone 2 fire-zones.
For further details on the zoning boundaries, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84.
Related Documents

1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84

entities/west-flagler-street.title: west-flagler-street
entities/west-flagler-street.content:
West Flagler Street is a major thoroughfare in city-of-miami whose precise municipal boundaries and alignments are extensively detailed in the 1935-miami-dade-building-code. The code defines the street’s route beginning at the Lawrence Estate and extending westward through numerous historic subdivisions, including Orange Park, 12th Street Manors, Flagler Pines, and into the coral-gables city limits (p. 97).
The document specifies property lines, lot boundaries, and alley centers that form the street’s corridor, noting landmarks such as West Flagler Street Building Lots and adjacent residential and commercial plats (p. 97). Additionally, West Flagler Street is explicitly referenced in relation to municipal fire safety regulations, particularly where its “South Fire Limit Line” dictates building restrictions along adjacent avenues like SW 1st Street (p. 99). These definitions establish the geographic scope for fire-zones and construction regulations within the city’s expanding urban grid.
For more details on the boundary definitions and fire zone restrictions associated with this corridor, see 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104.
