Sec. 1303. LOCATION ON PROPERTY. All exterior walls or parts of walls, except on street fronts, of Group H buildings which are less than three (3) feet from adjacent property line shall be of not less than one-hour fire-resistive construction as specified in Section 4302. All openings in exterior walls except on street fronts, which are less than five (5) feet from adjacent property lines shall be protected by doors or windows of one-hour fire-resistive construction as specified in Section 4304. When openings are placed closer than three (3) feet to property lines other than street fronts, the sum of the width of such openings shall constitute not more than twenty-five (25) per cent of the total length of the walls affected. See Section 504 for regulating adjacent buildings on the same property.
58
Sec. 1304. STAIRS AND EXITS. Stairs and exits shall be provided as specified in Chapter 33.
Smokeproof towers shall be provided as and when specified in Chapter 33.
All stairs and exits in Group H buildings shall open directly upon a street or alley or upon a yard or court not less than four (4) feet in width directly connected to a street or alley by means of a passageway not less in width than the stairway opening into such passageway and not less than seven (7) feet in height.
Passageways and corridors shall be provided as required in Chapter 33.
Sec. 1305. LIGHT, VENTILATION AND SANITATION. All rooms of Group H buildings used for eating, living and/or sleeping purposes shall be provided with light and ventilation by means of windows with an area of not less than fifteen (15) per cent of the total floor area of such room or rooms.
TOILETS AND FLOORS. Every building of Division 1 occupancies in this Group shall be provided with toilet and toilet rooms as required by the “Current Rules Governing Construction of Hotels, Apartment Houses, Rooming Houses and Restaurants” as promulgated by the Department of Hotel Commission of the State of Florida. Every building of Division 2 occupancies in this Group shall be occupied and/or where more than four or more persons are employed shall be provided with at least one (1) toilet. All buildings and each subdivision thereof occupied and/or where both sexes are employed shall be provided with access to at least two (2) toilets, either located in such building or conveniently located in a building adjacent thereto. The above minimum requirements for uses by persons of the Caucasian race and where persons other than this race are employed, separate toilets shall be provided.
Toilets shall not open directly into a Restaurant, Kitchen or public place where food is served, except through a vestibule.
“For Floor Construction see Chapter 31.”
Sec. 1306. ENCLOSURE OF VERTICAL OPENINGS. All elevator shafts, vent shafts, stairways and other vertical openings shall be enclosed as specified under Types of Construction.
Sec. 1307. FIRE EXTINGUISHING APPARATUS. Automatic sprinklers, standpipes and basement pipe inlets shall be installed as and when specified in Chapter 38.
Sec. 1308. SPECIAL HAZARDS. Chimneys and heating apparatus shall conform to the requirements of Chapter 37.
Every boiler room or room containing a central heating plant using solid or liquid fuel shall be separated from the rest of the building by a “Special Fire Separation” as specified in Section 503.
The storage and handling of gasoline, fuel oil and other inflam-
69
mable liquids shall not be permitted in any Group H building, unless such storage and handling complies with the suggested ordinance regulating the Use, Handling, Storage and Sale of Inflammable Liquids and the Products Thereof, adopted by the National Fire Protection Association May, 1926, and its subsequent amendments. All doors leading into rooms in which volatile inflammable liquids are used or kept shall be of one-hour fire-resistive construction as specified in Section 4304 and shall be kept normally closed.
Sec. 1309 Reserved.
Sec. 1310. MIXED OCCUPANCIES. Separations between Group H occupancies and all other occupancies shall be provided as specified in Section 503.
Sec. 1311. METER ROOM. All buildings of multiple occupancy connected by electrical service from the public service line or generating plant shall have an independent central room of not less than three feet by five feet (3’x5’) and seven (7) foot ceiling height with a ventilated door accessible to all occupancies of the building; for the housing of the main switch, disconnecting equipment and its controlled devices and meters for serving the occupants. The construction of this room shall conform to the same types of material and construction as the main building.
CHAPTER 14
REQUIREMENTS FOR GROUP I BUILDINGS
Sec. 1401. GROUP I OCCUPANCY DEFINED. Each Group I occupancy shall be considered as a separate building and the Group shall include dwellings and garage apartments accommodating not more than two (2) families, or hospitals and sanitariums accommodating not more than six (6) patients.
Sec. 1402. CONSTRUCTION, HEIGHT AND AREA ALLOWABLE. Buildings or parts of buildings classed in Group I because of use or the character of the occupancy shall be of Types I, II, III, IV* or V* construction. The floor areas of Types I and II shall be unlimited, the floor area of Types III and IV shall be limited to ten thousand (10,000) square feet, and the floor area of Type V shall be limited to seventy-five hundred (7500) square feet.
(*See Sections 1602 and 1603 for restrictions in Fire Zones.)
Sec. 1403. LOCATION ON PROPERTY. All exterior walls or parts of walls (including windows or doors), except on street fronts, of Group I buildings which are less than three (3) feet from adjacent property lines shall be of not less than one-hour fire-resistive construction as specified in Section 4302. When openings are placed closer than three (3) feet to property lines other than street fronts, the sum of the widths of such openings shall constitute not more than twenty-five
60
(25) per cent of the total length of the walls affected. (See Section 504 for regulating adjacent buildings on the same property.)
Sec. 1404. STAIRS AND EXITS. Stairs and exits shall be provided as and when specified in Chapter 33.
Sec. 1405. LIGHT, VENTILATION AND SANITATION. All rooms of Group I buildings used for eating, living and/or sleeping purposes shall be provided with light and ventilation by means of windows with an area not less than one-eighth (1/8) of the total floor area of such room or rooms.
Every bath and toilet room shall have at least one window of not less than three and one-half (3½) square feet in area, opening through the outer wall of the building, or shall be ventilated by a wall or ceiling vent of not less than two (2) square feet net area.
Sec. 1406. ENCLOSURE OF VERTICAL OPENINGS. Stairs in Group I buildings need not be enclosed. Dumb-waiter shafts, clothes chutes and other similar vertical openings shall be protected as specified in Section 3003.
Sec. 1407. FIRE EXTINGUISHING APPARATUS. Fire extinguishing apparatus when installed shall conform to the requirements of Chapter 38.
Sec. 1408. SPECIAL HAZARDS. Chimneys and heating apparatus shall conform to the requirements of Chapter 37.
Inflammable liquids shall not be stored or used in Group I buildings in quantities in excess of one (1) gallon and all such inflammable liquids shall be kept in tight or sealed containers when not in actual use.
Sec. 1409. EXCEPTIONS AND DEVIATIONS. Dwellings constructed on the roof of multiple storied buildings shall be considered as an additional story in so far as the construction, location, exposure, stairs, exits and fire extinguishing apparatus is concerned.
Sec. 1410. MIXED OCCUPANCIES. Separation of Group I occupancies from all other occupancies shall be provided as specified in Section 503.
Sec. 1411. ATTACHED GARAGES. When a private garage is located underneath or attached to or included in Group I occupancy, the following regulations as to its construction shall be rigidly observed:
The ceiling construction above the garage, when it is located beneath a Group I occupancy, or the roof when the garage is attached to a Group I occupancy, shall be unpierced and shall have a fire-resistance of at least one (1) hour, as specified in Chapter 43. The floor of the garage may be of earth fill or any incombustible material.
61
The floor of any Group I building shall be not less than seven (7) inches above the garage level. Walls, partitions and the under side of any stairway of the garage portion shall be of at least one (1) hour fire-resistive construction, as specified in Chapter 43.
Openings into the garage shall be restricted to a single doorway; such openings shall be protected by a solid slab wood door, not less than one and three-eighths (1⅜″) inch thick at all points, or a metal-covered door, or other closure of equivalent or greater fire-resistance, mounted so as to be gravity closing, or with spring hinges or door closers. No glass shall be permitted in such wood slab doors.
VENTILATION. “See Section 1505.”
Sec. 1412. CEILING HEIGHTS. All ceilings except bathroom shall have a clear height of eight feet four inches (8′-4″) minimum.
Sec. 1413. METER ROOM. All buildings of multiple occupancy connected by electrical service from a public service line or generating plant shall have an independent central room of not less than three feet by five feet (3′x5′) and seven (7) foot ceiling height with a ventilated door accessible to all occupancies of the building; for the housing of the main switch, disconnecting equipment and its controlled devices and meters for serving the occupants. The construction of this room shall conform to the same type of material and construction as the main building.
CHAPTER 15
REQUIREMENTS FOR GROUP J BUILDINGS
Sec. 1501. GROUP J BUILDINGS DEFINED. Each Group J building or occupancy shall be considered as a separate building and the Group shall include:
Division 1: Private Garages.
Division 2: Accessory buildings and structures such as sheds, fences over five (5) feet high, water tanks and towers.
Division 3: Stadiums, reviewing stands and amusement park structures.
Sec. 1502. CONSTRUCTION, HEIGHT AND AREA ALLOWABLE. Buildings or parts of buildings classed in Group J because of the use or the character of the occupancy shall be of Types I, II, III, IV* or V* construction as regulated by the requirements of Chapter 16. The floor area of Types I and II construction shall not be limited, the floor area of Types III and IV shall be limited to ten thousand (10,000) square feet and buildings of Type V construction shall not exceed one thousand (1,000) square feet in area and/or two (2) stories in height, except that such restriction of Type V* construction shall not apply to stadiums, reviewing stands or amusement park structures of the open skeleton-framed type. (*See Sections 1602 (F) and 1603 (F) for restrictions and special permit required in Fire Zones.)
62
Reviewing stands and amusement park structures shall be designed and constructed in a substantial manner so as to fully withstand all impact loads in addition to the static loads specified in Chapter 23. (See Appendix, Section 1502, for recommendations as to temporary stands.)
Events under canvas shall be termed as temporary structures and may have seating accommodations constructed of wood or iron or a combination of these two materials, provided the construction as contemplated and as erected is in accordance with the provisions of this Code. Permit for these structures shall be issued to cover a period of not exceeding six (6) months and may then be renewed for another six (6) months upon the payment of the proper fee if the structure is found safe and substantial by the Building Inspector.
Sec. 1503. LOCATION ON PROPERTY. All exterior walls or parts of walls (including windows or doors), except on street fronts, of Group J buildings which are less than three (3) feet from adjacent property lines shall be of not less than one-hour fire-resistive construction as specified in Section 4302. (See Section 504 for regulating adjacent buildings on same property.)
Sec. 1504. RAMPS, STAIRS, EXITS, AISLES AND SEATS. (a) Stairs and exits for amusement park devices shall be provided as specified in Chapter 33, except that stairs and ramps in buildings not exceeding two stories in height need not be enclosed. (See Section 3310 for Ramps.)
(b) Stairs, exits aisles and seating for stadiums and reviewing stands shall be as follows:


Stairs. All stairs shall have a rise of not more than seven and one-half (7½) inches and a tread of not less than ten (10) inches not including the nosing. (See Section 3310 for Ramps.)


Exits. There shall be provided one exit not less than seven (7) feet wide for each two thousand (2000) persons or major fraction thereof which the stadium or reviewing stand is designed to seat. Such exits shall be spaced not more than seventy-five (75) feet apart. Passageways serving such exits shall be not less than seven (7) feet in clear height nor less than seven (7) feet in clear width.


Aisles. Aisles not less than three feet six inches (3’ 6”) in width shall be provided so that there are not more than twenty (20) seats between any seat and aisle.


Seats. Where seats are not spaced or marked off in any stadium or reviewing stand, a distance of eighteen (18) inches along any bench or platform shall constitute one seat in computing the


63
required aisles, stairs and exits. Seats shall be spaced not less than twenty-six (26) inches back to back and where backs are provided for the seats they shall be spaced thirty (30) inches back to back.
Where the space under the stadium or reviewing stand is used for any purpose whatsoever, exits passing through this space shall be separated therefrom by walls, floors and ceilings of not less than one-hour fire-resistive construction.
Sec. 1505. LIGHT, VENTILATION AND SANITATION. Private garages which are constructed in conjunction with any Group H or I buildings and which have openings into such buildings shall be equipped with fixed louvered or screened openings or exhaust ventilation with exhaust openings located at least six (6) feet above the floor. The clear area of the louvered openings or the openings into the exhaust ducts shall be not less than sixty (60) square inches per car stored in such private garage. Under no circumstances shall a private garage have any opening into the living or sleeping room.
Amusement park structures which have enclosed spaces open to and used by the public shall be provided with light and ventilation, either natural or artificial, sufficient for safe and healthful conditions.
TOILETS AND FLOORS. All buildings occupied and/or where more than four (4) persons are employed shall be provided with at least one toilet. All buildings and each subdivision thereof occupied and/or where both sexes are employed shall be provided with access to at least two toilets, either located in such building or conveniently located in a building adjacent thereto. The above minimum requirements for uses by persons of the Caucasian race, and where persons other than this race are employed, separate toilets shall be provided.
“For Floor construction see Chapter 31.”
Sec. 1506. ENCLOSURE OF VERTICAL OPENINGS. Elevator shafts, vent shafts, stair-wells and similar vertical openings shall be enclosed as specified in Chapter 30 when extending through three (3) or more stories.
Sec. 1507. FIRE EXTINGUISHING APPARATUS. Fire extinguishing apparatus shall be installed as and when specified in Chapter 38 and as required by the Fire Department of the City of Miami.
Where more than three automobiles are stored in any private garage there shall be installed not less than one chemical extinguisher to each five cars or major fraction thereof.
Sec. 1508. SPECIAL HAZARDS. Chimneys and heating apparatus shall conform to the requirements of Chapter 37.
Inflammable liquids shall not be stored, handled or used in Group J buildings unless such storage or handling shall comply with the Suggested Fire Prevention Ordinance, Edition of 1930, recommended by the National Board of Fire Underwriters, and its subsequent amendments.
64
Sec. 1509. EXCEPTIONS AND DEVIATIONS. When storage space, termed by this Code a private garage, is designed, used or provides for the storage of more than ten (10) automobiles, such storage space shall be deemed a public garage.
Amusement park structures into which the public is admitted, other than those of the open frame type of construction, when more than one story or two hundred (200) square feet in area shall have the exterior walls, bearing partitions and floors of not less than one-hour fire-resistive construction as specified in Chapter 43.
Sec. 1510. MIXED OCCUPANCIES. Separation of Group J occupancies from any other occupancies shall be provided as specified in Section 503 and in Section 1509.
PART IV
CHAPTER 16
RESTRICTIONS IN FIRE ZONES
Sec. 1601. FIRE ZONES DEFINED. The entire city of Miami, Florida, is hereby declared to be and is hereby established a Fire District and said Fire District shall be known and designated as Fire Zones One, Two and Three. Fire Zone No. 1 (or “Fire District”) shall include such territory or portions of the City as outlined in this Section. Fire Zone No. 2 (or “Secondary Fire District”) shall include such territory or portions of the City as outlined in this Section. Fire Zone No. 3 shall include all other territory or portions of the City not otherwise included in Fire Zones No. 1 and No. 2. Wherever reference is made to any Fire Zone, it shall be construed to mean one of the three Fire Zones designated and referred to in this Section.
Moving of all buildings in Fire Zones One, Two and Three shall be subject to the requirements of Section 201 of this Code (and Sections 1602, 1603).
FIRE ZONE NO. 1 (OR “FIRE DISTRICT”). Starting at the mouth of the Miami River and extending North along harbor line of Biscayne Bay to center line of N. E. 13th Street; thence West on N. E. 13th Street to center line of N. E. 2nd Avenue; thence North on N. E. 2nd Avenue to center line of N. E. 20th Street; thence West on N. E. 20th Street to center line of Block 20 of Johnson & Waddell Addition; thence South on center line of Blocks 20, 21, 30, 40 and 41 of Johnson & Waddell Addition to center line of N. W. 14th Street; thence West on center line of N. W. 14th Street to center line of Block Japes or Sosts’ Addition; thence South on center line of Blocks 2, 8 and 11 of Japes or Sosts’ Addition, 7-N, 14-N, 27-N, 34-N, 47-N and 54-N to center line of Block 67-North; thence West on center line of Blocks 67-N, 68-N, 69-N, 70-N and 14, Spring Garden Subdivision, to East Channel line of Seybold Canal. summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p105_124
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This excerpt from the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code outlines fire zone restrictions and establishes minimum requirements for building construction types, specifically focusing on Fire-Resistive, Heavy Timber, and Ordinary Masonry structures (p. 105-124).
Fire Zone Restrictions
The code delineates strict regulations for buildings located in Fire Zone No. 1, No. 2, and No. 3. In Fire Zone No. 1, buildings over 400 square feet must comply with stringent rules, including mandatory “fire retardant” roofing and one-hour fire-resistive protection for exterior doors and windows (p. 105). Alterations exceeding 20% of a building’s value in this zone require full compliance with new building standards (p. 105). Fire Zone No. 2 mandates one-hour fire-resistive exterior walls for Type V construction and restricts Type IV buildings to under 1,000 square feet (p. 106). Fire Zone No. 3 imposes fewer restrictions, allowing any building that meets the general code requirements (p. 107).
Classification of Construction Types
Part V classifies all buildings into five types based on fire resistance, with Type I being the most fire-resistive and Type V the least (p. 108). If a building contains multiple construction types without absolute fire separation, it is classified under the least fire-resistive type present (p. 108).
Type I: Fire-Resistive Construction
Type I buildings feature structural frames of fireproofed steel/iron or reinforced concrete, with masonry or concrete exterior walls (p. 109). There are no limits on height or floor area for this type (p. 109). Structural members require extensive fireproofing: buildings over 85 feet or 8 stories need 4-hour protection for columns and 3-hour for floors, while shorter buildings require 3-hour and 2-hour protection respectively (p. 110-111). Combustible materials are heavily regulated, though wood is permitted for decorative trim, mezzanine floors (up to 33 1/3% of room area), and specific roof sheathing conditions (p. 113).
Type II: Heavy Timber Construction
Type II buildings are limited to 75 feet in height and 7 stories, with foundations and exterior walls of masonry or reinforced concrete (p. 114). The structural frame can utilize heavy timbers, provided wood columns are at least 8 inches in their least dimension and beams/girders are at least 6 inches (p. 116). Fireproofing for steel members is required unless the building is one story or under 65 feet tall (p. 116). Wood floor construction must be at least 3 inches thick with a 1-inch top layer, and roof construction must be covered with fire-retardant roofing (p. 117).
Type III: Ordinary Masonry Construction
Type III buildings are capped at 55 feet in height and 5 stories, allowing interior load-bearing construction of masonry, concrete, steel, or wood, while requiring masonry or concrete for exterior walls (p. 119). Fireproofing of structural members is generally omitted unless dictated by occupancy or location (p. 123). In buildings with cellars or basements, the underside of combustible first-floor construction must be protected by a 1-hour fire-resistive ceiling (p. 124).
Technical Illustrations and Foundation Requirements
The document includes detailed technical diagrams for typical CBR (Concrete Block Reinforced) wall sections for both residential and commercial construction, highlighting roof sheathing, rafter spacing, and bolt anchoring specifications (p. 120-121). Additionally, a table outlines the minimum allowable dimensions for foundation walls and footings in private residences, specifying top widths, footing widths, and minimum thicknesses based on whether the wall is joist-bearing (p. 122).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p125_144
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This excerpt from the 1935-miami-dade-building-code establishes construction standards for Type IV and Type V buildings, alongside comprehensive engineering regulations for structural-loads and wind-pressure-design.
Type IV (Metal Frame) Buildings
Type IV structures feature steel, iron, masonry, or reinforced concrete frameworks with metal or incombustible exterior walls and roofs (p. 125). building-height-and-area-limits restrict height to forty-five feet, extending to fifty-five feet on sloping ground, with allowable areas dictated by occupancy and location (p. 126). structural-fireproofing of structural members is not required, and wood is permitted only where specifically allowed (p. 127). Foundations must include ventilated openings covered with wire mesh, proportioned at two square feet per fifteen feet of exterior wall (p. 126).
Type V (Wood Frame) Buildings
Type V buildings utilize wood or wood-combination construction for walls, partitions, floors, and roofs, except where prohibited by fire-zones or occupancy-classification rules (p. 127). Maximum height is thirty-eight feet, or forty-five feet on slopes, with foundations required to rest on continuous masonry or reinforced concrete piers (p. 128). Exterior walls must use 2x4 studs spaced sixteen inches on centers, with thicker studs required for three-story buildings (p. 130). Detailed specifications govern exterior coverings, including weatherboarding, shingles, stucco over metal lath, and masonry veneer (p. 132-135). Floor and roof joist spans are tabulated based on wood grade and spacing, with mandatory fire-separation every seven feet to prevent concealed air spaces (p. 131, 135-138). Attached private garages require one-hour fire-resistive-construction walls and ceilings, gravity-closing doors, and dedicated ventilation-requirements (p. 139-140).
Engineering Regulations & Load Standards
The code defines dead loads as permanent structural weight and live loads as all other imposed forces (p. 140). Minimum unit live loads vary by occupancy, ranging from forty pounds per square foot for dwellings to over two hundred fifty for heavy storage (p. 141-142). Live load reductions are permitted for girders and columns based on tributary area and floor level, with stricter limits for warehouses (p. 142-143). Wind pressure design standards scale from twenty-five to forty-five pounds per square foot depending on building height, with specific exceptions for exposed structures (p. 143). Owners must conspicuously post designed live loads and maximum seating capacities, and certificate-of-occupancy require verification of floor load suitability (p. 144).
Key structural requirements intersect with broader principles of construction-types, fire-resistive-construction, structural-loads, and wind-pressure-design.

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p145_164
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This document presents Chapters 24 and 25 of the 1935-miami-dade-building-code, establishing material quality, testing protocols, and structural design standards for masonry-standards and wood-framing-standards.
Chapter 24: Masonry Quality and Design
The code mandates that all masonry materials—including brick, plain concrete, hollow concrete block, gypsum, hollow clay tile, and cast stone—comply with minimum standards and american-society-for-testing-materials (ASTM) specifications (p. 145). The Building Inspector retains authority to order quality tests at the owner’s or contractor’s expense if materials appear substandard (p. 145). Specific material requirements include:

Brick: Burned clay, sand-lime, and concrete bricks must have an average water absorption of ≤12% (max 15% individual) in a 5-hour test, and a minimum flatwise modulus of rupture of 450 psi average (300 psi individual) (p. 146-147).
Hollow Concrete Block: Units for exterior or party walls must meet gross cross-sectional compressive strength thresholds (700–1000 psi average depending on face shell thickness) and absorb ≤10% water in a 24-hour test (p. 147). Mix proportions and curing times (≥48 hours) are strictly defined (p. 147).
Gypsum: Used structurally in floors/roofs, neat gypsum must achieve 1,800 psi compressive strength, while fiber and coarse aggregate variants range from 500–1,000 psi depending on composition (p. 148).
Mortars: Only lime-cement or cement mortar is permitted for unit masonry, with cement mortar required below grade or in wet conditions (p. 150). The code provides a formula to calculate allowable working stress for intermediate mortar ratios (p. 150).

Allowable allowable-working-stress limits for compression are tabulated by material type and mortar composition, ranging from 70–80 psi for concrete/clay tile block up to 800 psi for granite ashlar in Portland cement mortar (p. 151-153). Concentrated loads may exceed these limits by no more than 25% (p. 153).
Chapter 25: Wood Quality and Design
Wood structural members must conform to American Lumber Standards, with quality classified into Structural, Yard, and Other lumber (p. 154). Structural lumber requires grade-marking and adherence to association rules, while Yard and Other lumber require Building Inspector approval and stress assignment based on defect analysis (p. 154-156). Extensive tables provide allowable unit stresses for bending, shear, perpendicular compression, and modulus of elasticity across species like Douglas Fir, Southern Pine, Oak, and Redwood (p. 156-158).
Column design utilizes three categories based on the unsupported length-to-least dimension ratio (l/d):

Short columns (l/d ≤ 10) use standard compressive stress values (p. 159).
Intermediate columns apply a parabolic reduction formula until stress drops to one-third of the short column value (p. 159).
Long columns (l/d > K) use the Euler formula, with a maximum slenderness limit of l/d = 50 (p. 159).

Framing details dictate bearing requirements (≥4 inches on masonry), preservative treatments, joist/rafter spacing, and mandatory metal anchors at masonry intersections (p. 160-161). Stud walls require doubled top plates in bearing partitions, minimum 2”x4” studs, and proper framing around openings (p. 162). Roof framing mandates valley rafter sizing, minimum 25/32” sheathing, and 18-inch ventilation air spaces for flat-deck construction (p. 163), addressing specific ventilation-requirements.
The code enforces strict fire stopping measures at all wall/floor/ceiling intersections to block concealed air spaces, requiring continuous bridging or blocking no larger than 7 feet in any dimension (p. 163-164), contributing to fire-separation and fire-resistive-construction. Additionally, termite prevention provisions require removal of stumps/roots to 12 inches below grade, placement of wood on masonry/concrete foundations, and maintaining a minimum 6-inch elevation of foundation tops above final grade (p. 163-164).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p165_184
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This excerpt from the 1935-miami-dade-building-code outlines regulatory standards for building foundations and the design, materials, and construction of reinforced-concrete-standards.
Foundations and Wood Requirements
This section addresses foundation-construction-requirements, mandating an 18-inch clearance between floor joists and the ground, with the subfloor area leveled and debris-free (p. 165). Regarding wood-framing-standards, wood sills and plates contacting masonry or concrete must be isolated using 85-pound slate roofing or 24-gauge galvanized iron (p. 165). Wood furring and foundation vents require preservative treatment on all sides (p. 165). Approved lumber must meet specific heartwood or pressure-treated standards, explicitly excluding brush treatment except for end cuts (p. 165). ventilation-requirements dictate that foundation wall vents must provide at least two square feet of opening per 15 lineal feet of wall, covered with 16-mesh non-corrosive wire, and distributed across at least three sides for cross-ventilation (p. 165–166).
Reinforced Concrete: Scope and Definitions
Chapter 26 establishes comprehensive regulations for reinforced-concrete-standards, superseding conflicting code provisions (p. 166). It requires detailed drawings, reinforcement layouts, and load calculations prior to permit issuance (p. 166). A dedicated board-of-examiners-for-special-construction may evaluate special concrete systems not covered by standard rules (p. 166). Pages 167–169 provide extensive definitions for key terms, including aggregate, anchorage, flat slab, water-cement ratio, and modular ratio (p. 167–169).
Materials, Testing, and Concrete Quality
All materials must comply with standards from the american-society-for-testing-materials in effect at adoption, with testing overseen by the Building Inspector (p. 170). Aggregates must be clean, durable, and free of excessive shale, silt, or organic impurities (p. 171). Concrete quality is strictly tied to the water-cement ratio and 28-day compressive strength, with prescribed mix proportions for plastic and moderately wet concrete ranging from 1,500 to 3,000 psi (p. 173). Field testing requires both control cylinders (moist-cured at 70°F) and job cylinders (cured on-site), with mandatory compression tests per batch volume (p. 173–174).
Allowable Unit Stresses
The code specifies maximum allowable-working-stress for concrete and steel based on concrete strength (f'_c) (p. 176–177). Flexural compression limits range from 800 to 1,200 psi depending on strength grade, while shear and bond stresses scale with reinforcement anchorage and bar type (p. 176–177). Axial compression allowances differ for tied versus spirally reinforced columns, with spiral columns permitting higher stresses based on reinforcement ratio (p. 177). Reinforcement tension limits are capped at 20,000 psi for intermediate and rail steel, with compression limits tied to the modular ratio (p. 177–178).
Mixing, Placing, and Curing
Concrete operations require dry excavations, cleaned forms, and continuous mixing for at least one minute (p. 178). Placement must be continuous to avoid construction joints where possible, with thorough compaction around reinforcement (p. 179). Exposed surfaces require at least seven days of moist curing, with increased frequency in hot weather (p. 179). Cold-weather placement mandates concrete temperatures between 50°F and 120°F, maintained for 72 hours, prohibiting salt additives or direct manure contact (p. 179).
Forms, Reinforcement, and Joints
Forms must be tightly sealed and properly braced, removable only after concrete achieves sufficient strength (p. 179–180). Reinforcement must be free of rust, bent cold without heating, and spaced at minimum clear distances of 1 inch or 1⅓ times the aggregate size (p. 180). Protective concrete cover ranges from 3 inches for ground-contact footings to 1 inch for fire-resistive-construction slabs, with plaster alternatives permitted under strict conditions (p. 181). Construction joints must be roughened, cleaned, and strategically placed near mid-spans or offset at beam intersections (p. 181).
Design Assumptions and Notation
Structural design relies on elastic flexure theory, assuming steel carries all tensile stress and utilizing a modular ratio n = 30,000/f'_c (p. 182). Pages 182–184 define an extensive notation system for spans, moments, reinforcement areas, and stress variables. Structures must be designed for full dead and live structural-loads, with wind-pressure-design allowances permitting a 150% increase in allowable stresses (p. 184). Span length calculations vary by support conditions, and flexural computations must adhere to standardized formulas (p. 184).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p185_204
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
Beams, Slabs, and T-Beams
The effective depth of a beam or slab is measured from the centroid of tensile reinforcement to the top surface, excluding non-monolithic finishes (p. 185). For symmetrically loaded equal spans, the point of inflection is assumed at the fifth point of the span (p. 185). T-beam construction requires integral slab-beam formation, with effective flange widths capped at one-fourth the span length for symmetrical beams and one-twelfth for single-flange beams (p. 185). Ribbed floor systems mandate ribs spaced no more than 36 inches apart, with minimum slab thicknesses tied to rib spacing and tile usage (p. 186).
Moment Coefficients and Two-Way Slabs
The code provides explicit moment-coefficients for uniformly loaded continuous beams and slabs, differentiating between freely supported/slightly restrained systems and fully restrained continuous systems (p. 186–188). For two-way reinforced floors, structural-loads distribution depends on panel aspect ratios, with square panels splitting load equally and rectangular panels distributing based on length-to-width ratios (p. 189). Minimum shrinkage reinforcement ratios are specified based on bar type, ranging from 0.003 for plain bars to 0.0022 for welded wire fabric (p. 189).
Shear, Diagonal Tension, and Anchorage
Shearing unit stress calculations dictate when shear-reinforcement such as vertical stirrups or bent-up bars must be provided (p. 189–190). Stirrup spacing is limited to three-quarters or three-eighths of the effective depth depending on stress levels (p. 190). Bond stress computations govern anchorage lengths, requiring sufficient embedment beyond support faces and points of inflection to develop the full allowable-working-stress in tension (p. 191–192). Special anchorage rules apply for high shear stresses or unusual members, mandating personal supervision by the designing engineer (p. 192–193).
Flat Slab Construction
flat-slabs are defined as beamless slabs reinforced in two or four directions, with standard design provisions limited to panels with length-to-width ratios not exceeding 1.33 (p. 193–194). Moment distribution is divided between column strips and middle strips, with specific coefficients provided for slabs with or without dropped panels (p. 195). Minimum slab thickness is calculated using a formula incorporating span length, column capital size, and load intensity (p. 196). Reinforcement layout rules detail bar extensions beyond points of inflection and column heads for both two-way and four-way systems (p. 197–198). Additional provisions address wall panels, marginal beams, and permissible openings within middle and column strips (p. 198–199).
Columns and Footings
column-design limits unsupported lengths and lateral dimensions, with principal columns requiring a minimum 12-inch diameter or thickness (p. 200). Spiral columns permit higher axial loads based on core area and reinforcement ratio, while tied columns use gross area calculations with stricter longitudinal steel limits (p. 200–201). Composite steel-concrete columns and long columns (slenderness exceeding 40R or 50R) include explicit reduction factors for buckling (p. 202–204). foundation-construction-requirements dictate that footings are proportioned for total column load plus self-weight, with critical bending sections located at column faces or base centers, and sloped or stepped designs permitted if shear requirements are met (p. 204).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p205_224
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This excerpt from the 1935-miami-dade-building-code outlines structural engineering standards for concrete pedestals, structural steel, foundations, piles, and masonry walls.
Concrete Pedestals and Footings
The code specifies load distribution and bending moment calculations for footings, including formulas for trapezoidal load areas (p. 205). Reinforcement spacing and pitch requirements are detailed, along with design rules for sloped or stepped footings (p. 206). Unreinforced pedestals are limited to a maximum compressive stress of 0.25f'_c and strict depth-to-width ratios (p. 206).
Structural Steel and Iron Design
Chapter 27 establishes quality standards referencing american-society-for-testing-materials specifications for structural steel, cast steel, and cast iron (p. 206-207). It defines allowable-working-stress limits for tension, compression, bending, and shear across various steel and iron types, including slenderness ratio (l/r) constraints for columns (p. 207-208). Provisions for beams and girders cover web thickness, stiffener spacing, crane runway horizontal forces, and flange plate width limits (p. 210-211). structural-steel-connections standards address rivet/bolt spacing, net section deductions, compression splices, and minimum rivet counts (p. 211-213). Welded connections are permitted with specific allowable stresses, electrode requirements per the american-welding-society, and mandatory operator competency testing (p. 213-214).
Steel Joists and Construction Details
steel-joist-design may utilize rolled, built-up, or welded truss designs, but non-solid-web joists are prohibited in buildings over eight stories (p. 215). Deflection is limited to 1/360 of the span, and permanent bridging must transfer lateral forces (p. 216). Workmanship rules dictate hot riveting temperatures, restrict gas cutting to non-stressed members, and mandate corrosion protection via specific paint systems or concrete encasement (p. 217-218). Erection tolerances require frames to be plumb within 1:500, or 1:1000 for exterior columns (p. 219).
Excavations, Foundations, and Piles
Chapter 28 regulates foundation-construction-requirements and soil bearing capacities, specifying limits for rock, natural sand, and filled land, with mandatory load tests for uncertain soils (p. 219-220). pile-foundation-engineering mandates that piles be driven to solid bearing without eccentric loading. Wood piles require specific taper, diameter, and creosote treatment standards per the american-wood-preservers-association, with allowable loads calculated via hammer drop formulas (p. 220-221). Concrete piles (precast or cast-in-place) have strict reinforcement, curing, and length-to-diameter limits, while structural steel piles follow Chapter 27 standards (p. 221-222). Load tests require sustaining twice the design load for 48 hours with settlement limits (p. 222).
Solid Masonry Walls
Chapter 29 mandates lateral support for masonry walls at intervals not exceeding 20 feet horizontally and 256 sq. ft. per panel (p. 223). reinforced-concrete-standards tie-beams and coping are required for wind resistance and structural continuity (p. 223). Exterior wall thickness scales with building height, with a minimum of 12 inches for the top 35 feet and incremental increases downward (p. 224). masonry-standards restrict corbels to one-fourth the wall thickness, and all masonry units must be wetted before placement (p. 224).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p225_244
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This document excerpt from the 1935-miami-dade-building-code outlines detailed structural, fire-resistance, and construction standards for walls, floors, roofs, and vertical circulation elements. It establishes minimum thicknesses, material quality, bonding requirements, and load-bearing capacities across multiple construction-types.
Wall Construction & Materials
The code specifies rigorous thickness and bonding standards for solid masonry-standards, hollow tile/block, reinforced-concrete-standards, and stone walls, with detailed tables correlating wall thickness to building-height-and-area-limits (p. 225-230). Lateral support, pier dimensions, and limits on chases/recesses are strictly regulated to maintain structural integrity, prohibiting chases in walls twelve inches thick or less and limiting their depth to one-third of the wall thickness (p. 226). Requirements for wall-veneer-and-facings dictate specific attachment methods, backing rigidity, and material thicknesses, with extensive provisions for glass veneer installation, including mastic cement application, metal shelf angles, and expansion joints (p. 231-233). Stone ashlar facing requires bond units comprising at least fifteen percent of the superficial area (p. 234).
Fire Resistance & Enclosures
Standards for fire-separation, including fire walls, fire division walls, and parapet walls, mandate minimum thickness and continuity, extending from the foundation to eighteen inches above the roof level (p. 234-235). Vertical openings, including stairways, ramps, elevators, and utility shafts, require enclosed fire-resistive-construction with self-closing doors and wire glass to prevent smoke and fire spread (p. 237-239). Elevator enclosures are limited to two elevators per shaft, with one-hour fire-resistive walls required between separate banks (p. 238).
Floor & Roof Construction
Provisions under floor-and-roof-standards vary by material, mandating specific depths for concrete slabs (minimum four inches for solid floors) and structural-fireproofing for steel-joist-design (p. 240-241). Roof construction must integrate with the building framework, with requirements for truss bracing, ventilation-requirements such as air spaces of at least eighteen inches, and approved coverings based on occupancy-classification and construction-types (p. 241-242). Roof drainage systems must include adequately sized downspouts (minimum one inch per 150 sq ft of roof) and overflow weep holes (p. 242).
Stairs, Ramps & Access
Stairways and ramps must sustain a minimum structural-loads of 100 pounds per square foot, with uniform tread/riser dimensions and slip-resistant surfaces (p. 243-244). The code mandates roof access via scuttles or penthouses for multi-story buildings, with specific sizing (minimum 2’x3’) and fire-resistive requirements for attic penetrations (p. 242, p. 243). Winders are permitted only in Group I buildings or monumental stairways not serving emergency-egress (p. 243).
These provisions collectively establish a comprehensive framework for structural walls, fire resistance, vertical enclosures, and building codes in early 20th-century urban construction.

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p245_264
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
Overview
This document contains excerpts from the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code, covering Chapters 33 through 37. It establishes comprehensive regulations for emergency-egress, fire-resistive-construction, structural elements, and mechanical installations, reflecting early 20th-century priorities in urban fire safety and building durability.
Stairways and Egress Requirements
The code mandates strict dimensional and safety standards for stairways to ensure reliable occupant evacuation. Stairways must be at least 44 inches wide, with a maximum rise of 7½ inches and a minimum tread depth of 10 inches (p. 247). Landings at the top and bottom of every flight must be level, at least 44 inches long, and match the width of the connected stair flight (p. 247). Interior stairways require 2-hour fire-resistive-construction enclosures, while passageways serving exits must maintain a minimum clear height of 7 feet and be protected by 1-hour fire doors (p. 245). For buildings three or more stories in height, the code requires smokeproof-towers featuring 4-hour fire-resistive walls, exterior access vestibules, and continuous uniform egress to street grade (p. 250-251). Outside stairways are permitted for buildings up to three stories or 55 feet high but may not constitute more than 50% of the required exit capacity (p. 251).
Doors, Windows, and Skylights
Exit enclosure doors must be self-closing, normally closed, and constructed to 1-hour fire-resistive standards, swinging in the direction of travel without obstructing stair width (p. 245-246). First-floor plate glass windows facing public streets must be at least ¼ inch thick, limited to 91 square feet without division bars, and secured with non-corrosive metal settings (p. 252). The code mandates storm-shutter-requirements capable of withstanding 30 pounds per square foot of wind pressure for public-facing openings to protect against flying debris (p. 252). Skylights angled less than 45 degrees above the first story must be set at least one foot above the roof, and wire glass panels are restricted to 720 square inches or 48 inches in any single dimension (p. 253).
Balconies, Penthouses, and Roof Structures
Exterior balconies and bays require incombustible supports, railings at least 3 feet 6 inches high, and structural design to carry a 100 psf live load (p. 254). Penthouses and roof structures housing mechanical equipment, water tanks, or stair enclosures are excluded from building height calculations but are strictly limited to 20% of the total roof area and a maximum projection of 12 to 28 feet above the roof (p. 255). Walls of roof structures within 4 feet of exterior walls must match the fire-resistive rating of the story below and project 2 feet above the roofline (p. 255).
Chimneys and Heating Apparatus
Chimney construction requires ⅝-inch thick fire clay linings with a softening point of at least 1994°F, with all joints thoroughly slushed and grouted (p. 256). Steel smokestacks must include 25 feet of fire brick lining from the intake and external masonry or ventilated metal shielding up through the roof (p. 260). Gas vents must utilize unglazed fire clay or concrete tile pipe with sleeves at every joint, maintaining a minimum 1-inch rise per foot and strict clearances from combustibles (p. 261). Fireplaces require 8-inch thick walls, fire brick facing, and properly supported hearths extending at least 20 inches from the chimney breast (p. 263). Warm air furnaces must be double metal-shielded, installed on masonry floors, and maintain protected clearances of at least 24 inches from overhead wood framing (p. 263-264).
Key Themes
The document emphasizes building codes as a systematic approach to mitigating urban fire risks, enforcing material durability, and standardizing egress pathways. It reflects the era’s engineering standards for wind resistance, structural load distribution, and mechanical ventilation safety.

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p25_44
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This excerpt from the 1935-miami-dade-building-code outlines administrative procedures, enforcement mechanisms, definitions, and occupancy-based requirements for construction in Miami. It begins with a fee schedule based on occupancy groups and construction types, doubling fees for unpermitted work (p. 25). The code establishes strict building-inspection protocols, requiring permits to be posted and mandating sequential inspections for foundations, framing, stucco, and final completion before a certificate-of-occupancy can be issued (p. 26). For Type I buildings or complex projects, a registered inspector must be employed, bonded at $5,000, and responsible for continuous oversight until a Certificate of Compliance is granted (p. 27).
Enforcement powers are vested in the Building Inspector, who holds police authority to halt unsafe work, condemn dangerous structures, and mandate repairs or demolition, with costs liened against the property (p. 29). The code also addresses alternate materials by allowing their use if proven sufficient, with appeals handled by the board-of-examiners-and-appeals comprising an architect, builder, and structural engineer (p. 32). Violations carry penalties, and the code explicitly preserves enforcement of related ordinances covering dry cleaning, awnings, zoning, contractors, electrical, plumbing, and gas systems (p. 34).
A comprehensive glossary defines core terminology, including building definitions such as “Grade,” “Story,” “Fire Wall,” “Occupancy,” and “Structure” (p. 36). Part III introduces occupancy-classification, categorizing buildings into Groups A through J based on use and hazard level (p. 41). It mandates fire-separation for mixed-occupancy buildings, specifying three tiers: Absolute (4-hour resistance, no openings), Special (3-hour resistance, restricted openings), and Ordinary (1-hour resistance, self-closing doors) (p. 42). A detailed matrix dictates the required separation levels between different occupancy groups to prevent fire spread and ensure structural safety (p. 44).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p265_284
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
Overview
This excerpt from the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code establishes comprehensive regulations for heating appliances, fire suppression systems, theatrical safety, and fire-resistive construction standards.
Heating & Combustion Appliances
The code mandates strict clearance, shielding, and ventilation requirements for various heat sources. Low-pressure steam plants and hot water heaters must rest on masonry or reinforced concrete and be externally protected by asbestos (p. 265). Large boilers require 5 to 10 feet of clearance from combustibles, with closer materials protected by concrete or brick (p. 265). Domestic and commercial stoves and gas ranges require elevated metal supports, asbestos pads, and shielded partitions to prevent ignition (pp. 265-266). Oil burners must comply with national-board-fire-underwriters regulations (p. 266). Requirements for heating-and-chimney-safety dictate that warm air ducts require precise sizing, asbestos insulation, and secure metal fastening, with risers braced rather than nailed (pp. 267-268). Incinerators demand thick masonry or concrete walls lined with fire brick (p. 268). Solar water heaters are regulated for structural anchoring, galvanized construction, 100-pound pressure ratings, and cork insulation to minimize heat loss (pp. 268-269).
Fire Extinguishing & Standpipe Systems
sprinkler-systems are mandated in basements, theaters, woodworking and mattress factories, film exchanges, and multi-story commercial buildings (pp. 269-270). Systems must adhere to 1931 National Board standards, with limited allowances for domestic water supply under specific pressure conditions (pp. 270-271). standpipe-systems are required in buildings four stories or taller, with strict specifications for wrought iron or galvanized steel construction, hydrostatic testing, and sizing based on building height (4-inch for up to 6 stories, 6-inch for taller) (pp. 271-272). Standpipes must be located near exits, equipped with Siamese connections for fire department access, and supplied by pressure tanks or fire pumps capable of maintaining 30 psi at the highest outlet (pp. 272-273). Basement pipe inlets are required in commercial structures lacking sprinkler systems (p. 274).
Theatrical & Stage Safety
Theatrical spaces require rigorous fire isolation and ventilation under theatrical-stage-safety. Stage ventilators must be metal, positioned above the roof, and equipped with fusible links that open at 160°F (p. 274). Gridirons and fly galleries must be constructed of incombustible materials capable of supporting 75 psf (p. 275). Backstage areas require fire-separation from the main stage (p. 275). Proscenium walls must be solid masonry with a 4-hour fire rating or metal-stud assemblies with cement plaster, limited to four protected openings (pp. 275-276). Stage floors require Type I construction or heavy timber/steel supports, rated for 125 psf (p. 276). Motion picture booths must be enclosed in 1-hour fire-resistive rooms with self-closing fire doors, restricted viewing ports, metal shutters on fusible links, and dedicated exhaust ventilation (pp. 277-278). Nitrocellulose film safety requires storage in metal cabinets with tight self-closing doors and compliance with national-fire-protection-association guidelines (p. 279). Proscenium curtains must be constructed from metal-reinforced asbestos cloth, fully painted with mineral silicate, and mechanically operated with counterweights and safety chains to lower completely in under 30 seconds (pp. 279-282).
Fire-Resistive Construction Standards
Part VIII defines 1, 2, 3, and 4-hour fire-resistive-construction ratings based on testing standards from the american-society-for-testing-materials (p. 283). Approved materials include brick, concrete, gypsum, metal, and asbestos combinations (p. 283). Installation requires solid bedding in compatible mortars, metal ties for structural bonding, and reinforced edges for structural-fireproofing applied steel protection to prevent cracking (p. 284). Plaster fire protection must utilize gypsum or Portland cement mortar, with galvanized metal lath reinforcement unless applied directly to masonry surfaces (p. 284).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p285_304
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
Summary: 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code (pp. 285-304)
This excerpt from the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code establishes detailed regulatory standards for fire-resistive-construction, structural-fireproofing, roofing classifications, and the management of public rights-of-way during and after building projects.
Structural Fire Protection
The code specifies minimum thicknesses for insulating materials applied to steel columns, beams, trusses, and reinforcing steel to achieve 1 to 4-hour fire-resistive ratings (p. 285). Materials include concrete, gunite, brick, clay tile, gypsum blocks, and plaster, with thickness requirements varying by structural component (p. 285). Concrete is classified into two grades: Grade A contains aggregates with no more than 65% siliceous material, while Grade B uses other coarse aggregates, typically requiring thicker protective layers (p. 286). Plaster protections exceeding 1 inch must incorporate embedded galvanized metal lath or wire mesh securely tied to supporting members (p. 286). Concrete, gypsum, and gunite protections require embedded galvanized wire or expanded metal mesh, with specific weight and gauge standards for ties and bases (p. 286-287).
Walls, Partitions, and Floors
Section 4302 defines minimum thicknesses for fire-resistive bearing and non-bearing walls and partitions, accounting for solid, hollow, and plastered constructions using brick, clay tile, concrete, and gypsum blocks (p. 287-290). Plaster must average at least ½ inch per side to contribute to fire resistance, with thicker applications requiring embedded reinforcement (p. 287). Section 4303 outlines floor-and-roof-standards for 3-hour, 2-hour, and 1-hour ratings, mandating reinforced concrete, solid masonry, or protected steel joists, while permitting wood joisted construction with double flooring and fire-resistive ceilings for 1-hour ratings (p. 291). Ceiling protection thicknesses may be reduced by ½ inch if a free-air space of at least 1 inch is maintained above the member (p. 291).
Fire Doors, Shutters, and Windows
Section 4304 details construction requirements for 1-hour fire doors, including tin-clad wood-core, double sheet-metal with asbestos fill, and metal-clad wood panel types (p. 292-293). Hardware must be made of malleable iron or structural steel, with specified dimensions for hinges, locks, latches, and astragals on paired doors (p. 293-294). Fire-resistive windows require solid or hollow metal frames, ¼-inch wire glass, and specific groove depths and bearing dimensions, with maximum light areas and frame sizes strictly regulated (p. 294-295). All fire-rated-openings, shutters, and windows must be closed at the end of each business day (p. 295).
Roof Coverings
Section 4305 categorizes roofs into “Fire Retardant” and “Ordinary” classes under roofing-standards. Fire retardant roofings require multiple layers of saturated felt, asphalt, or mineral surfacing, applied with bituminous mopping to prevent unmopped layer contact (p. 295-296). Approved materials include hydraulic compressed shingles, metal coverings, slate, and tile, with strict installation rules for nailing, cementing, and edge protection (p. 296-297). Ordinary roofings permit fewer layers or specific felt combinations but still require non-corrosive fasteners and proper lapping (p. 298). Physical properties for roofing felts and membranes are standardized by weight, saturation percentage, and american-society-for-testing-materials testing methods for pliability, heating resistance, and surface integrity (p. 299-301).
Construction and Public Property Regulations
Chapter 44 governs construction-site-regulations for the temporary use of streets and sidewalks during construction, limiting material storage to one-third of the street width and prohibiting obstruction of rail tracks, fire hydrants, and drainage (p. 301-302). In fire-zones No. 1 or for buildings over two stories, a covered walkway with specific load-bearing capacity, height, and lighting requirements must be maintained (p. 302). Chapter 45 regulates permanent projections over public property, allowing limited overhangs for cornices, marquees, balconies, and porches, provided they are fire-resistive, properly anchored, and maintain at least 10 feet of clearance from sidewalks (p. 303-304). The code explicitly prohibits vaults, excavations, or cellar-ways beneath sidewalks to preserve public safety and infrastructure integrity (p. 304).
Key Themes
The document reflects early 20th-century urban planning priorities, emphasizing standardized fire resistance ratings, material science specifications, and strict enforcement of public safety and right-of-way protections. It bridges structural engineering requirements with municipal zoning controls, ensuring both building resilience and pedestrian accessibility.

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p305_324
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This document contains excerpts from the city-of-miami Building Code (originally 1935/1936) and subsequent amendments, detailing municipal regulations for urban infrastructure and safety.
Signs and Billboards (Chapter 47)
Strict regulations govern the construction, placement, and maintenance of advertising-sign-standards (p. 305). Billboards are prohibited in R, B-1, and B-2 zoning districts and must avoid traffic hazards or visual blight (p. 306). Special restrictions ban new advertising devices along biscayne-boulevard between NE 13th and NE 54th Streets, with daily penalties for violations (p. 306). The code defines various sign types, including projecting, roof, and banner signs (pp. 306-307). building-permits are required for most structures over specific size thresholds, requiring detailed engineering drawings to ensure wind-pressure-design and structural safety (p. 307). Material requirements mandate fireproof or metal construction for large signs, with strict clearance rules for electrical conductors to prevent hazards (pp. 308-310). All signs must bear the manufacturer’s imprint (p. 311).
Elevators, Escalators, and Dumbwaiters (Chapter 48)
vertical-transportation-safety standards align with the 1925 american-standards-association code and the 1935 National Electrical Code (p. 311). Regulations cover carrying capacity (minimum 100 lbs/sq ft for passenger cars), operator competency certification, and mechanical door interlocks to prevent operation when shaft doors are open (pp. 312-313). Structural requirements specify incombustible car materials, steel guide rails, substantial buffer springs, and emergency roof exits (pp. 313-314). High-rise buildings over 100 feet must maintain a fire department-ready elevator at all times (p. 314). The Building Inspector conducts quarterly building-inspection and enforces safety device mandates for elevators exceeding rated speeds (p. 314).
Awnings, Canopies, and Tents (Chapter 49)
Movable and stationary temporary-canvas-structures require permits and must conform to strict construction standards to withstand wind-pressure-design (p. 314). Metal frames are mandatory within fire-zones 1 and 2, with specific pipe thickness requirements based on span width (pp. 315-316). Sidewalk projections are limited to nine feet, with minimum clearance heights of 7’6” for metal parts and 6’6” for canvas (p. 318). Awnings cannot be used for merchandise storage or business transactions without special approval, and temporary structures over public walkways must be quickly collapsible (pp. 316, 318).
General Provisions and Historical Amendments
Chapter 50 establishes the code as an emergency measure for public safety, repealing conflicting ordinances and setting fines up to $500 or 60 days imprisonment for violations (pp. 319-320). The appendix lists adopted american-society-for-testing-materials standards for materials like brick, concrete, and gypsum (pp. 320-321). Subsequent ordinances expand the code’s administrative framework: Ordinance 1678 (1937) creates a Board of Appeals for building condemnations (p. 321); Ordinance 2568 (1941) expands the secondary fire zone into coconut-grove to restrict frame construction (pp. 322-323); and Ordinance 2972 (1945) revises building permit fees (p. 324). Together, these sections illustrate the evolution of urban zoning, building code enforcement, and municipal safety standards in mid-century Miami.

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p325_344
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This document compiles several amendments to Ordinance No. 1554, the Building Code of the City of Miami, Florida, enacted between 1941 and 1950.
Building Permit Fees and Cost Schedules (pp. 325–330)
Amendments to Section 203 establish fees for building permits based on the estimated cost of construction, calculated using cost-per-cubic-foot tables categorized by building Group and Type (p. 327). A 1945 amendment sets initial rates, while a 1950 update (Ordinance No. 4049) increases these rates to reflect rising construction costs (p. 330). The Building Inspector is tasked with maintaining accurate records of all collected fees, and fees are doubled if work begins without a permit (p. 327).
Structural and Material Specifications (pp. 331–340)
Ordinance No. 3334 (1947) introduces detailed requirements for cellular steel floors, mandating a minimum steel thickness of U.S. Standard Gauge No. 18, ASTM compliance, and specific fire-resistive construction standards including concrete fill and suspended ceilings (p. 331). Flexural stress values are capped at 60% of the steel’s yield point (p. 332).
Ordinance No. 3703 (1949) updates numerous code sections to harmonize with surrounding municipalities. Key changes include:

Requiring a 12-hour curing period for masonry foundations before laying elements (p. 334).
Adding span tables for floor and roof joists based on fiber stress and live load (pp. 335–337).
Adopting ACI 318-47 for reinforced concrete and the AISC 5th Edition for structural steel (p. 336).
Specifying reinforcement for concrete columns and limiting unsupported spans for U-type beam blocks to seven feet (p. 336).
Establishing standards for plate glass windows, bulkheads, and storm shutters (p. 338).
Providing detailed guidelines for metal roofing and siding, including minimum gauges, fastening methods, and corrosion protection for steel and aluminum (pp. 339–340).

Fire Zone Expansion (pp. 341–344)
Ordinance No. 2471 (1941) expands fire zones by adding a significant area to Fire Zone Number I to prevent new frame structures and reduce fire hazards (p. 341). The ordinance provides a precise legal boundary description encompassing parts of Greenwood Park, Highland Park, Spring Garden Subdivisions, and areas along the Miami River and Northwest avenues (pp. 341–344). Violations carry fines up to $500 or imprisonment (p. 344).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p345_364
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This document compiles six ordinances from city-of-miami, Florida, enacted between 1949 and 1950 to update municipal building codes and public safety regulations.
Fire Zone Expansion
Ordinance No. 3970 amends the building code to extend fire-zones, incorporating a specifically delineated area bounded by northeast-second-avenue, northeast-thirteenth-street, and various local plats and property lines (p. 345). Violations of the zone regulations are punishable by fines up to $500 or imprisonment (p. 346).
Construction Permits and Bonds
Ordinance No. 3746 mandates that owners or contractors post a performance bond or cash deposit of at least $50 before receiving building-permits for construction, excavating, or grading to protect public property from heavy equipment damage (p. 347). The chief-building-inspector sets the amount based on site improvements, with appeals directed to the department-of-public-service (p. 348). Funds are returned after final inspection if no damage or debris remains (p. 348).
Similarly, Ordinance No. 3971 requires cash deposits before issuing permits for carnivals (100 minimum) or circuses (500 minimum) to cover potential damage to pavements and public property (p. 362). The deposit amount is determined by site area and travel routes, with unused funds refunded after cleanup (p. 363).
Survey and Licensing Requirements
Ordinance No. 3827 requires a registered land surveyor’s certificate and plan for all building-permits applications, detailing property corners, dimensions, utilities, elevations, and zoning records (p. 350).
Ordinance No. 3965 amends permit application rules to accept requests only from licensed contractors, with an exception for sole owners working on their own private residences (p. 360). Maintenance, repairs, or additions under $2,500 are exempt from licensing requirements unless they involve structural changes (p. 360).
Dangerous Buildings and Public Nuisances
Ordinance No. 3963 establishes comprehensive procedures for addressing dangerous-buildings and public nuisances (p. 352). It defines dangerous structures based on structural defects, fire hazards, inadequate sanitation, and overcrowding, utilizing appraisal techniques from the american-public-health-association (p. 353). The chief-building-inspector must order repairs, vacation, or demolition based on established standards, such as a 50% deterioration threshold for demolition (p. 354).
The ordinance creates a five-member building-board-of-appeals composed of master builders, structural engineers, and architects to hear appeals and issue final orders (p. 356). It also outlines reporting duties for the Fire and Police divisions, which must notify the chief-building-inspector within 24 hours of discovering suspected dangerous structures (p. 358). Emergency cases allow for immediate repair or demolition to protect public safety (p. 358). Penalties for non-compliance include fines and imprisonment, with costs of city-mandated repairs attachable as tax liens (p. 357).
All ordinances include standard severability and conflict-repeal clauses (p. 346, p. 349, p. 351, p. 359, p. 361, p. 364).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p365_375
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This document comprises two distinct sections from the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code publication. The first section details Ordinance No. 2149, passed on August 2, 1939, which amends municipal regulations governing elevator and lifting apparatus inspections (p. 365). Key changes include mandating that all inspection reports, whether conducted by city employees or private insurance or casualty companies, be filed directly with the chief-building-inspector (p. 365-366). The ordinance adjusts the fee structure to $2.50 for inspections and 50 cents for operation certificates, while authorizing a full waiver of the inspection fee after July 1, 1939, for approved private inspectors (p. 366). Additionally, it redirects all collected fees to the city-of-miami Tax Collector rather than a dedicated elevator fund, declaring the changes an emergency measure for public safety (p. 367).
The remainder of the document (p. 368-375) presents an alphabetical Building Code Index that maps the code’s regulatory framework. The index organizes requirements by building occupancy classifications, ranging from Group A (public assembly) to Group J (accessory structures) under the occupancy-classification system (p. 368-375). It outlines five primary construction-types, detailing fire-resistive-construction, heavy timber, masonry-standards, metal frame, and wood-framing-standards standards (p. 375). Structural and material specifications are extensively cataloged, covering reinforced-concrete-standards, masonry quality, steel and iron design, and allowable-working-stress tables (p. 369-370). Fire safety provisions dominate several entries, including fire-zones, sprinkler-systems, fire-resistive-construction, and emergency-egress (p. 370-372). The index also references administrative procedures such as building-permits, building-board-of-appeals processes, building-inspection protocols, and penalty enforcement (p. 368, 372-375). Together, these sections illustrate the city’s mid-20th century approach to standardizing vertical-transportation-safety protocols and comprehensively regulating structural integrity, fire prevention, and building occupancy across Miami’s growing urban landscape.

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p45_64
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This document covers Chapters 6 through 10 of the 1935 Miami-Dade Building Code, establishing detailed safety, construction, and egress standards for occupancy-classification from Groups A through E.
Group A: Large Assembly Occupancies
Group A covers theaters, auditoriums, and similar venues with seating capacities of 1,000 or more (Division 1) or 3,500 or more (Division 2) (p. 45). These structures must use Type 1 Construction and face no restrictions on height, area, or seating capacity (p. 45). emergency-egress requirements are stringent: exits must be provided on three sides of the auditorium, each at least five feet wide, opening directly to a street or exit court (p. 46). A main foyer is mandatory, sized at one square foot per seat, with ramp slopes limited to 1:10 (p. 46). Aisles must be at least three feet wide, with no more than six seats between any seat and an aisle (p. 48). Fixed seats are required, and exit doors must swing outward with self-releasing panic hardware (p. 48-49). Buildings with multiple balconies require smokeproof towers on each side (p. 49). Ventilation must supply 30 cubic feet of air per minute per occupant, and sprinkler-systems are mandatory (p. 49-50).
Group B: Medium Assembly Occupancies
Group B includes similar assembly spaces with 300–1,000 seats (Division 1) or 750–3,500 seats (Division 2) (p. 51). Height and area limits vary by construction type (I, II, or III), with Type I allowing unlimited height and up to 3,500 seats (p. 51). Exit requirements largely mirror Group A but waive the foyer requirement and prohibit balconies except in Type I buildings (p. 52). fire-separation and sprinkler-systems follow similar protocols (p. 53-54).
Group C: Small Assembly Occupancies
Group C covers venues with fewer than 300 seats (Division 1), 50–750 seats (Division 2), or fewer than 50 seats (Division 3) (p. 54). These buildings may use Types I through V construction, with maximum floor areas strictly determined by building height and street frontage (p. 54-55). Rooms exceeding 30,000 square feet require tight draft stops to subdivide attic spaces into 20,000-square-foot sections (p. 55). Exit rules are adapted for classrooms and small rooms, allowing exits on one side if no point is more than 35 feet from an exit (p. 57). Schools face additional restrictions on upper-floor placement for younger grades (p. 59).
Group D: Institutional and Care Occupancies
Group D encompasses detention facilities, hospitals, sanitariums, and orphanages (p. 60). Division 1 (detention) mandates Type I construction throughout, while Division 2 requires one-hour fire-resistive floors and partitions for multi-story buildings (p. 60-61). emergency-egress provisions include mandatory ramps with a maximum 1:6 slope for bedridden patients (p. 61). Exit doors cannot be fastened except with self-releasing panic devices (p. 61-62). Ventilation and fire-separation standards align with previous groups, with strict prohibitions on volatile liquid storage (p. 62-63).
Group E: Hazardous Industrial and Commercial Occupancies
Group E regulates high-risk facilities like paint storage, planing mills, gasoline stations, and aircraft hangars (p. 63). Floor area and height limits are tightly constrained by construction type and street frontage (p. 63-64). Aircraft hangars are specifically capped at 30,000 square feet for Type I and 15,000 square feet for Type II, with a 100% area increase permitted if fully sprinklered (p. 64). Draft stops are again required for rooms over 30,000 square feet (p. 64).
Common Requirements
Across all groups, the code mandates fire-separation for boiler rooms and heating plants, prohibits inflammable liquids except in controlled laboratory quantities, and requires independent meter rooms for multi-occupancy buildings (p. 50, 54, 60, 63). Historical sanitation provisions note segregated toilet facilities based on race and gender (p. 50, 53, 58, 62).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p5_24
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
The 1935-miami-dade-building-code establishes minimum safety, design, and construction standards for all buildings and structures within the city-of-miami, Florida (p. 18). The provided excerpt outlines the code’s administrative framework, compliance workflow, fee structure, and regulations for existing buildings and structural relocations.
Compliance Workflow
The code prescribes a six-step procedure for builders and property owners: verify zoning district, identify the applicable fire-zones, classify the building’s occupancy-classification, select the appropriate construction types, secure a building permit, and obtain a certificate of occupancy upon completion (p. 5). Projects with an estimated cost of $5,000 or more require plans prepared or approved by a registered architect or structural engineer (p. 5, 21).
Code Structure
Organized into fifteen parts, the code covers administrative rules, definitions, occupancy requirements for Groups A through J, fire zone restrictions, five construction types (fire resistive to wood frame), engineering standards for materials (masonry, wood, concrete, steel), detailed construction regulations, fire protection standards, and rules for public property occupancy, signs, elevators, and awnings (pp. 6–16).
Administrative Provisions & Permits
Chapter 1 defines the code’s scope, applying primarily to new construction while setting strict thresholds for existing buildings. Major alterations or repairs exceeding 50% of a building’s value within twelve months trigger full code compliance or demolition requirements (p. 18). Changes in use or occupancy that would be prohibited in new construction also mandate full compliance, with limited exceptions for segregated portions or internal group changes (p. 19). All structures must be maintained in a safe condition, and existing safety devices cannot be removed without inspector approval (p. 19).
Chapter 2 details the building-permits, requiring written applications, scaled plans, lot layouts, and multi-departmental approvals for plumbing, electrical, zoning, and health compliance (p. 20). Approved plans must be kept on-site during construction and cannot be altered without authorization (p. 22).
Fees & Structural Relocation
Permit fees are calculated based on the estimated cubic content cost of the project, with tiered rates for new construction, additions, alterations, repairs, elevators, signs, and awnings (pp. 23–24). The code imposes stringent regulations on moving buildings within or into city limits, requiring written permits, performance bonds, cash bonds for street damage, continuous city inspector accompaniment during transit, and mandatory red warning lights on structures left in streets after dark (pp. 21–22).
This excerpt establishes the foundational administrative and procedural mechanisms that govern Miami’s early 20th-century building regulation, emphasizing systematic compliance, professional oversight for larger projects, and strict safety maintenance.
