summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p65_84
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This excerpt from the 1935-miami-dade-building-code details construction, safety, and zoning mandates for occupancy groups E through J, alongside precise boundary definitions for Miami’s municipal fire districts.
Group E Buildings (p. 65-67)
Mandates at least two emergency-egress per story, with specific rules for automobile ramps and smokeproof-towers (p. 65). Requires mechanical ventilation supplying 30 cubic feet of air per minute per occupant, and separate toilet facilities based on sex and race (p. 65). Special hazards like dry cleaning plants require Type I construction, four-hour fire-resistive partitions, and power-driven exhaust systems (p. 66). Public garages face strict height limits and must use incombustible floor materials, while service stations require approved grease drains (p. 67).
Group F Buildings (p. 67-70)
Regulates moderately hazardous commercial and industrial occupancies, including retail stores, offices, and factories (p. 67). Establishes maximum floor areas based on construction type, height, and street frontage, allowing a 100% area increase for complete sprinkler-systems protection (p. 68). Exterior walls within four feet of property lines must be masonry or reinforced concrete (p. 68). Storage of volatile inflammable liquids is prohibited unless compliant with the 1930 national-board-fire-underwriters ordinance (p. 69).
Group G Buildings (p. 70-73)
Covers non-hazardous occupancies like ice plants, power plants, and workshops using incombustible materials (p. 70). Imposes similar height and area restrictions, capping Type V construction at three stories and 38 feet (p. 71). Unlike higher-hazard groups, vertical openings do not require enclosure except as specified in Chapter 33 (p. 72), and under-roof fireproofing may be omitted (p. 72).
Group H Buildings (p. 73-76)
Governs hotels, apartments, dormitories, and convents, deferring to florida-hotel-state-commission rules where applicable (p. 73). Enforces stricter dimensional limits, restricting Type V construction to two stories plus an attic (p. 74). Living and sleeping rooms must feature windows comprising at least 15% of the floor area (p. 75). Boiler rooms require “Special Fire Separation” from the rest of the building (p. 75).
Group I Buildings (p. 76-78)
Applies to small-scale dwellings (max 2 families) and minor hospitals/sanitariums (max 6 patients) (p. 76). Caps floor areas at 10,000 sq ft for Types III/IV and 7,500 sq ft for Type V (p. 76). Attached private garages must be separated by a one-hour fire-resistive-construction ceiling or floor and feature gravity-closing doors without glass (p. 77). Minimum ceiling heights are set at 8’4” (p. 78).
Group J Buildings (p. 78-81)
Includes private garages, accessory structures, and stadiums/amusement parks (p. 78). Stadiums require one 7-foot-wide exit per 2,000 persons, with aisles spaced every 20 seats (p. 79). Private garages attached to residential buildings need fixed louvered or exhaust ventilation, and direct openings into living/sleeping rooms are banned (p. 80). Garages storing over three cars require chemical extinguishers (p. 80).
Fire Zones (p. 81-84)
Declares the entire city-of-miami a Fire District divided into Zones 1, 2, and 3 (p. 81). Provides extensive legal boundary descriptions for fire-zones: Zone 1 covers the downtown core along biscayne-bay and the miami-river (p. 81-82), while Zone 2 extends north along biscayne-boulevard and federal-highway (p. 83-84). A boundary plat map for Zone 1 is included (p. 82).
Across all groups, the code mandates independent central meter rooms for multiple-occupancy buildings (p. 67, 70, 73, 76, 78) and requires strict separation of mixed occupancies via fire-separation per Section 503 (p. 67, 70, 73, 76, 81).

summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.title: 1935_Miami_Dade_Building-_Code_p85_104
summaries/1935_miami_dade_building-_code.content:
This excerpt from the 1935-miami-dade-building-code provides exhaustive legal boundary descriptions for numerous streets, avenues, and districts across city-of-miami, delineating specific geographic jurisdictions (p. 85-103). The text traces routes along major thoroughfares such as ne-2nd-avenue (p. 86), north-miami-avenue (p. 87), nw-7th-avenue (p. 90), and west-flagler-street (p. 97), referencing historical plats, subdivisions, and landmarks like the florida-east-coast-railroad and coral-gables city limits to establish precise municipal-boundary-definitions. The document concludes with Section 1602, which outlines strict building regulations for Fire Zone No. 1, explicitly prohibiting the erection, alteration, or relocation of Type V construction structures and imposing limits on Type IV construction within the designated zone (p. 104). These boundary definitions and construction restrictions reflect early 20th-century urban planning and fire safety regulations in Miami-Dade County, establishing the geographic scope for fire-zones and construction type classifications.
